title: CLAUDE.md
text: Guidance writing R code, emphasis function design package development. Grounded tidy tools manifesto, tidy design principles, modern tidyverse / tidy evaluation practice.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Write R using tidyverse syntax. Prefer purrr::map() family loops. Use explicit pkg::fn() namespacing inside function bodies unless told otherwise. Use native pipe |>, %>%. Assignment <-, =.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Don’t explain every detail something unless asked. Bullets verbose prose. Lead answer; support just enough reasoning. user asks yes/short question, answer first, add context needed.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Minimal comments. line needs one make sense — running narration. meta-commentary code (# Updated, # Modified, # New:, # Changed previous). Code read written , changelog. “chose ” commentary code chunks. matters, put response text around chunk. Prefer self-explanatory function variable names explanatory comments.
code: # Bad — running narration fit_model <- function(data) {   # First, filter out missing values   clean <- data |> dplyr::filter_out(is.na(y))   # Now fit the model   mod <- lm(y ~ x, data = clean)   # Return the model   mod }  # Good — names carry the meaning fit_model <- function(data) {   data |>     dplyr::filter_out(is.na(y)) |>     lm(y ~ x, data = _) }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: functions run several discrete steps (fitting multiple models, long pipelines, simulation workflows), print short progress message step using cli::cli_inform(). Format: "Step N: <verb> <>". Gate behind quiet = FALSE argument function might called inside larger pipeline — user can suppress ’re noise.
code: fit_all_models <- function(data) {   cli::cli_inform("Step 1: Compiling Model 1: Null Model")   m1 <- fit_null(data)    cli::cli_inform("Step 2: Compiling Model 2: Main Effects")   m2 <- fit_main(data)    cli::cli_inform("Step 3: Compiling Model 3: Interaction")   m3 <- fit_interaction(data)    list(null = m1, main = m2, interaction = m3) } fit_all_models <- function(data, quiet = FALSE) {   inform <- if (quiet) \(...) invisible() else cli::cli_inform   inform("Step 1: Compiling Model 1: Null Model")   ... }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Suppress default cmdstanr / brms compilation sampling chatter: Keep user’s step messages visible; silence everything else. progress really useful (long chain might hang), make opt-via verbose = FALSE argument rather default.
code: Compiling Stan model... Running MCMC with 4 parallel chains... Chain 3 finished in 1.1 seconds. ... Total execution time: 3.3 seconds. # cmdstanr fit <- model$sample(   data = stan_data,   chains = 4,   parallel_chains = 4,   refresh = 0,            # suppress iteration progress   show_messages = FALSE,  # suppress informational messages   show_exceptions = FALSE # suppress Stan exception chatter )  # brms fit <- brms::brm(   y ~ x,   data = data,   backend = "cmdstanr",   refresh = 0,   silent = 2              # 2 = suppress compilation + sampling messages )  # rstan fit <- rstan::sampling(   model,   data = stan_data,   refresh = 0,   verbose = FALSE )

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Every piece code visibly serve least one : Reuse existing data structures. Prefer tibbles/data frames rectangular data; prefer existing atomic vector types 1-D data. Build custom classes (S7 default) top nothing existing fits. Compose simple functions pipe. function one thing; pipe combine. function can’t described one sentence, split . Embrace functional programming. Immutable inputs, copy--modify, generics mutable state, purrr::map() . Design humans. Thinking time bottleneck, compute time. Name things reader.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Verbs imperative mood actions: fit_model(), summarise(), parse_date(). Nouns acceptable builders return object: tibble(), recipe(), geom_point(). Prefix families, don’t suffix . str_detect(), str_replace(), str_split() — autocomplete works prefixes. Reserve suffixes variations theme: purrr::map_int(), purrr::map_chr(). Length inversely proportional frequency. Short names (n(), c()) earn brevity used everywhere. Rare operations deserve descriptive names (validate_model_specification()). snake_case throughout. Dots (.) function names reserved S3 method dispatch — avoid new function names regardless class system ’re using.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Primary data first (makes function pipe-friendly). Arguments determine shape output next. ... required arguments. Optional arguments defaults last — come ..., users must name .
code: # Signature template my_function <- function(data, primary_arg, ..., option_a = default, option_b = NULL) {   ... }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Required arguments defaults. ’s sensible default, don’t invent one — let missing-argument error fire. Optional arguments always defaults. Keep defaults short. default needs computation, use NULL resolve body %||%:
code: my_function <- function(x, weights = NULL) {   weights <- weights %||% rep(1, length(x))   ... }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: string arguments fixed set valid values, use character-vector default rlang::arg_match(). first element default; users get helpful error typos.
code: summarise_method <- function(x, method = c("mean", "median", "trimmed")) {   method <- rlang::arg_match(method)   ... }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use fit — consistency across packages worth creative naming.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: ... forwards user data, prefix function’s arguments . avoid name collisions (dplyr / purrr convention): .data, ., .f, .progress.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Two families non-standard evaluation exist tidyverse. Know one function uses writing . Data-masking: dplyr::mutate(), filter(), summarise(), arrange(), group_by(). Tidy selection: dplyr::select(), relocate(), across(), tidyr::pivot_longer().

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Embrace pattern — bare column name summarise_var <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean({{ var }}, na.rm = TRUE)) }  # String column name — use .data pronoun summarise_string <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(.data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE)) }  # Forward dots my_group_by <- function(data, ...) {   data |> dplyr::group_by(...) }  # Character vector of columns my_select <- function(data, vars) {   data |> dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(vars)) }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: "{{ var }}" interpolates embraced expression output name. Use := LHS dynamic.
code: mean_named <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise("mean_{{ var }}" := mean({{ var }}, na.rm = TRUE)) }  # Allow user override via englue() mean_named <- function(data, var, name = rlang::englue("mean_{{ var }}")) {   data |> dplyr::summarise("{name}" := mean({{ var }}, na.rm = TRUE)) }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use embracing ({{ }}) default. Reach !! / !!! : Building symbols strings: !!rlang::sym(var) (safer: .data[[var]]). Splicing list arguments: !!!args. Splicing character vector column names: !!!rlang::syms(vars) — though dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(vars)) usually cleaner.

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Don't parse strings as code eval(parse(text = paste("mean(", var, ")")))   # unsafe  # Don't use get() inside a data mask dplyr::with(data, mean(get(var)))              # collision-prone  # Don't embrace a value (only embrace function arguments) my_fn <- function(x) {   x <- force(x)   rlang::quo(mean({{ x }}))                    # wrong — x is a value, not an argument }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Prefer .per-operation grouping. returns ungrouped data, almost always want.
code: # Preferred data |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(value), .by = c(group, year))  # Avoid the group_by() / ungroup() sandwich data |>   dplyr::group_by(group, year) |>   dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(value)) |>   dplyr::ungroup()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use dplyr::join_by() — supports equality, inequality, rolling, overlap joins clearer syntax.
code: # Good dplyr::inner_join(x, y, by = dplyr::join_by(id == user_id))  # Inequality / rolling dplyr::inner_join(x, y, dplyr::join_by(id == user_id, closest(date >= start)))  # Specify matching expectations for quality control dplyr::inner_join(x, y, by = dplyr::join_by(id), multiple = "error", unmatched = "error")

title: CLAUDE.md
text: dplyr::pick() — select columns inside data-masking function. dplyr::reframe() — summaries return multiple rows per group. tidyr::pivot_longer() / pivot_wider() — gather() / spread(). tidyr::separate_wider_delim() / separate_wider_regex() — separate() / extract(). purrr::map() |> purrr::list_rbind() — superseded map_dfr().

title: CLAUDE.md
text: filter() optimised keeping rows treats NA FALSE, works want drop rows. Use dplyr::filter_out() — syntax, designed around specifying rows drop. -combined conditions, use dplyr::when_any() instead nesting everything parentheses pipes. dplyr::when_all() counterpart (rarely needed inside filter() since comma-separated conditions already ). when_any() when_all() regular vector functions — usable anywhere, just filter().
code: # Drop deceased patients from before 2012 # filter_out() treats NAs as FALSE (don't drop), which is almost always what you want patients |> dplyr::filter_out(deceased, date < 2012)  # Rule of thumb: if you reach for `!`, `!=`, or `& !is.na()`, use filter_out() # Rows where US/CA scored 200-300, OR where PR/RU scored 100-200 countries |>   dplyr::filter(dplyr::when_any(     name %in% c("US", "CA") & dplyr::between(score, 200, 300),     name %in% c("PR", "RU") & dplyr::between(score, 100, 200)   ))

title: CLAUDE.md
text: recode/replace family now four members, organised whether ’re matching conditions values, whether ’re building new column partially updating existing one: Reach based ’re actually : dplyr::case_match() soft-deprecated favour recode_values(). Don’t reach new code.
code: # Recoding values — creating a new column from matched values likert |>   dplyr::mutate(     category = dplyr::recode_values(       score,       1 ~ "Strongly disagree",       2 ~ "Disagree",       3 ~ "Neutral",       4 ~ "Agree",       5 ~ "Strongly agree"     )   )  # With a lookup table — from/to interface is cleaner than splicing lookup <- tibble::tribble(   ~from, ~to,       1, "Strongly disagree",       2, "Disagree",       3, "Neutral",       4, "Agree",       5, "Strongly agree" )  likert |>   dplyr::mutate(category = dplyr::recode_values(score, from = lookup$from, to = lookup$to))  # Replacing values — partial update, type-stable on the input schools |>   dplyr::mutate(     name = name |>       dplyr::replace_values(         c("UNC", "Chapel Hill")       ~ "UNC Chapel Hill",         c("Duke", "Duke University")  ~ "Duke"       )   )  # replace_values() also subsumes common na_if/coalesce/replace_na patterns dplyr::replace_values(state, NA ~ "Unknown")          # fill NAs dplyr::replace_values(state, "Unknown" ~ NA)          # flag as missing dplyr::replace_values(state, c(NA, "Unknown") ~ "<missing>")  # normalise both

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use .unmatched = "error" case_when() recode_values() believe ’ve covered every case. function errors unhandled input rather silently returning NA — exactly behaviour want defensive programming packages. NA values must explicitly handled (e.g. NA ~ NA) unmatched = "error" set — missing values trigger error unless ’ve opted . smaller 1.2 changes worth knowing: dplyr::() gains ptype argument controlling output type (useful ordered factors). dplyr::if_else(), case_when(), coalesce() significantly faster thanks vctrs C rewrite. Returning != 1 row per group summarise() now defunct — use dplyr::reframe(). Underscored verbs (mutate_(), arrange_(), etc.) defunct. Use tidy evaluation. mutate_each() / summarise_each() defunct — use dplyr::across().
code: likert |>   dplyr::mutate(     category = score |>       dplyr::recode_values(         from = lookup$from,         to = lookup$to,         unmatched = "error"       )   ) # Errors immediately if score contains a value not in lookup$from

title: CLAUDE.md
text: ggplot2 4.0 rewrote internals S7 (dovetails nicely S7-first position elsewhere file). practical implications: Stricter input validation. Invalid property types error construction rather failing silently later. element_text(hjust = "foo") now errors immediately. @ S7-idiomatic accessor. plot@data rather plot$data. $ still works backwards compatibility phased . Extensions get double dispatch via update_ggplot() (successor ggplot_add()) — relevant writing geoms/stats/scales package.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Non-data styling (default colours, shapes, palettes) now lives theme via theme(geom = element_geom(...)). centralises look plot one place instead scattering across geom_*() calls. Themes now think ink (foreground), paper (background), accent roles rather raw colour/fill. Access theme property inside aes() ggplot2::from_theme(): Default palettes live theme , palette.{aesthetic}.{type}:
code: ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, ggplot2::aes(displ, hwy)) +   ggplot2::geom_point() +   ggplot2::geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x) +   ggplot2::theme_gray(paper = "cornsilk", ink = "navy", accent = "tomato") ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, ggplot2::aes(class, displ)) +   ggplot2::geom_boxplot(ggplot2::aes(colour = ggplot2::from_theme(accent))) +   ggplot2::theme(     geom = ggplot2::element_geom(accent = "tomato", paper = "cornsilk")   ) ggplot2::theme(   palette.colour.continuous = c("chartreuse", "forestgreen"),   palette.shape.discrete    = c("triangle", "triangle open", "triangle down open") )

title: CLAUDE.md
text: theme() lot long argument names. theme_sub_*() family less verbose ’re changing several related settings: available shortcuts theme_sub_axis() (_x/_y/_top/_bottom/_left/_right variants), theme_sub_legend(), theme_sub_panel(), theme_sub_plot(), theme_sub_strip(). Also: ggplot2::margin_auto(1, 2) CSS-style margin specification.
code: # Instead of this ggplot2::theme(   axis.ticks.x        = ggplot2::element_line(colour = "red"),   axis.ticks.length.x = grid::unit(5, "mm"),   panel.widths        = grid::unit(5, "cm"),   panel.spacing.x     = grid::unit(5, "mm"),   panel.background    = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = NA) )  # Use this ggplot2::theme_sub_axis_x(   ticks        = ggplot2::element_line(colour = "red"),   ticks.length = grid::unit(5, "mm") ) + ggplot2::theme_sub_panel(   widths       = grid::unit(5, "cm"),   spacing.x    = grid::unit(5, "mm"),   background   = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = NA) )

title: CLAUDE.md
text: ggplot2 4 picks label attribute columns default axis/legend title. makes labelled, Hmisc, gt workflows feel native. can also pass data-dictionary-style named vector via labs(dictionary = ...): Label priority (low → high): aes() expression → labs(dictionary) → column label attribute → labs(<aes> = ...) → scale_*(name) → guide_*(title). Functions accepted anywhere label taken, labs(y = \(x) paste0(x, " (log scale)")) works.
code: attr(df$bill_dep, "label") <- "Bill depth (mm)" ggplot2::ggplot(df, ggplot2::aes(bill_dep, bill_len)) + ggplot2::geom_point() # x-axis automatically reads "Bill depth (mm)" dict <- c(bill_dep = "Bill depth (mm)", bill_len = "Bill length (mm)") ggplot2::ggplot(penguins, ggplot2::aes(bill_dep, bill_len)) +   ggplot2::geom_point() +   ggplot2::labs(dictionary = dict)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Boxplot/violin styling — geom_boxplot() gains whisker.*, box.*, median.*, staple.* argument families. fatten deprecated favour median.linewidth. geom_violin() quantiles now computed real data, density. Position aesthetics — position_nudge() now exposes nudge_x/nudge_y mappable aesthetics. position_dodge() gains order aesthetic consistent group placement. stat_manual() — pass function transforms data frame skip whole Stat* class ceremony. Good quick custom stats. coord_*(reverse = "y") — flip axis direction coord level. Works coord_sf() scale_*_reverse() doesn’t. Discrete secondary axes via scale_x_discrete(sec.axis = dup_axis(...)). facet_wrap(dir = "br") etc. — eight filling directions via two-letter codes (tl, lt, tr, rt, bl, lb, br, rb). layer(layout = ...) — control data distributed across facet panels ("fixed", "fixed_rows", integer indices).

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Output type must predictable input types, never input values.
code: # Bad — type depends on the condition ifelse(cond, 1L, 2.0)  # Good — always the same type dplyr::if_else(cond, 1L, 2L)  # Use typed purrr variants when you know the return type purrr::map_dbl(xs, f)   # not purrr::map() when a numeric vector is wanted purrr::map_chr(xs, f) purrr::map_lgl(xs, f)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Output size predictable function input size (rows / rows , documented behaviour otherwise). Prediction functions return tibble one row per input row.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Functions called side effects (writing files, printing, plotting) must return primary input invisible() can live inside pipeline.
code: write_backup <- function(data, path) {   readr::write_csv(data, path)   invisible(data) }  data |>   write_backup("raw.csv") |>   dplyr::filter(keep) |>   write_backup("clean.csv")

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Don’t bury cat(), options(), Sys.setenv() inside function also computes result. Gate side effects behind explicit argument (verbose, quiet) move function.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: function offers multiple ways something, avoid boolean flags. Extract strategies helper constructors (regex(), fixed(), coll()) need different arguments. single enum (via rlang::arg_match()) enough strategies share arguments.
code: # Bad — what does perl = TRUE, fixed = TRUE even mean? grepl(pattern, x, perl = TRUE, fixed = FALSE, ignore.case = TRUE)  # Good — strategy objects carry their own arguments stringr::str_detect(x, stringr::regex(pattern, ignore_case = TRUE)) stringr::str_detect(x, stringr::fixed(pattern))

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Default purrr::map() friends. Reach genuinely iterative, side-effecting work step depends previous.
code: # Simple map results <- purrr::map(inputs, fit_model)  # Typed output mean_vec <- purrr::map_dbl(dfs, \(df) mean(df$value, na.rm = TRUE))  # Row-binding many data frames combined <- purrr::map(files, readr::read_csv) |> purrr::list_rbind()  # Two inputs in lockstep plots <- purrr::map2(datasets, titles, make_plot)  # Side effects only purrr::walk2(datasets, paths, readr::write_csv)  # Parallel (purrr 1.1+, requires mirai daemons) mirai::daemons(4) results <- purrr::map(inputs, purrr::in_parallel(\(x) expensive(x))) mirai::daemons(0)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use cli::cli_abort() user-facing errors. Pass call = caller_env() validators error points user’s code, internal helper. Error messages say ’s wrong . Prefer cli class styles ({.arg}, {.val}, {.fn}) consistent formatting.
code: validate_positive <- function(x, arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), call = rlang::caller_env()) {   if (!is.numeric(x) || any(x <= 0)) {     cli::cli_abort(       "{.arg {arg}} must be a positive numeric vector.",       call = call     )   }   invisible(x) }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Add dependency functionality gain real (string manipulation, dates, HTTP, modelling). Don’t add one trivial base R replacements. tidyverse core (dplyr, purrr, stringr, tidyr, rlang, cli) usually worth data-adjacent packages; avoid Imports: tidyverse package.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Inside every exported internal package function, call imports pkg::fn(). explicit, survives namespace changes, makes reviews easier. Reserve @importFrom infix operators (%||%, :=) generics define methods .

title: CLAUDE.md
code: #' @param var <[`data-masked`][dplyr::dplyr_data_masking]> Column to summarise. #' @param ... <[`dynamic-dots`][rlang::dyn-dots]> Passed to [dplyr::summarise()]. #' @param cols <[`tidy-select`][dplyr::dplyr_tidy_select]> Columns to select.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Default S7 new classes. S7 designed supersede S3 S4, CRAN status, tidyverse team reaches new projects. gives formal class definitions, automatic property validation construction mutation, safe @ property access, proper multiple dispatch, clear migration path S7 moves toward base R. Pick class system per-class, per-package. single package can mix systems appropriate: S7 — default new structured objects: properties, validators, inheritance, multiple dispatch. vctrs — vector-like objects live data frame column (custom dates, units, percentages, categoricals). Solves problems S7 doesn’t: coercion rules, arithmetic, vec_c() behaviour, tibble integration. Use vctrs::new_vctr() constructor. S3 — acceptable writing methods existing S3 generics (print, format, summary, .data.frame, as_tibble), extending packages whose class model S3. thin labelled wrapper nothing validate, S3 also fine — 2–3 slots validation , reach S7. S4 — working inside Bioconductor existing S4 codebase. R6 — genuinely need mutable reference semantics (rare; usually sign design reconsidered). Providing S3 methods S7 class. S7 S3 designed coexist. Every S7 object carries S3 class vector automatically, S3 generics (print, format, summary, plot, .data.frame, tibble::as_tibble, knitr::knit_print, dplyr::as_tibble, etc.) find methods ordinary S3 dispatch. idiomatic way make S7 objects feel native R ecosystem. two ways register S3 method S7 class. work; pick based whether generic S3-S7-aware. package development, Pattern 1 generally preferred — keeps methods class one visual location plays well S7::methods() introspection. Pattern 2 fine one-methods ’re gradually migrating S3 class S7. Either way, remember register S3 method NAMESPACE: Common S3 generics worth implementing user-facing S7 classes: Don’t write S3 method generic already S7 generic available. ’s S7 version need (S7::new_generic() existing one), define instead — get validation multiple dispatch. S3 methods bridge existing ecosystem, default choice new generics. Model objects. Never store training data match.call() inside model object — silently capture huge amounts state. Store predict() needs (coefficients, factor levels, preprocessing recipe).
code:
Range <- S7::new_class("Range",   properties = list(     start = S7::class_double,     end = S7::class_double   ),   validator = function(self) {     if (length(self@start) != 1 || length(self@end) != 1) {       "@start and @end must be length 1"     } else if (self@end < self@start) {       "@end must be >= @start"     }   } )  # Methods inside <- S7::new_generic("inside", "x") S7::method(inside, Range) <- function(x, y) {   y >= x@start & y <= x@end }  # Multiple dispatch — real strength over S3 combine <- S7::new_generic("combine", c("x", "y")) S7::method(combine, list(Range, Range))   <- function(x, y) ... S7::method(combine, list(Range, S7::class_numeric)) <- function(x, y) ... # Pattern 1: register with S7::method<-()  # Use for S3 generics that you want to feel first-class alongside your # other S7 methods. S7 registers the S3 method for you. S7::method(print, Range) <- function(x, ...) {   cat("<Range: [", x@start, ", ", x@end, "]>
", sep = "")   invisible(x) }  S7::method(format, Range) <- function(x, ...) {   sprintf("[%g, %g]", x@start, x@end) }  S7::method(summary, Range) <- function(object, ...) {   cat("Range of width", object@end - object@start, "
")   invisible(object) } # Pattern 2: write a plain S3 method using the class name # Use when you want the method to be visible as a regular S3 method # (easier for other packages to find via methods()) or when you prefer # the familiar S3 syntax. Both patterns dispatch identically. print.Range <- function(x, ...) {   cat("<Range: [", x@start, ", ", x@end, "]>
", sep = "")   invisible(x) } #' @export # (roxygen adds S3method(print, Range) automatically)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Test behaviour, implementation. Test error conditions testthat::expect_error(..., class = "my_error_class").
code: testthat::test_that("summarise_var returns a tibble with one row per group", {   result <- summarise_var(mtcars, mpg, .by = cyl)   testthat::expect_s3_class(result, "tbl_df")   testthat::expect_equal(nrow(result), 3) })  testthat::test_that("summarise_var supports tidyeval", {   testthat::expect_no_error(summarise_var(mtcars, mpg * 2))   var <- rlang::sym("mpg")   testthat::expect_no_error(summarise_var(mtcars, !!var)) })

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use gt table output package functions — summary methods, model coefficient tables, diagnostic outputs, anything user-facing. Don’t return raw data frames styled knitr::kable() kableExtra presentation tables. gt tables render cleanly HTML, LaTeX, Word, RTF, matters CRAN vignettes need build across formats. tables belong inside larger composed figure, gt integrates patchwork via patchwork::wrap_table().
code: summary_table <- function(fit) {   fit |>     broom::tidy() |>     gt::gt() |>     gt::fmt_number(columns = c(estimate, std.error), decimals = 3) |>     gt::fmt_number(columns = p.value, decimals = 4) |>     gt::cols_label(       term      = "Term",       estimate  = "Estimate",       std.error = "Std. Error"     ) }

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use patchwork combine ggplots (gt tables) multi-panel figures. Avoid gridExtra::grid.arrange() cowplot new code — patchwork cleaner API active development. package function returns composed figure, return patchwork object directly — users can add layers change layout without re-running function.
code: library(patchwork)  # Side by side p1 + p2  # Stacked p1 / p2  # Layout grammar (p1 | p2) / p3  # Collect shared legends (p1 | p2 | p3) + patchwork::plot_layout(guides = "collect")  # Mix a gt table in p1 + patchwork::wrap_table(gt_table)  # Annotate (p1 | p2) +   patchwork::plot_annotation(     title = "Model diagnostics",     tag_levels = "A"   )

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Write package vignettes .qmd files, .Rmd. Quarto produces nicer output, better cross-format handling, direction Posit broader R community moving. Minimal Quarto vignette setup DESCRIPTION: YAML header package vignette (vignettes/intro.qmd): quarto R package (CRAN) provides %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}. Keep one intro.qmd main “getting started” vignette, write task-specific ones separate files (modelling.qmd, plots.qmd, etc.). Make sure quarto (CLI) available CI — GitHub Actions, quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2 handles .
code: VignetteBuilder: quarto Suggests:     quarto --- title: "Introduction to <pkgname>" vignette: >   %\VignetteIndexEntry{Introduction to <pkgname>}   %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}   %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8} format:   html:     toc: true ---

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Every package going CRAN, build CRAN standards day one rather retrofitting submission time.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: DESCRIPTION — Title title case without “R package …”, Description full sentences ending full stops, Authors@R ORCID available, License standard SPDX identifier (e.g. MIT + file LICENSE, GPL (>= 3)). NAMESPACE — generated roxygen2, never edited hand. LICENSE file matches License field exactly. MIT use two-line LICENSE file pointing LICENSE.md. NEWS.md — required de facto, essential resubmission. One heading per version (# pkgname 0.1.0), bullet points changes, newest top. README.md — generated README.Rmd (README.qmd) via devtools::build_readme(). Include installation, minimal example, link pkgdown site. cran-comments.md — communication CRAN maintainers. Include R CMD check results list reverse dependencies checked.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Run clean every submission: Zero errors, zero warnings, zero notes target. Notes genuinely unavoidable (new submission, possibly-misspelled surnames) go cran-comments.md explanation.
code: devtools::check()                            # local R CMD check devtools::check(remote = TRUE, manual = TRUE) # includes URL + manual checks urlchecker::url_check()                      # every URL in the package devtools::spell_check()                      # DESCRIPTION, roxygen, vignettes goodpractice::gp()                           # broader hygiene rcmdcheck::rcmdcheck(args = "--as-cran")     # CRAN-specific checks

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Run R CMD check CRAN’s target platforms via GitHub Actions. Use usethis::use_github_action("check-standard") get canonical workflow covering: Linux (release) macOS (release) Windows (release) Linux (devel) Linux (oldrel-1) submission, additionally test : Reverse dependency checks matter second submission onward:
code: devtools::check_win_devel()    # win-builder, R-devel devtools::check_win_release()  # win-builder, R-release devtools::check_mac_release()  # mac-builder rhub::rhub_check()             # rhub v2, includes sanitizer/valgrind builds revdepcheck::revdep_check(num_workers = 4)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Examples must run < 5 seconds CRAN. Wrap slow examples \donttest{} (runs CRAN R CMD check ---cran) \dontrun{} (never runs). Prefer \donttest{} possible — examples still get checked. Vignettes tests must run < 10 minutes total. Skip slow things CRAN testthat::skip_on_cran(). Bayesian models usually means mocking fit using tiny toy dataset. writing user’s home directory, setting options() without restoring , install.packages() inside package. Use withr::local_*() state changes. cat() / print() package load. need startup messages use packageStartupMessage(), make suppressible. URLs must resolve redirect properly. urlchecker::url_check() catches issues; prefer canonical URLs shortened ones. Don’t ship files starting . inst/ anywhere ’ll installed (CRAN rejects ). LazyData: true package data. Otherwise omit — flagged otherwise. Use \doi{} \url{} Rd citations. Plain URLs trigger NOTEs. Non-ASCII characters source code data require explicit encoding declaration DESCRIPTION (Encoding: UTF-8). Package size — 5 MB installed soft target. Large data goes separate data package.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use semantic versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Development versions get fourth component (e.g. 0.1.0.9000). Bump via usethis::use_version() usethis::use_dev_version().

title: CLAUDE.md
text: usethis::use_release_issue() — creates checklist issue. Work checklist. devtools::submit_cran() — handles actual submission. Wait auto-check result; respond maintainer email. acceptance, usethis::use_github_release() tags releases.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: considering function done: Name verb (noun builder) shares prefix family. Primary data first argument. Required arguments defaults. ... sits required optional arguments. Internal arguments prefixed . ... forwards user data. String options use rlang::arg_match() enumerated default. Output type function input types, input values. Side-effect functions return input invisible()ly. hidden reads options(), locale, Sys.getenv(). Strategy variations use objects enums, boolean flags. User-facing errors go cli::cli_abort() call = caller_env(). Tidyeval arguments documented appropriate roxygen tag. Package function bodies use pkg::fn() namespacing. Dropping rows uses dplyr::filter_out(), filter() negation. Value-based recoding uses recode_values() / replace_values(), chained == case_when(). case_when() / recode_values() use .unmatched = "error" cases meant covered. New classes use S7 (unless writing S3 methods existing generics, building vector-like class vctrs). S7 classes validator covering invariants ’d otherwise check constructor. Tables use gt, plot composition uses patchwork, vignettes .qmd. Multi-step functions print "Step N: ..." messages, gated quiet argument. comment narration meta-comments (# Updated, # New) code. Bayesian fits run refresh = 0 compilation/sampling chatter suppressed. Slow examples wrapped \donttest{}; slow tests use testthat::skip_on_cran(). Package passes devtools::check(remote = TRUE, manual = TRUE) zero errors/warnings/notes.

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Magrittr pipe in new code data %>% dplyr::filter(x > 0)                        # use |>  # Character-vector join syntax dplyr::inner_join(x, y, by = c("a" = "b"))           # use join_by(a == b)  # Superseded purrr purrr::map_dfr(xs, f)                                # use map() |> list_rbind()  # Persistent grouping where .by would do data |> dplyr::group_by(g) |> dplyr::summarise(...) |> dplyr::ungroup()  # Growing a vector in a loop out <- c(); for (i in seq_along(x)) out <- c(out, f(x[[i]]))   # use purrr::map()  # Type-unstable base R sapply(xs, f)                                        # use purrr::map_*()  # String parsing as code eval(parse(text = paste0("mean(", var, ")")))       # use .data[[var]] or !!sym(var)  # Boolean strategy flags my_fn(x, use_fast = TRUE, use_parallel = FALSE)     # enum or strategy object  # Storing training data on a model model$training_data <- df                            # keep only what predict() needs  # Filtering out with negation and NA handling data |> dplyr::filter(!(deceased & date < 2012))     # use filter_out() data |> dplyr::filter(!cond & !is.na(cond))          # use filter_out()  # OR conditions nested in parens data |> dplyr::filter((a & b) | (c & d))             # use when_any()  # Chained ==s in case_when() dplyr::case_when(x == 1 ~ "a", x == 2 ~ "b", x == 3 ~ "c")  # use recode_values()  # case_match() in new code dplyr::case_match(x, ...)                            # soft-deprecated — use recode_values()  # Partial-update dance with case_when/if_else dplyr::if_else(is.na(state), "Unknown", state)       # use replace_values(NA ~ "Unknown")  # case_when() without .unmatched = "error" when you're sure you've covered everything dplyr::case_when(x == 1 ~ "a", x == 2 ~ "b")         # add .unmatched = "error" for safety  # $ access on a ggplot2 4 object plot$data                                             # use plot@data (S7 idiom)  # Hard-coding layer defaults instead of using the theme ggplot2::geom_point(colour = "navy", fill = "cornsilk")   # set via element_geom() in theme  # fatten in boxplots ggplot2::geom_boxplot(fatten = 2)                    # deprecated — use median.linewidth  # Defaulting to S3 for a new structured class structure(list(start = 1, end = 10), class = "range")  # use S7::new_class() with a validator  # $ access on an S7 object x$start                                              # use x@start — it's validated  # Narration comments # First, filter the data # Then fit the model # Return the result # — drop them, let names carry the meaning  # Meta / changelog comments in code # Updated: now handles NA # NEW: added weight argument # — these belong in NEWS.md, not in function bodies  # Loud Bayesian fits in package functions brms::brm(y ~ x, data = d)                           # add refresh = 0, silent = 2  # kableExtra or gridExtra in new package code knitr::kable(df) |> kableExtra::kable_styling()      # use gt::gt() gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1, p2)                      # use patchwork: p1 + p2  # .Rmd vignettes in new packages vignettes/intro.Rmd                                  # use intro.qmd + quarto engine  # Package writes to ~/ or changes options() without restoring options(digits = 3)                                  # use withr::local_options()

title: R coding preferences
text: Tidyverse syntax throughout. Native pipe |>, %>%. Assignment <-, =. purrr::map() family loops. Explicit pkg::fn() namespacing every function body. snake_case names. Dots function names reserved S3 dispatch.

title: R coding preferences
text: Lead answer. Bullets prose. Yes/question → yes/first, context . Don’t explain every detail unless asked.

title: R coding preferences
text: Minimal comments. Names carry meaning. running narration (# First, filter..., # fit...). meta/changelog comments (# Updated, # NEW, # Changed ...). “chose ” commentary chunks — put response text around chunk.

title: R coding preferences
text: Primary data first argument. Required args defaults. Optional args always . ... required optional args. Prefix internal args . ... forwards user data (., .f). String options → rlang::arg_match() enumerated default. Strategy variations → objects enums, boolean flags. Side-effect functions return input invisible(). Output type predictable input types, values.

title: R coding preferences
text: Bare column → embrace {{ var }}. String column → .data[[var]]. Character vector → dplyr::all_of(vars) across()/select(). Bare names via ... → forward ... directly. Dynamic output names → "{{ var }}" := rlang::englue(). Don’t parse strings code. Don’t get() inside data mask.

title: R coding preferences
text: Per-op grouping → ., group_by()/ungroup() sandwich. Joins → dplyr::join_by(id == user_id), = c("" = "b"). Dropping rows → dplyr::filter_out(), filter() negation. filter → dplyr::when_any(), nested parens. Value recode → dplyr::recode_values() / replace_values(), chained == case_when(). Add .unmatched = "error" cases meant covered. Row-bind many frames → purrr::map() |> purrr::list_rbind(), map_dfr(). Multi-row summaries → dplyr::reframe(). tidyr::pivot_longer()/pivot_wider(), gather/spread.

title: R coding preferences
text: User-facing errors always go cli::cli_abort() call = caller_env().
code: validate_x <- function(x, arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), call = rlang::caller_env()) {   if (bad(x)) {     cli::cli_abort("{.arg {arg}} must be ...", call = call)   }   invisible(x) }

title: R coding preferences
text: New structured class → S7 validator. Vector-like column class → vctrs. Writing S3 methods existing generics (print, format, summary, as_tibble) → S3, registered via S7::method(print, MyClass) <- .... Use @ S7 property access, $.

title: R coding preferences
text: Multi-step functions: print "Step N: <verb> <>" via cli::cli_inform(), gated quiet = FALSE argument. Bayesian fits: suppress compilation/sampling chatter.
code: # cmdstanr model$sample(data = d, refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE, show_exceptions = FALSE)  # brms brms::brm(y ~ x, data = d, backend = "cmdstanr", refresh = 0, silent = 2)

title: R coding preferences
text: Tables user-facing → gt::gt(), kableExtra. Plot composition → patchwork (p1 + p2, p1 / p2), gridExtra/cowplot. Vignettes → .qmd %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}, .Rmd.

title: R coding preferences
code: data %>% dplyr::filter(x > 0)                    # use |> dplyr::inner_join(x, y, by = c("a" = "b"))       # use join_by() purrr::map_dfr(xs, f)                            # use map() |> list_rbind() data |> dplyr::group_by(g) |> summarise(...) |> ungroup()   # use .by sapply(xs, f)                                    # use purrr::map_*() dplyr::filter(!cond & !is.na(cond))              # use filter_out() dplyr::case_when(x == 1 ~ "a", x == 2 ~ "b")     # use recode_values() dplyr::case_match(x, ...)                        # soft-deprecated structure(list(...), class = "range")            # use S7::new_class() x$start                                          # use x@start on S7 brms::brm(y ~ x, data = d)                       # add refresh=0, silent=2 knitr::kable(df) |> kableExtra::kable_styling()  # use gt::gt() options(digits = 3)                              # use withr::local_options()

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: Egger’s regression (Egger et al., 1997) detects funnel plot asymmetry regressing standardised effect estimates precision. symmetric funnel — expected pattern publication bias — regression passes origin. non-zero intercept indicates small-precision (small-sample) studies yield systematically different effects large-precision (large-sample) studies, pattern consistent publication bias small-study effects. bayesma implements fully Bayesian version Egger’s regression via egger(). Bayesian formulation provides: posterior distributions Egger intercept slope, just p-values flexible modelling residual heterogeneity (multiplicative additive) coherent quantification evidence asymmetry via Bayes factors

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: Following Shi et al. (2020), Bayesian Egger model yisi=α⋅si−1+β+εi \frac{y_i}{s_i} = \alpha \cdot s_i^{-1} + \beta + \varepsilon_i yi/siy_i / s_i standardised effect (z-score) sis_i standard error. Rearranging: yi=α+β⋅si+εi y_i = \alpha + \beta \cdot s_i + \varepsilon_i α\alpha effect infinite precision (publication bias) β\beta Egger regression coefficient: rate effect changes function standard error. Two models error term εi\varepsilon_i: Multiplicative heterogeneity (Egger’s original formulation): εi∼𝒩(0,κ2si2) \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \kappa^2 s_i^2)  κ\kappa multiplicative overdispersion factor. κ=1\kappa = 1 corresponds standard fixed-effect Egger model. Additive heterogeneity: εi∼𝒩(0,si2+τ2) \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, s_i^2 + \tau^2)  τ\tau -study heterogeneity, identical standard random-effects parameterisation. Priors: α∼𝒩(0,1),β∼𝒩(0,1),κ∼Half-Normal(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \kappa \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5)

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: Two models error term εi\varepsilon_i: Multiplicative heterogeneity (Egger’s original formulation): εi∼𝒩(0,κ2si2) \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \kappa^2 s_i^2)  κ\kappa multiplicative overdispersion factor. κ=1\kappa = 1 corresponds standard fixed-effect Egger model. Additive heterogeneity: εi∼𝒩(0,si2+τ2) \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, s_i^2 + \tau^2)  τ\tau -study heterogeneity, identical standard random-effects parameterisation. Priors: α∼𝒩(0,1),β∼𝒩(0,1),κ∼Half-Normal(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \kappa \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5)

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: heterogeneity = "multiplicative" reproduces original Egger model. "additive" preferable goal estimate publication-bias adjusted effect α\alpha allowing genuine heterogeneity.
code: egger_fit <- egger(   data,   heterogeneity = "additive" )  summary(egger_fit) egger_plot(egger_fit)

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: summary() returns: large |β||\beta| posterior mass away zero indicates funnel asymmetry. Bayes factor quantifies evidence continuous scale:

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: egger_plot() produces scatter plot standardised effects vs standard error fitted Egger regression line 95% credible band.

title: Bayesian Egger’s test
text: Egger’s test low power k<10k < 10. non-significant result rule publication bias; indicates data insufficient detect asymmetry measured precision. Funnel asymmetry can also arise causes publication bias: genuine small-study effects, heterogeneity correlated study size, artefacts effect size computation. positive Egger test prompt investigation (selection models, PET-PEESE) rather reflexive conclusion publication bias. visual complement Egger test, see Funnel Plots.

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: bias-corrected Bayesian non-parametric (BC-BNP) model Verde Rosner (2025) flexible approach publication bias adjustment impose parametric form selection mechanism. Unlike selection weight models (specify piecewise constant smooth weight function), PET-PEESE (assumes linear relationship effect standard error), BC-BNP model uses Bayesian non-parametric mixture model joint distribution true effects publication indicators. key properties BC-BNP model : Non-parametric flexibility: distribution true effects modelled infinite mixture using Dirichlet process prior. avoids committing parametric form heterogeneity. Bias correction: Publication probabilities estimated data, fixed advance. Coherent uncertainty: Uncertainty selection mechanism propagates posterior pooled effect.

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: Let (θi,δi)(\theta_i, \delta_i) denote true effect publication indicator study ii, δi=1\delta_i = 1 study published. published studies observed. joint distribution effects publication indicators modelled (θi,δi)∼G (\theta_i, \delta_i) \sim G GG drawn Dirichlet process: G∼DP(α0,G0) G \sim \text{DP}(\alpha_0, G_0) concentration parameter α0\alpha_0 base distribution G0G_0. selection mechanism enters publication probability: P(δi=1∣θi,si)=π(si,θi) P(\delta_i = 1 \mid \theta_i, s_i) = \pi(s_i, \theta_i) BC-BNP model, π\pi estimated non-parametrically function observed precision 1/si1/s_i effect θi\theta_i. likelihood observed data p(𝐲∣G)=∏=1k∫𝒩(yi∣θi,si2)⋅π(si,θi)⋅dG(θi) p(\mathbf{y} \mid G) = \prod_{=1}^{k} \int \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \theta_i, s_i^2) \cdot \pi(s_i, \theta_i) \cdot dG(\theta_i)

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: primary estimand marginal distribution θ\theta GG, averaged studies: μadj=𝔼G[θ] \mu_{\text{adj}} = \mathbb{E}_G[\theta] bias-corrected pooled effect. BC-BNP model also provides posterior predictive distribution θ\theta new study drawn (corrected) distribution.

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: Dirichlet process concentration α0\alpha_0 controls prior expected number distinct components mixture. Higher α0\alpha_0 allows clusters. bayesma uses Gamma(1,1)\text{Gamma}(1, 1) prior α0\alpha_0 default.

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: p_bias_prior controls prior probability given study suppressed. Beta(1,1)\text{Beta}(1, 1) prior (uniform) weakly informative.
code: fit_bcbnp <- bayesma(   data,   model_type    = "bc_bnp",   alpha_prior   = gamma(1, 1),   p_bias_prior  = beta(1, 1) )

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: BC-BNP model flexible three. informative large meta-analyses (k>20k > 20) non-parametric component can identified data. smaller meta-analyses, posterior μadj\mu_{\text{adj}} sensitive priors α0\alpha_0 π\pi.

title: BC-BNP model (Verde & Rosner 2025)
text: Dirichlet process prior strong influence posterior kk moderate, sensitivity analysis α0\alpha_0 recommended. Compare bias-corrected estimate across: bias-corrected estimate stable across , conclusion robust non-parametric specification.
code: fit_alpha1  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "bc_bnp", alpha_prior = gamma(1, 1)) fit_alpha5  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "bc_bnp", alpha_prior = gamma(1, 0.2)) fit_alpha10 <- bayesma(data, model_type = "bc_bnp", alpha_prior = gamma(1, 0.1))

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Verde (2021) proposed Bayesian hierarchical model explicitly represents risk bias study level. Unlike simple RoB subgroup analysis (discards high-risk studies) meta-regression RoB score (estimates correlation, causal adjustment), Verde’s model treats potential bias latent quantity partially adjusts within model. model shares hierarchical structure Verde Jung (2022; see Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe 2026)), source characterisation bias parameter differ.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Let yiy_i observed effect study ii bib_i latent bias study. model decomposes observed effect true effect bias component: yi∣θi,bi∼𝒩(θi+bi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i, b_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i + b_i,\, s_i^2) θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\, \tau^2) bi∣ρi∼𝒩(ρi⋅ξ,σb2) b_i \mid \rho_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\rho_i \cdot \xi,\, \sigma_b^2) : μ\mu bias-adjusted pooled true effect τ\tau -study heterogeneity true effects bib_i study-specific bias ρi∈[0,1]\rho_i \[0, 1] observed risk--bias indicator (composite score) ξ\xi mean bias per unit ρi\rho_i σb\sigma_b residual variability bias across studies ρi\rho_i ρi=0\rho_i = 0 (low risk bias), expected bias zero bib_i centred zero SD σb\sigma_b. ρi=1\rho_i = 1 (high risk), expected bias ξ\xi.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) ξ∼𝒩(0,0.5),σb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \xi \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5), \qquad \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.5) prior ξ\xi weakly informative centred zero, allowing data determine whether high-risk studies inflated deflated relative low-risk studies.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Verde (2021) Jung & Aloe (2026) variant share decomposition yi=θi+biy_i = \theta_i + b_i. key difference lies bib_i parameterised: Verde (2021) parsimonious; Jung & Aloe (2026) provides domain-specific bias estimates.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: rho_col name column data containing composite risk--bias score study, scaled [0,1][0, 1].
code: fit_verde <- bayesma(   data,   model_type  = "bias_corrected",   bias_source = "verde_2021",   rho_col     = "rob_composite_score" )  summary(fit_verde)

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: posterior μ\mu bias-adjusted pooled effect. posterior ξ\xi quantifies magnitude bias per unit RoB score. 95% credible interval ξ\xi excludes zero provides evidence high-risk studies report systematically different effects low-risk studies. posterior bib_i gives estimated bias study, can plotted alongside forest plot show studies affected.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: composite RoB score ρi\rho_i typically constructed domain ratings. Different aggregation schemes (simple average, weighted, domain-level indicators) yield different values ρi\rho_i hence different adjustments. Reporting results two aggregation schemes recommended.

title: Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Bias acts additively effect scale. direction bias across studies (positive ξ\xi means high-risk studies overestimate; negative means underestimate). relationship ρi\rho_i bias linear. Non-linear relationships require extended model.

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: Publication bias selection models address systematic inflation driven publication process. different equally important problem within-study bias: studies may report inflated effects due methodological limitations (low risk--bias items, selective reporting, inadequate blinding) rather selectively published. Jung & Aloe (2026) extend bias-adjusted framework Verde (2021) handle problem. model assigns study latent bias indicator estimates much bias inflates reported effect, still recovering valid estimate true underlying effect unbiased component. bayesma implements model via bayesma() model = "jung_aloe".

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: study ii latent bias indicator Ii∈{0,1}I_i \\{0, 1\}, Ii=1I_i = 1 means study biased. observed effect : yi∣Ii∼𝒩(θiB,si2) y_i \mid I_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta_i^B,\; s_i^2\right) apparent effect bias θiB=θi+Ii⋅βi \theta_i^B = \theta_i + I_i \cdot \beta_i θi\theta_i true study effect (study reported ideal conditions). βi\beta_i bias magnitude study ii (zero unbiased studies, positive upwardly biased ones). IiI_i binary bias indicator. True effects exchangeable across studies: θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) Bias magnitudes also treated random: βi∣μβ,τβ∼𝒩(μβ,τβ2),μβ≥0 \beta_i \mid \mu_\beta, \tau_\beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\beta,\; \tau_\beta^2), \quad \mu_\beta \geq 0 constraint μβ≥0\mu_\beta \geq 0 encodes assumption bias inflates rather deflates effects. Bias prevalence: Ii∣ϕ∼Bernoulli(ϕ),ϕ∼Beta(1,4) I_i \mid \phi \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\phi), \qquad \phi \sim \text{Beta}(1,\; 4) Beta(1, 4) prior places prior mass low bias prevalence, reflecting assumption studies well-conducted systematic review severely biased. μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) μβ∼Half-Normal(0,0.5),τβ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu_\beta \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\; 0.5), \qquad \tau_\beta \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5)

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: study ii latent bias indicator Ii∈{0,1}I_i \\{0, 1\}, Ii=1I_i = 1 means study biased. observed effect : yi∣Ii∼𝒩(θiB,si2) y_i \mid I_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta_i^B,\; s_i^2\right) apparent effect bias θiB=θi+Ii⋅βi \theta_i^B = \theta_i + I_i \cdot \beta_i θi\theta_i true study effect (study reported ideal conditions). βi\beta_i bias magnitude study ii (zero unbiased studies, positive upwardly biased ones). IiI_i binary bias indicator.

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: True effects exchangeable across studies: θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) Bias magnitudes also treated random: βi∣μβ,τβ∼𝒩(μβ,τβ2),μβ≥0 \beta_i \mid \mu_\beta, \tau_\beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\beta,\; \tau_\beta^2), \quad \mu_\beta \geq 0 constraint μβ≥0\mu_\beta \geq 0 encodes assumption bias inflates rather deflates effects. Bias prevalence: Ii∣ϕ∼Bernoulli(ϕ),ϕ∼Beta(1,4) I_i \mid \phi \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\phi), \qquad \phi \sim \text{Beta}(1,\; 4) Beta(1, 4) prior places prior mass low bias prevalence, reflecting assumption studies well-conducted systematic review severely biased.

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) μβ∼Half-Normal(0,0.5),τβ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu_\beta \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\; 0.5), \qquad \tau_\beta \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5)

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: key extension Jung & Aloe (2026) bias indicator probability ϕi\phi_i can informed risk--bias assessments (e.g., Cochrane RoB 2 tool INSPECT-SR). High-risk--bias studies receive higher prior probability biased: logit(ϕi)=α+γ⋅RoBi \text{logit}(\phi_i) = \alpha + \gamma \cdot \text{RoB}_i RoBi\text{RoB}_i numeric risk--bias score study ii. Without rob_scores, studies share ϕ\phi (Verde’s original formulation).
code: #| eval: false fit_jung <- bayesma(   data,   model      = "jung_aloe",   rob_scores = data$rob_score   # numeric RoB score per study )

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
code: #| eval: false # Without RoB scores (Verde 2021 version) fit_verde <- bayesma(data, model = "jung_aloe")  # With risk-of-bias scores (Jung & Aloe 2026) fit_jung <- bayesma(   data,   model      = "jung_aloe",   rob_scores = data$rob_score )  summary(fit_jung)

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: posterior mu primary quantity interest: estimates pooled true effect removing contribution study-level bias.

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: downward shift mu standard RE model bias-adjusted model indicates within-study bias inflating naive pooled estimate.
code: #| eval: false fit_re   <- bayesma(data) fit_jung <- bayesma(data, model = "jung_aloe", rob_scores = data$rob_score)  compare_models(fit_re, fit_jung, labels = c("Random-effects", "Jung & Aloe"))

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: bayesma integrates INSPECT-SR assessments. Risk--bias scores inspect_sr() can passed directly: See INSPECT-SR Assessment details computing rob_score.
code: #| eval: false rob <- inspect_sr(data) fit_jung <- bayesma(data, model = "jung_aloe", rob_scores = rob$rob_score)

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: model requires informative priors ϕ\phi (per-study RoB scores) identify bias component. Without prior information bias prevalence, bias parameters weakly identified. assumption βi≥0\beta_i \geq 0 (upward bias) checked subject-matter knowledge. Downward bias (e.g., measurement attenuation) requires setting μβ≤0\mu_\beta \leq 0. model assumes bias process independent true effect. may hold biased studies conducted precisely true effects small.

title: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe)
text: Verde PE (2021). bias-corrected meta-analysis model combining studies different types quality. Biometrical Journal, 63(2), 406–422. Jung Y, Aloe (2026). Bayesian bias-adjusted meta-analysis risk--bias information. Research Synthesis Methods.

title: Common-effect model
text: common-effect model simplest Bayesian meta-analysis model. assumes every study synthesis estimates underlying true effect θ\theta, observed differences studies arise entirely sampling error. assumption strong. implies every study infinite sample size, estimates converge value. practice defensible : studies share population, intervention, comparator, outcome kk small -study variation reliably estimated sensitivity check random-effects estimates wanted heterogeneity plausible, prefer random-effects model informative priors τ\tau.

title: Common-effect model
text: Let yiy_i denote effect estimate study =1,…,ki = 1, \ldots, k, sis_i known standard error. common-effect likelihood yi∣θ∼𝒩(θ,si2) y_i \mid \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta,\, s_i^2) weakly informative prior placed shared effect: θ∼𝒩(0,σθ2) \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_\theta^2) σθ\sigma_\theta set reflect plausible effect magnitudes analysis scale (log-, SMD, etc.). default bayesma 𝒩(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0, 1). posterior θ∣𝐲∝𝒩(0,σθ2)∏=1k𝒩(yi∣θ,si2) \theta \mid \mathbf{y} \propto \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2) \prod_{=1}^{k} \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \theta, s_i^2) conjugate Gaussian prior posterior also Gaussian: θ∣𝐲∼𝒩(μ̂,σ̂2) \theta \mid \mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\hat{\mu},\, \hat{\sigma}^2\right) σ̂−2=σθ−2+∑=1ksi−2,μ̂=σ̂2∑=1kyisi2 \hat{\sigma}^{-2} = \sigma_\theta^{-2} + \sum_{=1}^{k} s_i^{-2}, \qquad \hat{\mu} = \hat{\sigma}^2 \sum_{=1}^{k} \frac{y_i}{s_i^2} precision-weighted averaging: studies smaller standard errors contribute posterior.

title: Common-effect model
text: two-stage meta-analysis raw outcome data first summarised (yi,si)(y_i, s_i) combined using Gaussian likelihood . default bayesma continuous outcomes log-scale binary outcomes.

title: Common-effect model
text: binary outcomes, two-stage analysis discards within-study information treating sis_i known. one-stage common-effect model replaces normal approximation exact binomial likelihood: rij∼Binomial(nij,πij),j∈{ctrl,trt} r_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij},\, \pi_{ij}), \quad j \\{\text{ctrl}, \text{trt}\} logit(πij)=γi+θ⋅𝟙[j=trt] \text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + \theta \cdot \mathbb{1}[j = \text{trt}] γi\gamma_i study-specific baseline log-odds (nuisance parameters) θ\theta common log-odds ratio. γi\gamma_i appear fixed effects rather random effects, one-stage common-effect model — heterogeneity baseline risk absorbed study dummies.

title: Common-effect model
text: posterior θ\theta lives analysis scale (log-, log-RR, SMD, etc.). bayesma reports posterior median equal-tailed 95% credible interval scale , applicable, back-transforms natural scale (, RR, etc.).

title: Common-effect model
text: common-effect model single parameter, posterior sensitive σθ\sigma_\theta kk small standard errors large. sensitivity check wider narrower priors (e.g., 𝒩(0,0.5)\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5) 𝒩(0,2)\mathcal{N}(0, 2)) recommended.

title: Common-effect model
text: common-effect model estimate -study heterogeneity. heterogeneity exists, pooled estimate μ̂\hat{\mu} still consistent posterior credible interval narrow — quantifies uncertainty study-average effect, distribution effects across target population studies. applied meta-analyses, random-effects model preferred starting point. common-effect model best viewed boundary case diagnostic tool.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: Gaussian random-effects model default meta-analysis model bayesma. assumes true effects across studies exchangeable draws normal distribution, -study variability governed heterogeneity parameter τ\tau. correct starting point strong reason assume every study estimates identical effect. common-effect model (studies share one θ\theta) limiting case τ=0\tau = 0. See Common-Effect Model preferable.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: Let yiy_i effect estimate sis_i known standard error study =1,…,ki = 1, \ldots, k. two-level hierarchy : Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\; s_i^2) Random-effects distribution: θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) Marginalising study-specific effects θi\theta_i gives integrated likelihood used inference: yi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,si2+τ2) y_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; s_i^2 + \tau^2) Studies smaller sis_i still receive weight, weight differential narrows τ\tau grows.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: bayesma defaults: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) Half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) prior τ\tau weakly informative: assigns prior mass small heterogeneity keeping moderate--large values within plausible range. effect sizes standardised scales (log-, SMD), heterogeneity τ=1\tau = 1 already large. Priors can overridden via prior_mu prior_tau arguments: See Prior Predictive Checks Sensitivity Analysis guidance.
code: #| eval: false fit <- bayesma(   data,   prior_mu  = normal(0, 0.5),   prior_tau = half_normal(0, 0.5) )

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: summary() output reports posterior median 95% credible interval μ\mu, τ\tau, I2I^2, along convergence diagnostics.
code: #| eval: false fit_re <- bayesma(data)  # Gaussian RE is the default  summary(fit_re)

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: posterior μ\mu quantifies mean distribution true effects. best estimate new, exchangeable study find average. τ\tau standard deviation true-effect distribution. Larger τ\tau means variation true effects across studies. posterior τ\tau examined directly — substantial mass near zero, data consistent negligible heterogeneity. bayesma reports posterior distribution I2I^2, defined : I2=τ2τ2+s̃2 ^2 = \frac{\tau^2}{\tau^2 + \tilde{s}^2} s̃2\tilde{s}^2 typical within-study variance. I2I^2 relative measure: proportion total observed variance attributable -study heterogeneity. depends τ\tauand precision primary studies, interpreted alongside τ\tau rather isolation. 95% prediction interval new study’s true effect μ±1.96τ \mu \pm 1.96\,\tau wider credible interval μ\mu reported summary() prediction_interval = TRUE.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: posterior μ\mu quantifies mean distribution true effects. best estimate new, exchangeable study find average.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: τ\tau standard deviation true-effect distribution. Larger τ\tau means variation true effects across studies. posterior τ\tau examined directly — substantial mass near zero, data consistent negligible heterogeneity.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: bayesma reports posterior distribution I2I^2, defined : I2=τ2τ2+s̃2 ^2 = \frac{\tau^2}{\tau^2 + \tilde{s}^2} s̃2\tilde{s}^2 typical within-study variance. I2I^2 relative measure: proportion total observed variance attributable -study heterogeneity. depends τ\tauand precision primary studies, interpreted alongside τ\tau rather isolation.

text: 95% prediction interval new study’s true effect μ±1.96τ \mu \pm 1.96\,\tau wider credible interval μ\mu reported summary() prediction_interval = TRUE.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: Gaussian random-effects model assumes: Exchangeability: true effects θi\theta_i drawn distribution. requires studies sufficiently similar population, intervention, comparator, outcome justify pooling. Normal random-effects distribution: distribution true effects symmetric. implausible — example, outlier studies suspected — consider Alternative RE Distributions RE Mixture Models. Known within-study variances: si2s_i^2 treated fixed estimated value. approximation deteriorates small studies.

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: produces table τ\tau, I2I^2, H2H^2 posterior intervals. visual assessment see Funnel Plots Posterior Predictive Checks.
code: #| eval: false bayesma_output(fit_re, type = "heterogeneity")

title: Gaussian random-effects model
text: random-effects model appropriate default meta-analyses. Reserve common-effect model planned sensitivity analyses, exchangeability genuinely implausible question (e.g., replications study team), number studies small τ\tau estimated reliably. Bayesian model comparison via compare_models() provides formal evidence heterogeneity:
code: #| eval: false fit_ce <- bayesma(data, model = "common_effect") compare_models(fit_ce, fit_re, labels = c("Common-effect", "Random-effects"))

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: MCMC inference valid chains converged target posterior. Convergence failure produces unreliable estimates, overconfident credible intervals, incorrect Bayes factors. Every bayesma fit pass convergence diagnostics results reported.

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: diagnostics() returns summary convergence statistics. Key statistics also printed automatically quiet = FALSE (default).
code: fit <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect") diagnostics(fit)

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: R̂\hat{R} compares variance parameter within chains variance across chains. value close 1 indicates chains mixed. threshold R̂<1.01\hat{R} < 1.01 reliable inference (Vehtari et al., 2021). Values 1.05 indicate mixing failure.
code: diagnostics(fit) |>   dplyr::filter_out(rhat < 1.01)

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: ESS estimates number independent samples correlated MCMC chain. Two variants: Bulk ESS: measures mixing centre distribution (relevant means medians). Tail ESS: measures mixing tails (relevant 95% credible intervals). Thresholds: bulk ESS > 400 per parameter; tail ESS > 400 per parameter. Low tail ESS τ\tau common kk small posterior τ\tau near-zero.

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: Divergent transitions occur HMC integrator fails track posterior geometry. indicate pathological posterior, often caused funnel-shaped geometry near τ=0\tau = 0. Zero divergences target. divergences require investigation fit can trusted. Common solutions:
code: diagnostics(fit)$divergences

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: Visual inspection trace plots catches mixing failures summary statistics miss: Well-mixing chains look like “hairy caterpillars” — rapid, uncorrelated oscillation across full posterior range. Signs poor mixing include: Stuck chains: one chains staying one region many iterations. Slow mixing: autocorrelated, sluggish movement across parameter space. Bimodal chains: abrupt jumps two regions.
code: bayesplot::mcmc_trace(fit$draws(), pars = c("mu", "tau"))

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: Pair plots reveal correlations parameters can show characteristic funnel geometry: banana-shaped joint distribution (μ,τ)(\mu, \tau) near τ=0\tau = 0 expected harmless NCP used. tight funnel chains fail explore warning sign.
code: bayesplot::mcmc_pairs(fit$draws(), pars = c("mu", "tau"), off_diag_args = list(size = 0.5))

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: fitting, LOO-IC computes per-observation Pareto k̂\hat{k} statistics. Values 0.7 flag observations whose LOO contribution unreliable: Studies k̂>0.7\hat{k} > 0.7 influential. Refit without studies (LOSO-CV) verify pooled estimate robust.
code: fit$loo()$diagnostics

title: Model fitting and convergence diagnostics
text: published meta-analysis using bayesma, report: Number chains iterations (warmup + sampling). adapt_delta value used. Maximum R̂\hat{R} across parameters. Minimum bulk tail ESS. Number divergent transitions. parameter R̂>1.01\hat{R} > 1.01 ESS < 400, resolution taken.

title: Custom models
text: bayesma covers broad range meta-analytic models, applied synthesis sometimes requires departures built-options. package supports three levels customisation increasing flexibility complexity.

what: Level 1: Modify priors or add constraints
title: Custom models
text: simplest customisation changes prior distributions, without touching model structure. Example: Use informative prior τ\tau published prior predictive analysis. requires knowledge *_prior arguments. See ?priors available constructors: Constraints pooled effect can added via mu_constraint: restricts μ>0\mu > 0 — appropriate direction effect known priori (e.g., toxin beneficial).
code: fit_informative <- bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   tau_prior  = half_normal(0, 0.25),   mu_prior   = normal(0, 0.5) ) normal(mu, sigma) half_normal(mu, sigma) half_cauchy(mu, sigma) half_student_t(nu, mu, sigma) exponential(rate) uniform(lower, upper) fit_positive <- bayesma(   data,   model_type    = "random_effect",   mu_constraint = "positive" )

title: Custom models
text: second level modifies Stan data adds blocks generated model without replacing core likelihood. workflow : Extract autogenerated Stan code bayesma(data, return_stage = "code"). Edit Stan code. Pass modified code back bayesma() via custom_model. Example: Add study-level covariate random-effects model. custom_data argument passes additional data blocks Stan. Stan code must declare matching entries data {} block.
code: base_code <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "code")  modified_code <- base_code |>   stringr::str_replace(     "vector\\[N\\] y;",     "vector[N] y;\n  vector[N] x_cov;"   ) |>   stringr::str_replace(     "target \\+= normal_lpdf\\(y \\| mu \\+ u\\[study\\], se\\);",     "target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + beta * x_cov + u[study], se);"   )  fit_extended <- bayesma(   data,   custom_model = modified_code,   custom_data  = list(x_cov = data$covariate) )

title: Custom models
text: flexible option passes complete Stan program written scratch. bayesma provides data preparation pipeline posterior extraction infrastructure; user supplies model. data block must include minimum: one-stage binary models, replace y se : compatibility bayesma_extract() bayesma_output(), generated quantities {} block must export b_Intercept (pooled effect): three-level (study > sample > effect) model dependent effects: Pass bayesma():
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;          // number of observations   vector[N] y;             // effect estimates   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   // standard errors   array[N] int<lower=1> study;  // study indicator (for RE models) } array[N] int<lower=0> events;   array[N] int<lower=1> n;   vector[N] treat; generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; } data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;        // number of studies   int<lower=1> J;        // number of samples   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study;   array[N] int<lower=1> sample; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau_study;   real<lower=0> tau_sample;   vector[K] z_study;   vector[J] z_sample; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u_study  = tau_study  * z_study;   vector[J] u_sample = tau_sample * z_sample; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_study  | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_sample | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_study);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_sample);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u_study[study] + u_sample[sample], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; } fit_three_level <- bayesma(   data,   custom_model = three_level_stan,   custom_data  = list(J = n_samples, sample = data$sample_id) )

title: Custom models
text: data block must include minimum: one-stage binary models, replace y se :
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;          // number of observations   vector[N] y;             // effect estimates   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   // standard errors   array[N] int<lower=1> study;  // study indicator (for RE models) } array[N] int<lower=0> events;   array[N] int<lower=1> n;   vector[N] treat;

title: Custom models
text: compatibility bayesma_extract() bayesma_output(), generated quantities {} block must export b_Intercept (pooled effect):
code: generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Custom models
text: three-level (study > sample > effect) model dependent effects: Pass bayesma():
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;        // number of studies   int<lower=1> J;        // number of samples   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study;   array[N] int<lower=1> sample; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau_study;   real<lower=0> tau_sample;   vector[K] z_study;   vector[J] z_sample; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u_study  = tau_study  * z_study;   vector[J] u_sample = tau_sample * z_sample; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_study  | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_sample | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_study);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_sample);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u_study[study] + u_sample[sample], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; } fit_three_level <- bayesma(   data,   custom_model = three_level_stan,   custom_data  = list(J = n_samples, sample = data$sample_id) )

title: Custom models
text: Custom models checked : Identifiability: pairs() plots correlated parameters reveal non-identifiable structure. Prior coverage: Prior predictive simulation produce plausible ranges parameters. Convergence: R̂<1.01\hat{R} < 1.01 bulk ESS > 400 parameters. Divergent transitions: Zero divergences adapt_delta = 0.95. Use non-centred parameterisation random effects (examples ) reduce divergences.

title: Custom models
text: Custom models bypass built-argument validation. user responsible ensuring model correctly specified, identified, custom_data list contains variables referenced Stan data {} block.

title: Funnel plots
text: funnel plot displays study’s effect estimate measure precision (typically standard error). common-effect assumption systematic bias, study effects scatter symmetrically around pooled estimate, larger scatter lower precision. produces characteristic inverted-funnel shape. Asymmetry funnel — studies clustered one side, especially low precision — can indicate publication bias, small-study effects, genuine associations study size effect magnitude. bayesma produces funnel plots via funnel_plot(), optional contour lines Shi et al. (2020) pseudo-confidence regions based latent standard errors.

title: Funnel plots
text: default produces scatter plot yiy_i (x-axis) vs sis_i (y-axis, inverted), y-axis running 0 top max(si)\max(s_i) bottom. pooled effect line overlaid.
code: funnel_plot(fit)

title: Funnel plots
text: Contour lines p=0.10p = 0.10, 0.050.05, 0.010.01 (two-sided) show statistical significance regions. Studies outside p<0.05p < 0.05 contour significant null effect. cluster studies just inside p<0.05p < 0.05 boundary — studies outside — consistent selective reporting significant results.
code: funnel_plot(fit, contour = TRUE)

title: Funnel plots
text: Studies sometimes report effects scales mix signal noise ways make standard error unreliable precision proxy. Shi et al. (2020) proposed plotting effects latent standard error implied model, separates publication-bias-related precision sampling variation. latent SE funnel plot particularly informative context Bayesian Egger model.
code: funnel_plot(fit, se_type = "latent")

title: Funnel plots
text: grouping variable available, studies can coloured subgroup: useful investigating whether funnel asymmetry driven particular subgroup.
code: funnel_plot(fit, colour_by = "risk_of_bias_domain")

title: Funnel plots
text: Asymmetry multiple possible causes: funnel plot starting point investigation, conclusive test. Formal asymmetry tests (Egger, Bayesian Egger’s test) model-based adjustments (PET-PEESE, selection models) follow.

title: Funnel plots
text: funnel_plot() supports following estimands, automatic back-transformation display: Log-odds ratio () Log-risk ratio (RR) Log-hazard ratio (HR) Log-incidence rate ratio (IRR) Mean difference (MD) Standardised mean difference (SMD)

title: Funnel plots
code: fit_re <- bayesma(dat_binary, model_type = "random_effect")  funnel_plot(   fit_re,   contour   = TRUE,   colour_by = "year_quartile",   label     = FALSE )

title: INSPECT-SR: Assessing the Trustworthiness of RCTs
text: systematic review trustworthy primary studies includes. Risk bias tools assess whether trial conducted properly — designed detect whether reported data real. INSPECT-SR (INveStigating ProblEmatic Clinical Trials Systematic Reviews; Wilkinson et al., 2025) structured 21-item tool assessing trustworthiness RCTs included systematic review. bayesma implements full workflow four steps: inspect_sr() — run assessment (frequentist Bayesian) inspect_sr_table() — inspect per-check details inspect_plot() / inspect_summary_plot() — visualise filter_trustworthy() — filter meta-analysis

title: INSPECT-SR: Assessing the Trustworthiness of RCTs
text: domain receives concerns, concerns, Serious concerns. overall judgement severe domain. bayesma automates checks 4.3 (Carlisle), 4.6 (N consistency), 4.8 (GRIM), 4.9 (p-value verification); remaining 17 items read input data frame.

code: data(inspect_sr_example)  # One row per study — manual items as columns, variable-length data as list-columns dplyr::glimpse(dplyr::select(inspect_sr_example, study, d1_1, d2_1, d3_1, d4_1)) Rows: 8 Columns: 5 $ study <chr> "Callahan (1994)", "Peek (2005)", "Clayton (2010)", "Singer (200… $ d1_1  <chr> "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No … $ d2_1  <chr> "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "Som… $ d3_1  <chr> "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", NA, … $ d4_1  <chr> "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No concerns", "No … # Baseline data for the first study inspect_sr_example$baseline[[1]] variable mean_int sd_int mean_ctrl sd_ctrl p_value integer_scale 1       Age     71.2    8.3      69.8     9.1    0.45         FALSE 2       BMI     24.8    4.1      25.1     3.9    0.72         FALSE 3 ASA_score      2.5    0.5       2.5     0.5    0.34          TRUE # Test statistics for a study with two tests inspect_sr_example$statistics[[2]] test_type statistic  df df2 reported_p              context 1    chi_sq      4.12   1  NA       0.04   Delirium incidence 2         t     -0.85 437  NA       0.40 Duration of delirium freq_results <- inspect_sr(inspect_sr_example, studyvar = study) INSPECT-SR Trustworthiness Assessment ==================================================  Study                     D1    D2    D3    D4       Overall ---------------------------------------------------------------------- Callahan (1994)           OK    OK    OK    OK       No concerns Peek (2005)               OK    OK    OK    OK       No concerns Clayton (2010)            OK    OK    OK    OK       No concerns Singer (2003)             OK    OK    OK    OK       No concerns Underwood (2013)          OK    SOME  --    OK       Some concerns Rosa (2008)               OK    SOME  --    SERIOUS  Serious concerns Doe (1995)                SERIOUS SERIOUS SOME  SERIOUS  Serious concerns Kint (1995)               --    --    --    SERIOUS  Serious concerns  Domains: D1 post-publication, D2 conduct/governance,          D3 text/figures, D4 results (auto-filled for 4.3/4.6/4.8/4.9) OK = No concerns, SOME = Some concerns, SERIOUS = Serious concerns -- = Not assessed  For a per-check table, call inspect_sr_table(). # All automated check details inspect_sr_table(freq_results) # Only failures inspect_sr_table(freq_results, only_failed = TRUE) # Individual Tables # E.g. Callaghan study inspect_sr_table(freq_results, study = "Callaghan (1994)") inspect_plot(   freq_results,   add_legend = TRUE,   title    = "INSPECT-SR Trustworthiness Assessment",   subtitle = "EEG-guided anaesthesia and postoperative delirium" ) inspect_plot(freq_results, sort_studies_by = "overall",              title = "INSPECT-SR Assessment (sorted by concern level)") inspect_plot(freq_results, incl_checks = TRUE,              title = "INSPECT-SR per-item view") inspect_summary_plot(freq_results,                      title = "Distribution of Trustworthiness Judgements") # Primary analysis: exclude Serious concerns data_primary <- filter_trustworthy(   my_meta_data, freq_results,   studyvar = study, exclude = "serious" )  # Sensitivity: also exclude Some concerns data_strict <- filter_trustworthy(   my_meta_data, freq_results,   studyvar = study, exclude = "some" )  attr(data_primary, "excluded_studies")
