code: bayes_results <- inspect_sr(inspect_sr_example, studyvar = study, bayes = TRUE) Bayesian INSPECT-SR Trustworthiness Assessment ======================================================================  Study                        Prior  Posterior         BF  Interpretation ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ Callahan (1994)              0.900      0.999      0.010  Trustworthy (no concerns) Peek (2005)                  0.900      1.000      0.003  Trustworthy (no concerns) Clayton (2010)               0.900      0.996      0.036  Trustworthy (no concerns) Singer (2003)                0.900      0.995      0.049  Trustworthy (no concerns) Underwood (2013)             0.900      0.999      0.011  Trustworthy (no concerns) Rosa (2008)                  0.900      0.484      9.590  Likely untrustworthy (serious concerns) Doe (1995)                   0.900      0.000        Inf  Likely untrustworthy (serious concerns) Kint (1995)                  0.900      0.288     22.240  Likely untrustworthy (serious concerns)  BF: combined Bayes factor against trustworthiness (>1 = evidence against) Posterior: P(trustworthy | data), prior = 0.9 >0.90 no concerns, 0.50-0.90 some concerns, <0.50 serious inspect_sr_table(bayes_results, only_failed = TRUE) r90 <- inspect_sr(inspect_sr_example, studyvar = study, bayes = TRUE,                   prior_prob_trustworthy = 0.90, verbose = FALSE) r75 <- inspect_sr(inspect_sr_example, studyvar = study, bayes = TRUE,                   prior_prob_trustworthy = 0.75, verbose = FALSE) r50 <- inspect_sr(inspect_sr_example, studyvar = study, bayes = TRUE,                   prior_prob_trustworthy = 0.50, verbose = FALSE)  data.frame(   Study   = r90$Study,   Post_90 = round(r90$Posterior, 3),   Post_75 = round(r75$Posterior, 3),   Post_50 = round(r50$Posterior, 3) ) Study Post_90 Post_75 Post_50 1  Callahan (1994)   0.999   0.997   0.990 2      Peek (2005)   1.000   0.999   0.997 3   Clayton (2010)   0.996   0.988   0.965 4    Singer title: INSPECT-SR: Assessing the Trustworthiness of RCTs
text: assessed trustworthiness included RCTs using INSPECT-SR (Wilkinson et al., 2025). Domain 4 checks automated using bayesma R package (GRIM test, p-value verification, Carlisle’s test, participant number consistency). Domains 1–3 assessed manually two independent reviewers. Studies Serious concerns excluded; concerns studies retained sensitivity analysis.

title: INSPECT-SR: Assessing the Trustworthiness of RCTs
text: Brown NJL, Heathers JAJ (2017). GRIM test. Social Psychological Personality Science, 8(4), 363–369. Carlisle JB (2017). Data fabrication reasons non-random sampling 5,087 RCTs. Anaesthesia, 72(8), 944–952. Wilkinson J, Heal C, Flemyng E, et al. (2025). INSPECT-SR: tool assessing trustworthiness randomised controlled trials. BMJ, 389, e082938.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: Many clinical psychological interventions produce effects evolve time. studies report outcomes multiple follow-points, longitudinal meta-analysis (LMA) models trajectory treatment effect across time, rather selecting single time-point pooling. LMA answers questions univariate meta-analysis : treatment effect grow, decay, plateau time? time-point effect largest? evidence long-term short-term effects differ?

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: Let yity_{} effect estimate study ii time t∈{t1,…,tTi}t \\{t_1, \ldots, t_{T_i}\}, known standard error sits_{}. longitudinal model two levels. Level 1 (within-study): yit∣θit∼𝒩(θit,sit2) y_{} \mid \theta_{} \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_{},\, s_{}^2) Level 2 (-study trajectory): θit=f(t;𝛍)+ui+vit \theta_{} = f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) + u_i + v_{} : f(t;𝛍)f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) population-level trajectory (parametric non-parametric) ui∼𝒩(0,τu2)u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau_u^2) study-level random intercept vit∼𝒩(0,τv2)v_{} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau_v^2) study--time random deviation

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: f(t;𝛍)=μ0+μ1t f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_0 + \mu_1 t Appropriate effect changes roughly constant rate. μ0\mu_0 effect t=0t = 0 (baseline) μ1\mu_1 rate change per unit time. f(t;𝛍)=μ∞+(μ0−μ∞)e−λt f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) e^{-\lambda t} Appropriate effects peak near treatment initiation decay toward long-run asymptote μ∞\mu_\infty. λ>0\lambda > 0 controls decay rate. f(t;𝛍)=μ0+μ1t+∑jμ2j(t−κj)+ f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_0 + \mu_1 t + \sum_j \mu_{2j} (t - \kappa_j)_+ κj\kappa_j knot positions (x)+=max(0,x)(x)_+ = \max(0, x). Flexible enough capture non-monotone trajectories.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: f(t;𝛍)=μ0+μ1t f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_0 + \mu_1 t Appropriate effect changes roughly constant rate. μ0\mu_0 effect t=0t = 0 (baseline) μ1\mu_1 rate change per unit time.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: f(t;𝛍)=μ∞+(μ0−μ∞)e−λt f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) e^{-\lambda t} Appropriate effects peak near treatment initiation decay toward long-run asymptote μ∞\mu_\infty. λ>0\lambda > 0 controls decay rate.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: f(t;𝛍)=μ0+μ1t+∑jμ2j(t−κj)+ f(t;\, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mu_0 + \mu_1 t + \sum_j \mu_{2j} (t - \kappa_j)_+ κj\kappa_j knot positions (x)+=max(0,x)(x)_+ = \max(0, x). Flexible enough capture non-monotone trajectories.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: μ0,μ1∼𝒩(0,1),τu,τv∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu_0, \mu_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \quad \tau_u, \tau_v \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) Trajectory-specific parameters (e.g., λ\lambda, μ∞\mu_\infty) require domain-informed priors. Exponential decay requires λ>0\lambda > 0; Half-Normal(0,1)\text{Half-Normal}(0, 1) prior reasonable weakly informative choice.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: time_col argument names column containing time variable. trajectory accepts "linear", "exponential", "spline".
code: fit_lma <- bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   time_col   = "weeks",   trajectory = "linear" )

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: bayesma() returns posterior trajectory f(t)f(t) evaluated user-specified grid via bayesma_marginal(). can used : plot trajectory pointwise credible bands identify time maximum effect compare trajectories across subgroups (via meta_reg())

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: Studies rarely measure outcomes identical time-points. model handles naturally — study contributes (yit,sit,t)(y_{}, s_{}, t) triplets, trajectory estimated across studies simultaneously. imputation missing time-points needed.

title: Longitudinal meta-analysis
text: Longitudinal models require time measured comparably across studies (origin, units). trajectory form must specified. Model comparison trajectory specifications supported via compare_models(). k<10k < 10 heterogeneous time-points, τu\tau_u τv\tau_v poorly identified.

title: Meta-regression
text: Meta-regression extends random-effects model relating -study variation true effects study-level covariates (moderators). primary tool investigating effects vary across studies. Common moderators include: participant characteristics (mean age, proportion female, disease severity) intervention characteristics (dose, duration, delivery mode) study characteristics (year, risk bias, country income level) outcome characteristics (follow-duration, outcome instrument)

title: Meta-regression
text: Let xijx_{ij} denote jj-th moderator study ii. meta-regression model yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) θi=μ+∑j=1pβjxij+ui,ui∼𝒩(0,τ2) \theta_i = \mu + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} + u_i, \qquad u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2) : μ\mu intercept (effect moderators reference values) βj\beta_j moderating effect variable jj τ\tau residual -study heterogeneity unexplained moderators Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),βj∼𝒩(0,0.5),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) default β\beta prior weakly informative. Domain-specific priors can specified via beta_priors.

title: Meta-regression
text: formula argument uses standard R formula syntax. center = TRUE centres continuous moderators mean; scale = TRUE scales unit standard deviation. Centering strongly recommended improve sampling efficiency interpretability intercept.
code: fit_mr <- meta_reg(   data,   formula     = ~ intervention_duration + mean_age + risk_of_bias,   model_type  = "random_effect",   center      = TRUE,   scale       = TRUE )

title: Meta-regression
text: coefficient_evidence() returns posterior median, 95% credible interval, Bayes factor null hypothesis βj=0\beta_j = 0: βj\beta_j credible interval excludes zero indicates evidence moderation. Bayes factor quantifies evidence favour H1:βj≠0H_1 : \beta_j \neq 0 relative H0:βj=0H_0 : \beta_j = 0.
code: coefficient_evidence(fit_mr)

title: Meta-regression
text: metareg_mod_plot() plots posterior regression line credible band moderator. bubble_plot() produces bubble plot bubble size represents study precision.
code: metareg_mod_plot(fit_mr, moderator = "intervention_duration") bubble_plot(fit_mr, moderator = "mean_age")

title: Meta-regression
text: Categorical moderators dummy-coded automatically. reference category first level factor. Effect sizes category relative reference βj\beta_j posteriors corresponding dummy variables.
code: fit_mr_cat <- meta_reg(   data,   formula = ~ intervention_type,   model_type = "random_effect" )

title: Meta-regression
text: meta-regression model moderators compared intercept-random-effects model: τ\tau decreases substantially adding moderators, moderators explain meaningful proportion heterogeneity. Rτ2=1−τ̂adjusted2/τ̂unadjusted2R^2_\tau = 1 - \hat{\tau}^2_\text{adjusted} / \hat{\tau}^2_\text{unadjusted} quantifies proportion heterogeneity explained. τ\tau still large adding measured moderators, substantial unexplained heterogeneity remains.

title: Meta-regression
text: Meta-regression estimates association study-level moderators study-level effects. ecological association: identify causal patient-level effects. intervention works better studies older mean age necessarily work better older individuals within study.

title: Meta-regression
text: Overfitting. k<10k < 10 per moderator, regression estimates unreliable. rough rule, allow k/10k/10 moderators. Multiple testing. Testing many moderators inflates false discovery rates. Report tested moderators, significant ones. Confounding. Study-level moderators may correlated. Interpret individual βj\beta_j estimates cautiously moderators share variance. Missing data. Studies missing moderator values dropped default. Imputation sensitivity analysis needed missingness substantial.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: mixture model approach publication bias adjustment (Maier et al., 2023, metamix) models observed distribution effects mixture two components: studies reporting genuine effects studies reflecting publication-bias-inflated estimates. Unlike selection weight models (reweight existing studies) PET-PEESE (adjusts via regression), mixture model directly represents two-population structure hypothesised publication bias: component unbiased studies component overestimates survived selection filter.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: study ii assigned latent membership zi∈{0,1}z_i \\{0, 1\}: zi=0z_i = 0: study draws unbiased distribution zi=1z_i = 1: study draws biased distribution likelihood p(yi∣θ,σθ,μb,σb,πb)=(1−πb)⋅𝒩(yi∣θ,σθ2+si2)+πb⋅𝒩(yi∣μb,σb2+si2) p(y_i \mid \theta, \sigma_\theta, \mu_b, \sigma_b, \pi_b) = (1 - \pi_b) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \theta, \sigma_\theta^2 + s_i^2) + \pi_b \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_b, \sigma_b^2 + s_i^2) : θ\theta true pooled effect (unbiased component mean) σθ\sigma_\theta true -study heterogeneity (unbiased component SD) μb>θ\mu_b > \theta mean biased component (constrained exceed true effect) σb\sigma_b SD biased component πb\pi_b probability published study belongs biased component constraint μb>θ\mu_b > \theta encodes assumption publication bias inflates effects upward (positive direction). negative effects, constraint μb<θ\mu_b < \theta.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: θ∼𝒩(0,1),σθ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \sigma_\theta \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) μb∼𝒩(θ+δb,σb2),δb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \mu_b \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta + \delta_b, \sigma_b^2), \quad \delta_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.5) σb∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),πb∼Beta(1,4) \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5), \qquad \pi_b \sim \text{Beta}(1, 4) Beta(1,4)\text{Beta}(1, 4) prior πb\pi_b assigns prior mass primarily small proportions biased studies, encoding assumption published studies genuine.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: θ\theta: bias-corrected pooled effect, estimated unbiased component . πb\pi_b: posterior probability typical published study drawn biased component. Mixture-averaged effect: (1−πb)θ+πbμb(1 - \pi_b)\theta + \pi_b \mu_b — effect estimated naive meta-analysis ignoring mixture structure.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
code: fit_mix <- bayesma(   data,   model_type   = "mixture_model",   p_bias_prior = beta(1, 4) )  summary(fit_mix)

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: posterior πb\pi_b quantifies evidence biased subpopulation. 95% credible interval πb\pi_b excludes zero provides evidence existence biased studies. posterior θ\theta provides bias-corrected estimate.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: mixture model selection weight models (Vevea-Hedges, Copas) approach problem different angles: selection primarily pp-value based, selection models better theoretically motivated. selection mechanism unknown involves factors beyond significance, mixture model agnostic.

title: Mixture model (Maier)
text: mixture model sensitive prior πb\pi_b. sensitivity analysis comparing Beta(1,1)\text{Beta}(1, 1) (uniform) Beta(1,9)\text{Beta}(1, 9) (strong prior towards bias) recommended.

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: Fitting multiple models raises question: model best describes data? bayesma provides two complementary tools — formal predictive model comparison via compare_models() visual diagnostics via compare_plot() ecdf_model_plot().

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: primary comparison criterion leave-one-study-cross-validation (LOSO-CV). study ii, model fitted remaining k−1k - 1 studies used predict withheld study’s effect. predictive quality measured continuous ranked probability score (CRPS), averaged studies. CRPS proper scoring rule rewards sharpness (narrow predictive distributions) penalises miscalibration (predictive distributions miss true value). predictive distribution FiF_i observed value yiy_i: CRPS(Fi,yi)=∫−∞∞[Fi(x)−𝟙(x≥yi)]2dx \text{CRPS}(F_i, y_i) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[F_i(x) - \mathbb{1}(x \geq y_i)\right]^2 dx Lower CRPS better. total LOSO-CRPS CRPS¯=1k∑=1kCRPS(F−,yi) \overline{\text{CRPS}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{=1}^{k} \text{CRPS}(F_{-}, y_i) Cross-stage comparability. One-stage two-stage models fitted different likelihoods produce predictive distributions effect-size scale. LOSO-CV effect scale makes comparable. Diagnostic value. Per-study CRPS scores identify individual studies model predicts poorly — often studies drive sensitivity analysis results. distributional assumption. CRPS require predictive distribution Gaussian.

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: Cross-stage comparability. One-stage two-stage models fitted different likelihoods produce predictive distributions effect-size scale. LOSO-CV effect scale makes comparable. Diagnostic value. Per-study CRPS scores identify individual studies model predicts poorly — often studies drive sensitivity analysis results. distributional assumption. CRPS require predictive distribution Gaussian.

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: Within-stage model comparison (e.g., comparing RE distributions likelihood) can use LOO information criterion (LOO-IC) widely applicable information criterion (WAIC). approximate leave-one-observation-CV log-score scale. WAIC=−2(∑ilogp¯(yi∣θ)−∑iVarθ[logp(yi∣θ)]) \text{WAIC} = -2\left(\sum_i \log \overline{p}(y_i \mid \theta) - \sum_i \text{Var}_\theta[\log p(y_i \mid \theta)]\right) LOO-IC computed via Pareto-smoothed importance sampling reports Pareto k̂\hat{k} diagnostics observation. Studies k̂>0.7\hat{k} > 0.7 influential LOO contribution may unreliable; LOSO-CV preferable cases.

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
code: fit_ce  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "common_effect") fit_re  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect") fit_t   <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", re_dist = "t")  comp <- compare_models(   "Common-effect" = fit_ce,   "RE Gaussian"   = fit_re,   "RE Student-t"  = fit_t )  compare_table(comp) compare_plot(comp)

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: compare_table() returns ranked tibble columns: Differences loso_crps less 0.05 log-scale typically negligible. Prefer simpler models differences small.

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: compare_plot() shows prediction interval coverage nominal level (50%, 80%, 90%, 95%). perfectly calibrated model lies diagonal. Models diagonal intervals wide (conservative); models diagonal intervals narrow (anti-conservative).

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: $study_scores element compare_models() result contains per-study CRPS coverage statistics. Large per-study CRPS values flag studies systematically mispredicted — useful diagnostic identifying influential outliers subgroups require separate modelling.
code: comp$study_scores |>   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(loso_crps)) |>   head(5)

title: Model comparison and diagnostics
text: LOSO-CV appropriate tool selecting model without potential moderator — moderator comparison question explanation, prediction fixed set studies. Use posterior inclusion probabilities meta_reg() robma() moderator selection. Similarly, comparing bias-corrected model uncorrected model CRPS conflates model fit bias correction — model perfectly reproduces biased data outscore correctly specified bias-adjusted model. See Bias & Heterogeneity appropriate diagnostics.

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: Formal model comparison bayesma uses predictive accuracy criteria. vignette covers widely applicable information criterion (WAIC), LOO information criterion (LOO-IC), leave-one-study-cross-validation (LOSO-CV), explaining use interpret output. comparison workflow function reference, see Model Comparison & Diagnostics.

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: WAIC (Watanabe, 2010) approximates expected log predictive density new data: WAIC=−2(∑=1nlogp¯(yi∣𝛉)−∑=1nVarpost[logp(yi∣𝛉)]) \text{WAIC} = -2\left(\sum_{=1}^{n} \log \overline{p}(y_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) - \sum_{=1}^{n} \text{Var}_{\text{post}}[\log p(y_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})]\right) first term -sample log predictive density; second term penalises model complexity (effective number parameters). Lower WAIC better. WAIC fully Bayesian, using entire posterior distribution rather point estimate. can computed directly MCMC samples without refitting.
code: fit$waic()

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: LOO-IC approximates leave-one-observation-cross-validation using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling (PSIS-LOO; Vehtari et al., 2017): LOO-IC=−2∑=1nlogp(yi∣𝐲−) \text{LOO-IC} = -2 \sum_{=1}^{n} \log p(y_i \mid \mathbf{y}_{-}) PSIS-LOO reliable WAIC heavy-tailed posteriors provides per-observation diagnostics (Pareto k̂\hat{k}). Pareto k̂\hat{k} values: - k̂<0.5\hat{k} < 0.5: LOO reliable - 0.5≤k̂<0.70.5 \leq \hat{k} < 0.7: LOO somewhat reliable - k̂≥0.7\hat{k} \geq 0.7: LOO unreliable observation; use LOSO-CV
code: fit$loo()

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: LOSO-CV (leave-one-study-) refits model kk times, time withholding one study predicting remaining k−1k-1 studies. gold standard meta-analytic model comparison : exact (approximation). defined effect-size scale, enabling comparison across one-stage two-stage models. naturally handles influential studies (high-k̂\hat{k} observations LOO). LOSO-CV computationally expensive (kk additional fits per model). Use primary model comparison candidates screened LOO-IC.
code: compare_models(model1, model2, criterion = "loso")

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: model preferred loso_crps lowest coverage well-calibrated (close nominal levels). model lower CRPS miscalibrated coverage investigated . WAIC LOO-IC differences non-nested models (e.g., random-effects Gaussian vs random-effects Student-tt) can tested using standard error difference: z=ΔLOO-IC2⋅σ̂Δ z = \frac{\Delta\text{LOO-IC}}{2 \cdot \hat{\sigma}_{\Delta}} large |z||z| indicates difference unlikely sampling noise. However, null hypothesis (two models equally good) interpreted cautiously: small CRPS difference may practically negligible even statistically significant.
code: comp <- compare_models(   "Common effect" = fit_ce,   "RE Gaussian"   = fit_re,   "RE Student-t"  = fit_t,   criterion       = "loso" )  compare_table(comp) compare_plot(comp)

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: model preferred loso_crps lowest coverage well-calibrated (close nominal levels). model lower CRPS miscalibrated coverage investigated .

title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: WAIC LOO-IC differences non-nested models (e.g., random-effects Gaussian vs random-effects Student-tt) can tested using standard error difference: z=ΔLOO-IC2⋅σ̂Δ z = \frac{\Delta\text{LOO-IC}}{2 \cdot \hat{\sigma}_{\Delta}} large |z||z| indicates difference unlikely sampling noise. However, null hypothesis (two models equally good) interpreted cautiously: small CRPS difference may practically negligible even statistically significant.

what: When model comparison is not appropriate
title: Model comparison (WAIC / LOO)
text: Nested models simpler model null. Use Bayes factors (via robma()) instead LOO-IC testing H0:τ=0H_0: \tau = 0 vs H1:τ>0H_1: \tau > 0. Non-comparable likelihoods. LOO-IC different likelihoods (e.g., log-vs log-RR) comparable; use LOSO-CV common effect scale. Bias-corrected vs uncorrected models. models answering different questions; model comparison misleading. Use sensitivity analysis instead.

title: Multiarm models
text: multiarm (multi-treatment) trial assigns participants three arms — typically control two active interventions. Including multiarm studies pairwise meta-analysis raises two problems: Unit analysis error. Using active arm separate comparison control double-counts shared control arm, inflating precision producing correlated residuals. Missing comparisons. one arm-pair extracted, information arms discarded. one-stage approach bayesma handles problems modelling full multiarm data structure jointly.

title: Multiarm models
text: Let study ii AiA_i arms. Arm jj study ii contributes rijr_{ij} events nijn_{ij} participants (binary outcome). treatment indicator arm jj tij∈{0,1,…,T}t_{ij} \\{0, 1, \ldots, T\} TT number active treatments. rij∼Binomial(nij,πij) r_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij},\, \pi_{ij}) logit(πij)=γi+∑t=1Tθt⋅𝟙[tij=t]+ϵij \text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \theta_t \cdot \mathbb{1}[t_{ij} = t] + \epsilon_{ij} : γi\gamma_i study-specific baseline log-odds (control arm) θt\theta_t pooled log-treatment tt vs control ϵij\epsilon_{ij} study--treatment random effect capturing heterogeneity ϵij\epsilon_{ij} arms within study correlated. bayesma uses LKJ prior correlation matrix Ω\Omega among random effects: 𝛜i∼𝒩(𝟎,diag(𝛕)⋅Ω⋅diag(𝛕)) \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\, \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) \cdot \Omega \cdot \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau})) Ω∼LKJ(η) \Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(\eta) LKJ prior η=1\eta = 1 uniform correlation matrices. Larger η\eta concentrates mass near identity (.e., independent arms).

what: Why the one-stage approach avoids unit-of-analysis error
title: Multiarm models
text: one-stage model control arm appears per study (γi\gamma_i), active arm contributes additional θt+ϵij\theta_t + \epsilon_{ij} term. shared control arm enters likelihood single time, contribution precision double-counted. within-study correlation arm-level estimates handled covariance structure 𝛜i\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_i.

title: Multiarm models
text: multiarm = TRUE flag activates correlated random-effects structure. data must include arm column identifying arm treatment column coding treatment label.
code: fit <- bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   stage      = "one_stage",   multiarm   = TRUE,   rho_prior  = lkj(eta = 1) )

text: bayesma() returns posterior θt\theta_t (treatment vs control). Pairwise treatment contrasts θa−θb\theta_a - \theta_b can derived joint posterior:
code: bayesma_extract(fit, contrast = c("treatment_a", "treatment_b"))

title: Multiarm models
text: LKJ prior Ω\Omega limited influence number multiarm studies small (<5< 5). setting within-study correlation poorly estimated model reduces approximately independent arm-level analyses. Models many treatments (T>4T > 4) may better handled network meta-analysis frameworks (e.g., gemtc, multinma). Multiarm models computationally heavier pairwise models due correlation matrix. Increase adapt_delta (default 0.95 multiarm) divergent transitions occur.

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: Many systematic reviews report multiple outcomes per study — example, primary efficacy endpoint safety endpoint, outcome measured multiple time-points. Analysing outcome independently discards within-study correlation outcomes can yield inconsistent conclusions (e.g., significant benefit primary endpoint secondary, two strongly correlated within studies). Multivariate meta-analysis (MVMA) models outcomes jointly, exploiting within-study correlation improve estimation efficiency providing coherent inference across outcomes.

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: Let 𝐲i=(yi1,…,yiP)⊤\mathbf{y}_i = (y_{i1}, \ldots, y_{iP})^\top vector PP effect estimates study ii, known within-study covariance matrix 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i. MVMA model 𝐲i∣𝛉i∼𝒩P(𝛉i,𝐒i) \mathbf{y}_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}_i \sim \mathcal{N}_P(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i,\, \mathbf{S}_i) 𝛉i∼𝒩P(𝛍,𝚺) \boldsymbol{\theta}_i \sim \mathcal{N}_P(\boldsymbol{\mu},\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}) : 𝛍=(μ1,…,μP)⊤\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_P)^\top vector pooled effects 𝚺=diag(𝛕)⋅Ω⋅diag(𝛕)\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) \cdot \Omega \cdot \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) -study covariance matrix τp\tau_p -study standard deviation outcome pp Ω\Omega -study correlation matrix Priors: μp∼𝒩(0,σp2),τp∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),Ω∼LKJ(η) \mu_p \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_p^2), \quad \tau_p \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5), \quad \Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(\eta)

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: within-study covariance 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i typically unknown must approximated. bayesma supports: Known correlations. within-study correlation ρwithin\rho_\text{within} known (e.g., individual participant data published correlation matrices), 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i constructed marginal variances sip2s_{ip}^2 ρwithin\rho_\text{within}. Imputed correlations. ρwithin\rho_\text{within} reported, plausible value (typically 0.5) imputed. Sensitivity choice reported. Riley approximation. Riley et al. (2008) proposed marginalising ρwithin\rho_\text{within} uniform prior, avoiding need point estimate.

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: outcomes argument names columns containing per-outcome effect estimates; se_cols names standard error columns. rho_within accepts scalar (applied uniformly) list study-specific values.
code: fit_mv <- bayesma_mv(   data,   outcomes  = c("lnOR_primary", "lnOR_safety"),   se_cols   = c("se_primary", "se_safety"),   rho_within = 0.5 )

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: key benefit MVMA borrowing strength across outcomes. outcome pp observed subset studies, model uses -study correlation impute θip\theta_{ip} studies report . valid missing outcomes missing random — .e., probability reporting depend unreported outcome value.

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: Multivariate meta-analysis adds complexity. useful : outcomes strongly correlated within studies (|ρwithin|>0.3|\rho_\text{within}| > 0.3) outcomes partially missing across studies joint inference across outcomes required (e.g., benefit–risk trade-) outcomes weakly correlated fully observed, separate univariate analyses produce nearly identical estimates.

title: Multivariate meta-analysis
text: -study correlation Ω\Omega estimated -study variation. studies (k<10k < 10) many outcomes (P>3P > 3), Ω\Omega poorly identified posterior estimates depend heavily LKJ prior. Robust heterogeneity models (Student-tt RE, skew-normal RE) multivariate case computationally demanding currently supported model-type combinations.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: Meta-analyses binary outcomes (events vs. non-events) can conducted using either two-stage one-stage approach. distinction matters studies small, baseline event rates vary widely, outcome rare. Two-stage: study first summarised log-scale effect estimate yiy_i standard error sis_i, meta-analysis uses Gaussian likelihood summaries. default bayesma adequate applications. One-stage: original binomial counts modelled directly, avoiding normal approximation handling studies zero events without continuity corrections. bayesma implements one-stage model following Jackson et al. (2018).

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: study ii treatment arm (r1i,n1i)(r_{1i}, n_{1i}) control arm (r0i,n0i)(r_{0i}, n_{0i}), log odds ratio yi=log(r1i/(n1i−r1i)r0i/(n0i−r0i)) y_i = \log\!\left(\frac{r_{1i}/(n_{1i}-r_{1i})}{r_{0i}/(n_{0i}-r_{0i})}\right) approximate variance si2=1r1i+1n1i−r1i+1r0i+1n0i−r0i s_i^2 = \frac{1}{r_{1i}} + \frac{1}{n_{1i}-r_{1i}} + \frac{1}{r_{0i}} + \frac{1}{n_{0i}-r_{0i}} Log-risk ratios risk differences supported via effect_measure argument. two-stage random-effects model applies standard Gaussian hierarchy log-scale summaries: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2),θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\; s_i^2), \qquad \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) See Gaussian Random-Effects Model full details. normal approximation log-poor cell counts small (ri<5r_i < 5). Studies zero events require continuity correction, shifts estimate ways depend correction chosen. Within-study information baseline event rates discarded.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: study ii treatment arm (r1i,n1i)(r_{1i}, n_{1i}) control arm (r0i,n0i)(r_{0i}, n_{0i}), log odds ratio yi=log(r1i/(n1i−r1i)r0i/(n0i−r0i)) y_i = \log\!\left(\frac{r_{1i}/(n_{1i}-r_{1i})}{r_{0i}/(n_{0i}-r_{0i})}\right) approximate variance si2=1r1i+1n1i−r1i+1r0i+1n0i−r0i s_i^2 = \frac{1}{r_{1i}} + \frac{1}{n_{1i}-r_{1i}} + \frac{1}{r_{0i}} + \frac{1}{n_{0i}-r_{0i}} Log-risk ratios risk differences supported via effect_measure argument.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: two-stage random-effects model applies standard Gaussian hierarchy log-scale summaries: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2),θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\; s_i^2), \qquad \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) See Gaussian Random-Effects Model full details.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: normal approximation log-poor cell counts small (ri<5r_i < 5). Studies zero events require continuity correction, shifts estimate ways depend correction chosen. Within-study information baseline event rates discarded.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: one-stage model replaces Gaussian approximation exact binomial likelihood. formulation follows Jackson et al. (2018). study ∈{1,…,k}\\{1, \ldots, k\} arm j∈{0(ctrl),1(trt)}j \\{0\;\text{(ctrl)},\; 1\;\text{(trt)}\}: rij∼Binomial(nij,πij) r_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij},\; \pi_{ij}) logit(πij)=γi+θi⋅j \text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + \theta_i \cdot j γi\gamma_i study-specific log-odds control (baseline log-odds) θi\theta_i study-specific log-odds ratio. Treatment effects exchangeable across studies: θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) Baseline log-odds receive weakly informative priors: γi∼𝒩(0,σγ2),σγ=2 \gamma_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; \sigma_\gamma^2), \quad \sigma_\gamma = 2 covering wide range baseline event rates logit scale. μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) match defaults two-stage model. See Prior Predictive Checks outcome-specific guidance. study rij=0r_{ij} = 0 contributes valid binomial log-likelihood term — probability Binomial(0∣n,π)\text{Binomial}(0 \mid n, \pi) defined informative. nuisance parameters γi\gamma_i integrated MCMC, uncertainty baseline risk propagates posteriors μ\mu τ\tau.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: study ∈{1,…,k}\\{1, \ldots, k\} arm j∈{0(ctrl),1(trt)}j \\{0\;\text{(ctrl)},\; 1\;\text{(trt)}\}: rij∼Binomial(nij,πij) r_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij},\; \pi_{ij}) logit(πij)=γi+θi⋅j \text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + \theta_i \cdot j γi\gamma_i study-specific log-odds control (baseline log-odds) θi\theta_i study-specific log-odds ratio. Treatment effects exchangeable across studies: θi∣μ,τ∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \mid \mu, \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \tau^2) Baseline log-odds receive weakly informative priors: γi∼𝒩(0,σγ2),σγ=2 \gamma_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; \sigma_\gamma^2), \quad \sigma_\gamma = 2 covering wide range baseline event rates logit scale.

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) match defaults two-stage model. See Prior Predictive Checks outcome-specific guidance.

text: study rij=0r_{ij} = 0 contributes valid binomial log-likelihood term — probability Binomial(0∣n,π)\text{Binomial}(0 \mid n, \pi) defined informative. nuisance parameters γi\gamma_i integrated MCMC, uncertainty baseline risk propagates posteriors μ\mu τ\tau.

code: #| eval: false  # Two-stage (default for binary outcomes with pre-computed summaries) fit_2s <- bayesma(   data,   outcome        = "binary",   effect_measure = "log_or",   model_stage    = "two_stage" )  # One-stage (Jackson et al.) — expects raw count columns r1, n1, r0, n0 fit_1s <- bayesma(   data,   outcome        = "binary",   effect_measure = "log_or",   model_stage    = "one_stage" )  summary(fit_1s)

title: One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes
text: Jackson D, Law M, Stijnen T, Viechtbauer W, White IR (2018). comparison 7 random-effects models meta-analyses estimate summary odds ratio. Statistics Medicine, 37(7), 1059–1085.

title: PET-PEESE
text: PET-PEESE (Stanley & Doucouliagos, 2014) regression-based method detecting correcting small-study effects — tendency smaller, less precise studies report larger effects, pattern consistent publication bias inflated estimates underpowered research. key insight publication bias, small-sample studies survive publication filter produce large effects. creates correlation standard error effect size. PET PEESE use regression estimate effect reported study infinitely large (.e., SE→0SE \0). bayesma implements fully Bayesian version PET-PEESE via bayesma() model = "pet_peese".

title: PET-PEESE
text: PET regresses effect estimate standard error: yi=α+β⋅SEi+εi y_i = \alpha + \beta \cdot SE_i + \varepsilon_i intercept α\alpha estimates true effect infinite precision (SEi=0SE_i = 0). slope β\beta captures association standard error effect size: positive β\beta indicates less precise studies report larger effects. error term allows residual heterogeneity: εi∼𝒩(0,si2+τ2) \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; s_i^2 + \tau^2) use PET: PET correct specification true effect zero. intercept estimate unbiased case. true effect non-zero, SEiSE_i term regression absorbs real effect, biasing α̂\hat{\alpha} downward.

what: PEESE — Precision Effect Estimate with Standard Error
title: PET-PEESE
text: PEESE replaces linear SEiSE_i term SEi2SE_i^2: yi=α+β⋅SEi2+εi y_i = \alpha + \beta \cdot SE_i^2 + \varepsilon_i quadratic specification better calibrated true effect non-zero: SEi2SE_i^2 term changes slowly imprecise studies, leaving true effect intercept. PEESE therefore preferred primary bias-correction model prior evidence suggests non-zero effect.

title: PET-PEESE
text: α∼𝒩(0,1),β∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) defaults. prior β\beta weakly informative symmetric — enforce direction small-study effects.

title: PET-PEESE
text: summary() reports posterior α\alpha (bias-corrected effect), β\beta (small-study effect slope), τ\tau (residual heterogeneity).
code: #| eval: false fit_pet   <- bayesma(data, model = "pet") fit_peese <- bayesma(data, model = "peese")  summary(fit_peese)

title: PET-PEESE
text: Stanley & Doucouliagos (2014) proposed two-step decision rule: Fit PET. PET intercept α\alpha clearly different zero (95% CI excludes 0), proceed PEESE. Report PEESE intercept bias-corrected effect estimate. Bayesian version rule uses posterior probability αPET≠0\alpha_\text{PET} \neq 0: bayesma, Bayes factors α\alpha computed using Savage-Dickey density ratio, providing continuous measure evidence vs. non-zero effect.
code: #| eval: false # Fit both fit_pet   <- bayesma(data, model = "pet") fit_peese <- bayesma(data, model = "peese")  # Posterior probability of non-zero effect (PET intercept) interpret(fit_pet, parameter = "alpha")  # Report PEESE if PET provides clear evidence of non-zero effect summary(fit_peese)

title: PET-PEESE
text: PET-PEESE assumes small-study effects source funnel asymmetry. Genuine small-study effects (e.g., smaller studies targeting different populations) can mimic publication bias. method low power k<20k < 20 performs poorly heterogeneity large relative precision range included studies. true heterogeneity publication bias exist, PET-PEESE may overcorrect. comprehensive approach models multiple sources bias simultaneously, see Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA), averages PET-PEESE selection model assumptions.

title: PET-PEESE
text: Stanley TD, Doucouliagos H (2014). Meta-regression approximations reduce publication selection bias. Research Synthesis Methods, 5(1), 60–78.

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: posterior predictive check (PPC) evaluates whether fitted model can generate data resembles observed data. model well-specified, datasets simulated posterior predictive distribution statistically indistinguishable observed dataset. PPCs complement formal model comparison (LOO-IC, LOSO-CV): model comparison tells model better, PPCs tell whether best model actually good.

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: pp_check() generates SS replicated datasets posterior predictive distribution overlays observed data.
code: fit <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect") pp_check(fit)

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: common PPC plots density observed effect estimates density multiple simulated datasets: observed density (dark line) well within envelope simulated densities (light lines), model captures distributional shape data. Plots quantiles observed data corresponding quantiles posterior predictive distribution: Systematic deviations specific quantiles reveal model misfit particular effect sizes (e.g., model underestimates many studies small effects). PPCs can assessed specific summary statistics: mean, SD, minimum, maximum, user-defined functions. posterior predictive pp-value statistic TT pPPC=P(T(𝐲rep)≥T(𝐲)∣𝐲) p_\text{PPC} = P(T(\mathbf{y}^\text{rep}) \geq T(\mathbf{y}) \mid \mathbf{y}) Values near 0 1 indicate model misfit dimension captured TT. LOO predictive check plots observed yiy_i leave-one-posterior predictive median interval: LOO probability integral transform (PIT) approximately uniform predictive distributions well-calibrated.
code: pp_check(fit, type = "dens_overlay", n_sims = 100) pp_check(fit, type = "ecdf_overlay") pp_check(fit, type = "stat", stat = "mean") pp_check(fit, type = "stat", stat = "sd") pp_check(fit, type = "stat_2d", stat1 = "mean", stat2 = "sd") pp_check(fit, type = "loo_pit_overlay")

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: common PPC plots density observed effect estimates density multiple simulated datasets: observed density (dark line) well within envelope simulated densities (light lines), model captures distributional shape data.
code: pp_check(fit, type = "dens_overlay", n_sims = 100)

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: Plots quantiles observed data corresponding quantiles posterior predictive distribution: Systematic deviations specific quantiles reveal model misfit particular effect sizes (e.g., model underestimates many studies small effects).
code: pp_check(fit, type = "ecdf_overlay")

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: PPCs can assessed specific summary statistics: mean, SD, minimum, maximum, user-defined functions. posterior predictive pp-value statistic TT pPPC=P(T(𝐲rep)≥T(𝐲)∣𝐲) p_\text{PPC} = P(T(\mathbf{y}^\text{rep}) \geq T(\mathbf{y}) \mid \mathbf{y}) Values near 0 1 indicate model misfit dimension captured TT.
code: pp_check(fit, type = "stat", stat = "mean") pp_check(fit, type = "stat", stat = "sd") pp_check(fit, type = "stat_2d", stat1 = "mean", stat2 = "sd")

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: LOO predictive check plots observed yiy_i leave-one-posterior predictive median interval: LOO probability integral transform (PIT) approximately uniform predictive distributions well-calibrated.
code: pp_check(fit, type = "loo_pit_overlay")

title: Posterior predictive checks
text: Heavy tails. model predicts extreme effects. Switch Student-tt random-effects distribution. Skewness. observed distribution asymmetric model predicts symmetric distribution. Consider skew-normal RE distribution. Bimodality. observed distribution two peaks model generates unimodal distribution. mixture RE model may appropriate. Overdispersion. posterior predictive variance consistently smaller observed variance. prior τ\tau may tight, covariate needed.

what: Posterior predictive checks vs. model comparison
title: Posterior predictive checks
text: PPCs model comparison serve different roles: model can pass PPC (generate plausible data) outperformed another model. Conversely, best-performing model LOO-IC may still fail PPC competing models misspecified way. tools used: PPCs model criticism, model comparison model selection.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: Meta-analyses dependent effect sizes routinely involve complex hierarchical data — multiple effect sizes nested within samples, samples nested within studies, moderator variables several levels, auxiliary statistics like standard errors behave differently across effect size metrics. fitting formal model, careful analyst needs understand ’s actually database. PRIMED workflow (PReliminary Investigation MEta-analytic Databases) proposed Pustejovsky, Zhang, Tipton (2026) organises preliminary work four sequential steps: Describe amount data dependence structure Explore study characteristics potential moderators Inspect standard errors auxiliary data Visualise distribution effect size estimates Crucially, effect size distribution examined last, preserves room refine modelling decisions without influenced results . vignette demonstrates primed() function, automates workflow — producing summary tables visualisations step single call.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: Experienced meta-analysts already preliminary checks, process rarely written reported. databases grow larger complex, informal inspection becomes infeasible. structured workflow helps : Verifying database integrity modelling Building understanding - vs within-study variation Informing decisions model structure (e.g., whether three-level models feasible) Identifying sparse categories, outliers, missingness problems early Separating data-exploration decisions outcome-driven choices, reducing opportunities HARKing primed() function opinionated implementation produces reasonable default set outputs. ’s designed starting point analysts can extend customise.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: Dependencies: dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, purrr, tibble, rlang, forcats.
code: source("primed.R")

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: ’ll use dat.assink2016 dataset metafor package, contains 100 effect sizes 17 studies examining effect various psychotherapies recidivism juvenile delinquents. study contributes multiple effect sizes — classic dependent-effects structure. key variables : study — study identifier esid — effect size identifier within study yi — effect size estimate (standardised mean difference) vi — sampling variance (’ll convert SE) deltype — outcome type (potential moderator)
code: library(metafor)  data("dat.assink2016", package = "metafor")  dplyr::glimpse(dat.assink2016) meta_df <- dat.assink2016 |>   dplyr::mutate(     se = sqrt(vi),     df = 100  # approximate — dataset doesn't provide exact df   )

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: single call runs four steps: returned object list three slots: results$summary — high-level counts descriptive stats results$plots — named list ggplot objects results$tables — named list summary tibbles
code: results <- primed(   data        = meta_df,   es_col      = "yi",   se_col      = "se",   study_col   = "study",   sample_col  = NULL,              # one sample per study here   moderators  = c("deltype", "year"),   es_type     = "SMD",   df_col      = "df",   rho_values  = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.7) )  print(results)

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: first step counts observations level visualises effects nested within higher-level units. look : total number studies consistent inclusion criteria? much variation number effects per study? skewed distribution (handful studies contributing 20+ effects contribute 1–2) signals meta-analytic estimates may sensitive modelling assumptions dependency. studies implausibly many effects? ’s often coding error. sample_col supplied (.e., studies contribute multiple participant samples), second plot summarises samples per study — useful deciding whether three-level model (effects within samples within studies) feasible.
code: # Basic structure summary results$tables$step1_structure  # How many effect sizes contribute to each study/sample? results$plots$step1_es_per_sample

Categorical moderators get paired bar charts showing number studies number effects per level: look : Typos inconsistent coding — “Control”, “control”, “CONTROL” appear separate levels Sparse categories — fewer 3–5 studies level means moderator tests underpowered estimates unstable Missingness — “Reported” bar signals often variable unavailable Study vs effect counts — effect-level moderators, effect count exceeding study count indicates within-study variation (can exploit meta-regression) Continuous moderators get density plots rug marks showing individual observations: look : Bimodal distributions (maybe variable really categorical disguise) Outliers influential Narrow range — moderator barely varies can’t explain effect heterogeneity lets see glance moderators enough data include meta-regression sparse useful. categorical moderators, co-occurrence table shows often pairs categories appear together within study: Diagonal cells report total number studies (effects) level. -diagonal cells report co-occurrences — studies include levels. table empty -diagonals tells moderator within-study variation; explanatory power comes -study differences, confounded everything else differs across studies. continuous moderators, decomposition table separates variable -sample within-sample components: “Sample-Mean-Centered” row substantial spread, moderator varies within samples can used estimate within-sample effects. ’s nearly zero, moderator effectively sample-level ’re stuck -sample interpretation.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: now, validated everything else, look effect sizes . Solid vertical lines mark quartiles; dashed lines mark Tukey fences (default, 3 times IQR beyond quartiles — can change fence_multiplier argument). Points outside fences flagged potential outliers. sample-level density (averaged across effects within sample) typically tighter effect-level one. gap within-sample heterogeneity. look : outliers flags investigation, automatic removal. Common causes coding errors (decimal misplacement, wrong sign), unit mismatches (correlation reported SMD expected), genuinely influential studies important report . Unlike conventional forest plot, effects grouped single row per sample rather one-per-row. Samples sorted average effect. view lets see: Marginal outliers — points far overall distribution Within-sample outliers — points disagree sample’s estimates Heterogeneity sources — whether spread mostly samples within samples answer last question informs random-effects structure. Heavy -sample spread + tight within-sample spread → two-level model sample-level random effects probably fine. Heavy spread levels → want three-level model robust variance estimation. Asymmetry funnel plot preliminary diagnostic small-study effects selective reporting. conclusive — paper notes apparent asymmetry can also arise systematic differences older/smaller newer/larger studies (e.g., differences outcome measurement standards). Treat starting point formal bias assessments, final word.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: reasonable starting point methods sections: Prior fitting meta-analytic models, conducted preliminary data analysis following PRIMED workflow (Pustejovsky et al., 2026). examined distribution effect sizes across studies samples (Step 1), marginal hierarchical structure candidate moderators (Step 2), distribution standard errors inverse sampling covariance weights assumed within-sample correlations ρ=0.3,0.5,0.7\rho = 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 (Step 3), distribution effect size estimates effect sample levels (Step 4). Full workflow output available supplementary materials. describe decisions informed workflow (category collapsing, moderator exclusion, outlier investigation) separately pre-registered analysis plan.

title: PRIMED: Preliminary Investigation of Meta-analytic Databases
text: primed() function bundles standard set preliminary analyses meta-analyses dependent effect sizes single call, producing tables plots recommended Pustejovsky, Zhang, Tipton (2026). intended workflow : Run primed() cleaned database Work steps 1–3, making documenting coding/moderator decisions examine step 4 output Fit formal meta-analytic model Archive PRIMED output alongside analysis code Sharing outputs makes pre-modelling phase meta-analysis visible reproducible — something field historically -documented.
