what: What is a prior predictive check?
title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: prior predictive check simulates data model using prior distributions — observed data incorporated. likelihood bypassed entirely, draws reflect model considers plausible priori, nothing priors assumed data-generating process. resulting distribution, p(yrep)p(y_\text{rep}), answers question: knew nothing beyond prior beliefs model structure, data expect see?

what: What prior predictive checks are for
title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: Prior predictive checks two legitimate uses. Sanity-checking observable scale. Priors typically specified transformed scale (log-, log-RR, Cohen’s dd). easy choose prior looks reasonable units implies absurd data — e.g. normal(0, 10) prior log-implies studies routinely report ORs thousands. prior predictive translates prior back scale actual data, making problems immediately visible. Encoding external knowledge. domain expertise independent reference data inform prior — example, empirical estimates -study heterogeneity previous systematic review — prior predictive lets verify prior consistent knowledge analysis begins.

what: What prior predictive checks are not for
title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: tempting adjust priors iteratively yrepy_\text{rep} resembles observed data. circular. smuggles analysis dataset prior, prior likelihood carry information data. resulting posterior overconfident: appears updated data, data already shaped prior. Priors must specified sources genuinely independent analysis dataset: Domain expertise subject-matter knowledge Empirical estimates previous meta-analyses independent cohorts General plausibility constraints (effect sizes infinite; event rates must lie [0,1][0, 1]) prior predictive check used verify chosen prior coherent — select matching observed data.

title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: Pass sample_prior = TRUE bayesma(). model compiled sampled usual way, likelihood contribution excluded model block MCMC explores prior distribution. data argument still required: arm sizes standard errors used keep simulation observable scale real data. plot overlays observed data yy (dark line) draws p(yrep)p(y_\text{rep}) (light lines). observed data shown reference — orient scale data — calibration target.
code: prior_fit <- bayesma(   data       = my_data,   studyvar   = "study",   event_ctrl = "events_c",   event_int  = "events_i",   n_ctrl     = "n_c",   n_int      = "n_i",   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   mu_prior   = normal(0, 1),   tau_prior  = half_cauchy(0, 0.5),   sample_prior = TRUE )  pp_check(prior_fit)

title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: Evaluate prior predictive domain knowledge, observed data. Effect scale. log-scale, values |μ|>2|\mu| > 2 correspond ORs ~7 ~0.14. prior predictive assigns substantial mass effects magnitude, ask whether defensible clinical context. pharmacological interventions fall range |log-|<1.5|\text{log-}| < 1.5; prior placing 30% mass outside range vague. Heterogeneity scale. τ>1\tau > 1 log-scale implies studies differ 2 log-ORs — unusual literatures. Check whether prior predictive τ\tau consistent degree -study variation expect independent knowledge intervention area. Study-level effects. prior predictive individual study effects θi\theta_i cover plausible range. heavy tails (studies implying ORs > 100) suggest prior diffuse.

title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: Empirically-derived priors τ\tau available Turner et al. (2015) pharmacological non-pharmacological interventions binary outcomes, Rhodes et al. (2015) psychological interventions continuous outcomes. calibrated large collections meta-analyses provide principled starting point independent single analysis: Using externally-calibrated priors removes temptation tune analysis data grounds prior broader evidence base intervention class.
code: # Turner et al. (2015) median tau for pharmacological treatments (binary) bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   mu_prior   = normal(0, 1),   tau_prior  = half_normal(0, 0.32) )

title: Prior Predictive Checks
text: model fitted data, ecdf_prior_plot() overlays prior predictive ECDF posterior ECDF. diagnostic prior-data conflict: posterior sits far tail prior predictive, priors inconsistent data showed. Moderate conflict expected healthy — means data updating prior. Severe conflict may indicate prior tight, model misspecification, anomalous data.
code: # Fit with the prior predictive prior_fit    <- bayesma(data, ..., sample_prior = TRUE)  # Fit the full model posterior_fit <- bayesma(data, ...)  # Compare ecdf_prior_plot(posterior_fit)

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: standard random-effects model places Gaussian distribution study-level true effects: θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2) assumption convenient always appropriate. distribution true effects heavy-tailed, asymmetric, bimodal — example, minority studies target fundamentally different populations use qualitatively different implementations — Gaussian assumption can distort pooled estimate understate predictive uncertainty. bayesma supports three alternatives: Student-tt, skew-normal, two-component mixture.

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2) Gaussian natural starting point. implies symmetric heterogeneity exponentially light tails: large deviations μ\mu treated extremely unlikely. Use default; switch alternative substantive data-driven reason.

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text:
θi∼tν(μ,τ2) 	heta_i \sim t_
u(\mu, 	au^2) Student-tt distribution adds degrees--freedom parameter ν
u controls tail heaviness. ν→∞
u \infty converges Gaussian; ν=3
u = 3 tails substantially heavier. Heavy tails mean large study-level deviations μ\mu plausible, reducing influence outlier studies pooled estimate. prior ν
u must specified. bayesma uses ν∼Gamma(2,0.1) 
u \sim 	ext{Gamma}(2, 0.1) default, assigns mass ν∈(3,30)
u \(3, 30) allowing heavy near-Gaussian tails. can overridden via nu_prior. Student-tt random-effects model appropriate : small number studies effects far bulk source deviations unknown possibly real (artefact) analyst wants downweight outliers without excluding

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: θi∼SN(μ,τ,α) \theta_i \sim \text{SN}(\mu, \tau, \alpha) skew-normal distribution generalises Gaussian shape parameter α\alpha controls direction degree asymmetry. α=0\alpha = 0 reduces 𝒩(μ,τ2)\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2). Positive α\alpha yields right-skewed distribution effects; negative α\alpha left-skewed distribution. skew-normal appropriate : directional floor ceiling effect (e.g., effects negative) literature mixture small large positive effects negative effects theoretical reasons exist expect asymmetric heterogeneity prior shape parameter required. bayesma uses α∼𝒩(0,1)\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) default.

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: θi∼π⋅𝒩(μ1,τ12)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(μ2,τ22) \theta_i \sim \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \tau_1^2) + (1 - \pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \tau_2^2) mixture model allows distribution true effects bimodal. mixing weight π\pi estimates proportion studies belonging component. flexible option hardest fit reliably. mixture appropriate : substantive theory predicts two distinct populations studies (e.g., short vs long follow-; low vs high dose) forest plot shows clear gap bimodal distribution analyst suspects qualitative moderator measured Caution: two-component mixture requires adequate kk (typically k≥20k \geq 20) informative priors π\pi component parameters. small kk, components poorly identified posterior strongly prior-dependent. prior sensitivity analysis essential. Priors: π∼Beta(2,2),μj∼𝒩(0,1),τj∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(2, 2), \qquad \mu_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \tau_j \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5)

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: See Assessment RE Distributions model comparison workflow. heuristic:

title: Alternative random-effects distributions
text: choice RE distribution affects pooled estimate also predictive distribution new study. Heavier-tailed distributions produce wider prediction intervals. primary reason prefer heavier tails outliers present: Gaussian understates variable new study’s result might .

title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: Choosing random-effects (RE) distributions — Gaussian, Student-tt, skew-normal, mixture — done inspection data alone. Visual assessment forest plot can suggest departures normality, conflates sampling variability true heterogeneity unreliable kk small. bayesma provides two complementary strategies: formal predictive model comparison via compare_models(), graphical posterior predictive assessment via ECDF plot.

title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: preferred criterion comparing RE distributions leave-one-study-cross-validation (LOSO-CV). asks: well model predict withheld study’s effect, averaged studies? compare_table() reports LOSO continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) model, lower better. compare_plot() shows credible interval coverage nominal level. LOSO-CV preferable WAIC LOO-IC RE distribution assessment : defined effect-size scale across one-stage two-stage models directly measures predictive accuracy new studies, RE distribution governs robust influential-observation problems cause LOO-IC fail meta-analysis
code: fit_normal  <- bayesma(data, re_dist = "normal") fit_t       <- bayesma(data, re_dist = "t") fit_skew    <- bayesma(data, re_dist = "skew_normal")  comparison <- compare_models(   normal    = fit_normal,   t         = fit_t,   skew      = fit_skew )  compare_table(comparison) compare_plot(comparison)

title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) study-level effects, overlaid posterior predictive CDF model, shows whether assumed RE distribution can generate data look like observed effect distribution. ECDF plot replace predictive model comparison — may look reasonable models even LOSO-CRPS clearly favours one. Use diagnostic understand models differ, make primary selection.
code: ecdf_model_plot(fit_normal, fit_t, fit_skew)

title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: LOSO-CRPS differences less 0.1 log-scale (equivalent scales) typically negligible. Prefer simpler Gaussian model unless meaningful improvement CRPS compelling substantive reason use heavier-tailed distribution. Coverage calibration useful secondary diagnostic. well-calibrated model produce 50% prediction intervals contain 50% withheld study effects, 80% intervals contain 80%, . model systematically misses levels may misspecified distributional form.

title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: k<10k < 10, LOSO-CV high variance model comparison unreliable. situation: Default Gaussian. Use prior sensitivity analysis τ\tau rather distributional sensitivity analysis. Report RE distribution uncertainty formally assessed.

what: Prior sensitivity to the distribution choice
title: Assessment of random-effects distributions
text: RE distribution interacts prior τ\tau. heavier-tailed distribution τ\tau prior produce wider prediction intervals. attributing CRPS improvement distributional form, verify persists across range τ\tau priors.

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: bayesma_report() renders parameterised Quarto template publication-style statistical report. template structure follows workflow guide (data prep → fitting → comparison → bias → sensitivity → interpretation) prose style full systematic review (Introduction → Methods → Results → Overall Interpretation). supply data, measure-specific column names, handful narrative strings; function fills template, fits requested models, writes .qmd (optionally rendered .html) disk. split : Inputs — data, columns, priors, null range. Always required. Narrative slots — Background, Hypothesis, Inclusion/Exclusion, etc. Optional; sensible defaults. Section toggles — turn whole analyses (RoBMA, meta-regression, INSPECT-SR, …) . rendered, output normal .qmd can edit hand.

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: call returns path generated .qmd. Alongside , companion *_bundle.rds written containing data, rob_data, priors — template reads render time. render = TRUE, .qmd rendered HTML next .
code: library(bayesma)  bayesma_report(   data         = eeg_data,   studyvar     = "Author",   yearvar      = "Year",   estimand     = "OR",   event_ctrl   = "c_event",   event_int    = "i_event",   n_ctrl       = "c_n",   n_int        = "i_n",   outcome_name = "Postoperative Delirium",   null_range   = c(0.9, 1.1),   title        = "EEG-guided anaesthesia and POD",   author       = "Dr Benjamin Moran",   output_file  = "eeg_pod_report.qmd",   render       = TRUE )

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: Estimand-specific columns — pass whichever set matches data shape implied estimand. Names match bayesma() :
code: # Binary / count estimands (OR, RR, HR, IRR, RD, ARR, ATE, ATT, CATE) event_ctrl = "c_event", event_int = "i_event", n_ctrl     = "c_n",     n_int     = "i_n"  # Continuous estimands (MD, SMD) mean_ctrl = "c_mean", mean_int = "i_mean", sd_ctrl   = "c_sd",   sd_int   = "i_sd", n_ctrl    = "c_n",    n_int    = "i_n"

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: character strings (character vectors) fill prose blocks. optional; defaults generic. Example:
code: bayesma_report(   ...,   background = c(     "Postoperative delirium (POD) is a common complication ...",     "Processed EEG monitors have been proposed to ..."   ),   hypothesis = "pEEG-guided anaesthesia reduces the incidence of POD compared to usual care.",   inclusion_criteria = c(     "Randomised controlled trials (parallel-group or crossover)",     "Adults undergoing general anaesthesia for any surgery",     "EEG-guided anaesthesia versus standard care"   ),   exclusion_criteria = c(     "Non-RCT or no full text",     "Sedation other than general anaesthesia",     "Non-EEG depth-of-anaesthesia monitors"   ),   prospero_id = "CRD420251081478" )

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: toggle defaults sensible value (TRUE primary analyses, FALSE heavier optional ones). Setting toggle FALSE removes Methods description Results section analysis — orphan headings. Toggles take companion arguments: subgroup_var accepts character vector — one heading generated per variable, sub-headings per level.
code: bayesma_report(   ...,   run_metareg  = TRUE,  moderators   = c("MeanAge", "PercentFemale"),   run_subgroup = TRUE,  subgroup_var = c("SurgeryType", "Region"),   run_rob      = TRUE,  rob_data     = my_rob_df )

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: Regardless toggles, primary analysis section fits candidate suite: One-stage common-effect (one_stage_ce) Two-stage common-effect (two_stage_ce) One-stage random-effects (one_stage_re) Two-stage random-effects (two_stage_re) Two-stage random-effects HKSJ (two_stage_re_hksj) Two-stage random-effects mixture RE distribution (two_stage_re_mixture) primary model selected via LOSO-CV (compare_models()), diagnostics shown alongside. selected model drives forest plot, posterior, downstream sensitivity / RoBMA work. override automatic selection: run_robma = TRUE, additional set single-component bias-adjusted models fitted alongside RoBMA : bias_corrected — parametric bias-correction (Verde 2021) bc_bnp — Bias-Corrected Bayesian Non-Parametric (Verde 2025) selection_copas — Robust Bayesian Copas selection model (Bai et al. 2020) selection_weight — Vevea-Hedges weight-function selection model pet_peese — small-study adjustment via funnel-plot regression mixture_model — Bayesian meta-analytic mixture (Maier 2024)
code: bayesma_report(..., primary_model = "two_stage_re_hksj")

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: default report fits bayesma’s weakly-regularising priors reports prior sensitivity panel covering vague, weak_reg, informative specifications. pass custom priors: Priors stashed companion *_bundle.rds read template — need inline YAML.
code: my_priors <- list(   weak_reg = list(     mu_prior  = normal(0, 1),     tau_prior = half_student_t(3, 0, 0.5)   ),   informative = list(     mu_prior  = normal(-0.3, 0.3),     tau_prior = half_normal(0, 0.3)   ) )  bayesma_report(..., priors = my_priors)

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: estimand one "RD"/"ARR", "ATE", "ATT", "CATE", fitted model also carries $marginal element produced bayesma_marginal(): RD / ARR ATE one-stage binomial fits computed posterior draws per-arm linear predictor. RD / ARR ATE two-stage binomial fits require reference baseline. Pass baseline_risk = 0.15 (numeric) baseline_risk = "study_mean" (unweighted mean observed control rates). ATT computed ATE weighted treated-arm sample size; without IPD arm-size-weighted ATE rather true causal ATT. CATE routes meta-regression cate_covariate. Set cate_covariate = "MeanAge" (similar). user evaluates posterior covariate value interest downstream. relative-effect estimands (/RR/HR/IRR/MD/SMD) behave exactly .

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: Pass pre-scored RoB data frame via rob_data run_rob = TRUE: rob_data NULL data contains rob_* columns, used instead. Otherwise RoB section skipped message.
code: bayesma_report(..., run_rob = TRUE, rob_data = rob_2_scored)

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: Reproducing EEG-guided anaesthesia report:
code: library(bayesma) library(readxl)  eeg <- read_excel("EEG_POD_data.xlsx", sheet = "delirium")  bayesma_report(   data         = eeg,   studyvar     = "Author",   yearvar      = "Year",   estimand     = "OR",   event_ctrl   = "c_event", event_int = "i_event",   n_ctrl       = "c_n",     n_int     = "i_n",   outcome_name = "Postoperative Delirium",   null_range   = c(0.9, 1.1),    title       = "EEG-guided anaesthesia for reducing POD",   subtitle    = "A Bayesian meta-analysis",   author      = "Dr Benjamin Moran",   prospero_id = "CRD420251081478",    background = c(     "Postoperative delirium is a common complication of surgery in older adults ...",     "Processed EEG monitors have been proposed to guide anaesthetic depth ..."   ),   hypothesis = "pEEG-guided anaesthesia reduces the incidence of POD compared to usual care.",   inclusion_criteria = c(     "Randomised controlled trials",     "Adults undergoing general anaesthesia",     "pEEG-guided versus standard care"   ),   exclusion_criteria = c(     "Non-RCT or no full text",     "Sedation other than general anaesthesia",     "Non-EEG depth monitors"   ),    run_inspect_sr         = FALSE,   run_rob                = TRUE,   run_egger              = TRUE,   run_robma              = TRUE,   run_subgroup           = TRUE,   subgroup_var           = c("SurgeryType", "Region"),   run_sensitivity_priors = TRUE,    output_file = "eeg_pod_report.qmd",   render      = TRUE )

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: generated .qmd regular Quarto document. Common post-render edits: Tighten prose slots (Background, Limitations, Conclusion) — parts templated report get exactly right. Reorder Results sections, drop model didn’t converge, add domain-specific commentary. Swap format: block pdf docx submitting. Re-rendering edits just quarto render eeg_pod_report.qmd — bayesma_report() need called . (companion *_bundle.rds must remain alongside .qmd.)

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: write Background / Discussion / Limitations . slots accept prose verbatim; function generate clinical narrative. assess study quality. Pass pre-scored RoB data frame via rob_data = run_rob = TRUE. search literature, screen studies, extract data — starts clean tibble.

title: Generating a Bayesian meta-analysis report
text: workflow-guide — full walk-underlying functions. robma — RoBMA-specific details. primed — preliminary data exploration.

title: Reporting checklist
text: Transparent reporting Bayesian meta-analyses enables readers evaluate appropriateness modelling choices, assess robustness, reproduce results. checklist covers minimum reporting requirements bayesma analysis intended publication. checklist structured follow order items typically appear methods results section.

title: Reporting checklist
text: Eligibility criteria studies, populations, interventions, comparators, outcomes. Databases searched, date range, search strategy. Number studies identified, screened, assessed eligibility, included (PRISMA flow diagram). Pre-registration identifier (PROSPERO equivalent). Effect measure used (, RR, HR, SMD, MD, etc.) justification. two-stage analysis: software method used compute (yi,si)(y_i, s_i). one-stage analysis: raw cell counts means SDs used directly. Treatment multi-arm studies (applicable). Model type: common-effect random-effects; one-stage two-stage. Random-effects distribution: Gaussian, Student-tt, skew-normal, mixture. Prior distributions parameters, justification. Source informative priors (e.g., Turner et al. 2015 τ\tau). Software: bayesma, version, Stan backend (cmdstanr version). Number chains, warmup iterations, sampling iterations. adapt_delta value. Random seed (reproducibility). Maximum R̂\hat{R} across parameters (target: <1.01< 1.01). Minimum bulk tail ESS (target: >400> 400). Number divergent transitions (target: 0). parameters required intervention resolution. Posterior median 95% credible interval pooled effect μ\mu. Posterior median 95% credible interval -study heterogeneity τ\tau. Prediction interval effect new study. Back-transformation natural scale (, RR, etc.) applicable. Method used (Egger, PET-PEESE, selection model, BC-BNP, mixture, none). Justification method choice. Bias-adjusted pooled estimate comparison unadjusted estimate. Bayes factor posterior probability presence bias (applicable). Prior sensitivity: range τ\tau priors tested whether conclusions changed. Study-level influence: studies substantial influence pooled estimate. Model comparison: criteria used whether different model preferred. Bias assumption sensitivity (selection models: sensitivity selection mechanism). Estimated τ\tau uncertainty. I2I^2 (reported) accompanying τ\tau 95% CrI rather standalone measure. Prediction interval new study (informative I2I^2 alone). Moderators tested, justification. Posterior moderator coefficient Bayes factor null. tested moderators reported, significant results. R citation: citation(). bayesma citation: citation("bayesma"). cmdstanr Stan citations. additional packages used visualisation reporting.

title: Reporting checklist
text: Eligibility criteria studies, populations, interventions, comparators, outcomes. Databases searched, date range, search strategy. Number studies identified, screened, assessed eligibility, included (PRISMA flow diagram). Pre-registration identifier (PROSPERO equivalent).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Effect measure used (, RR, HR, SMD, MD, etc.) justification. two-stage analysis: software method used compute (yi,si)(y_i, s_i). one-stage analysis: raw cell counts means SDs used directly. Treatment multi-arm studies (applicable).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Model type: common-effect random-effects; one-stage two-stage. Random-effects distribution: Gaussian, Student-tt, skew-normal, mixture. Prior distributions parameters, justification. Source informative priors (e.g., Turner et al. 2015 τ\tau). Software: bayesma, version, Stan backend (cmdstanr version).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Number chains, warmup iterations, sampling iterations. adapt_delta value. Random seed (reproducibility).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Maximum R̂\hat{R} across parameters (target: <1.01< 1.01). Minimum bulk tail ESS (target: >400> 400). Number divergent transitions (target: 0). parameters required intervention resolution.

title: Reporting checklist
text: Posterior median 95% credible interval pooled effect μ\mu. Posterior median 95% credible interval -study heterogeneity τ\tau. Prediction interval effect new study. Back-transformation natural scale (, RR, etc.) applicable.

title: Reporting checklist
text: Method used (Egger, PET-PEESE, selection model, BC-BNP, mixture, none). Justification method choice. Bias-adjusted pooled estimate comparison unadjusted estimate. Bayes factor posterior probability presence bias (applicable).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Prior sensitivity: range τ\tau priors tested whether conclusions changed. Study-level influence: studies substantial influence pooled estimate. Model comparison: criteria used whether different model preferred. Bias assumption sensitivity (selection models: sensitivity selection mechanism).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Estimated τ\tau uncertainty. I2I^2 (reported) accompanying τ\tau 95% CrI rather standalone measure. Prediction interval new study (informative I2I^2 alone).

title: Reporting checklist
text: Moderators tested, justification. Posterior moderator coefficient Bayes factor null. tested moderators reported, significant results.

title: Reporting checklist
text: R citation: citation(). bayesma citation: citation("bayesma"). cmdstanr Stan citations. additional packages used visualisation reporting.

title: Reporting checklist
text: bayesma can produce fully formatted publication report key results, plots, tables via bayesma_report(). See report-generation.qmd full workflow.
code: bayesma_report(   fit,   output_file = "meta_analysis_report.qmd",   title       = "Bayesian meta-analysis of ...",   author      = "Author Name" )

title: Reporting checklist
text: GRADE framework (Grading Recommendations Assessment, Development Evaluation) assesses overall certainty evidence. Key domains affected modelling choices: Inconsistency: assessed via τ\tau prediction interval. Publication bias: assessed via selection models Egger’s test. Imprecision: assessed via width 95% credible interval. Certainty ratings applied bias assessment sensitivity analysis, .

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: Risk bias (RoB) assessment evaluates internal validity individual studies. meta-analysis pools high- low-bias studies without adjustment may produce estimates reflect truth target population. bayesma integrates RoB information two ways: visualisation tool moderator bias-adjusted models.

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: bayesma accepts RoB ratings widely used tools: Ratings can entered domain-level strings ("low", "concerns", "high") aggregate scores.

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: rob_plot() produces traffic-light display domain-level RoB ratings, one row per study. summary bar chart showing proportion studies risk level across domains can added summary = TRUE.
code: rob_plot(   data,   domains = c("randomisation", "deviations", "missing_data",               "measurement", "selection"),   tool    = "rob2" )

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: simplest approach treats aggregate RoB study-level moderator meta-regression: negative β̂\hat{\beta} rob_score (coded higher values = higher risk) indicate high-risk studies report larger effects, consistent bias inflating estimates.
code: fit_rob <- meta_reg(   data,   formula    = ~ rob_score,   model_type = "random_effect" )  coefficient_evidence(fit_rob)

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: targeted sensitivity analysis restricts meta-analysis low-risk studies : low-risk estimate substantially smaller full estimate, high-risk studies may inflating pooled effect.
code: low_risk <- dplyr::filter_out(data, rob_overall != "low")  fit_full    <- bayesma(data,      model_type = "random_effect") fit_lowrisk <- bayesma(low_risk,  model_type = "random_effect")  compare_models(full = fit_full, low_risk = fit_lowrisk)

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: model-based adjustment discard high-risk studies, see: Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe 2026) Bias-adjusted models (Verde 2021) models embed RoB domain ratings study-level indicators potential bias, allowing model partially adjust RoB retaining studies.

title: Risk of bias assessment
text: RoB ratings subjective may unreliable across raters. relationship RoB ratings effect size inflation fixed varies across domains interventions. Restricting low-risk studies substantially reduces kk increases uncertainty; sensitivity analysis may low power.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: single publication-bias correction method embeds strong assumptions nature selection mechanism. PET-PEESE assumes selection correlated standard error. Weight-function models assume selection operates p-values. Choosing requires subjective judgment, reporting best-fitting model ignores model uncertainty. Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA; Maier, Bartoš & Wagenmakers, 2023) addresses averaging family meta-analytic models rather committing one. RoBMA produces single model-averaged posterior true effect reflects uncertainty heterogeneity structure publication-bias mechanism. bayesma implements RoBMA via robma().

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: RoBMA fits grid models formed crossing: Heterogeneity: common-effect vs. random-effects Publication bias: none vs. PET-PEESE vs. selection weight function yields default grid 2×3=62 \times 3 = 6 component models. model MkM_k fitted separately. Custom model grids can specified via bias_models heterogeneity arguments.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: component model assigned prior probability p(Mk)p(M_k) (equal default: 1/61/6). fitting, Bayes factors used compute posterior model probabilities: p(Mk∣𝐲)∝p(𝐲∣Mk)⋅p(Mk) p(M_k \mid \mathbf{y}) \propto p(\mathbf{y} \mid M_k) \cdot p(M_k) p(𝐲∣Mk)p(\mathbf{y} \mid M_k) marginal likelihood model kk, estimated via bridge sampling. model-averaged posterior quantity ψ\psi (e.g., μ\mu) : p(ψ∣𝐲)=∑k=1Kp(Mk∣𝐲)⋅p(ψ∣𝐲,Mk) p(\psi \mid \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(M_k \mid \mathbf{y}) \cdot p(\psi \mid \mathbf{y}, M_k)

what: Bayes factors for heterogeneity and bias
title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: RoBMA computes two summary Bayes factors collapsing model grid: Bayes factor heterogeneity (random-effects vs. common-effect): BFτ=P(τ>0∣𝐲)P(τ=0∣𝐲) \text{BF}_\tau = \frac{P(\tau > 0 \mid \mathbf{y})}{P(\tau = 0 \mid \mathbf{y})} Bayes factor publication bias: BFb=P(bias∣𝐲)P(bias∣𝐲) \text{BF}_b = \frac{P(\text{bias} \mid \mathbf{y})}{P(\text{bias} \mid \mathbf{y})} primary inferential summaries: BFτ\text{BF}_\tau quantifies evidence heterogeneity exists; BFb\text{BF}_b quantifies evidence publication bias present.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: Component model priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) PET-PEESE coefficient: βPET∼𝒩(0,1) \beta_\text{PET} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1) Weight-function selection weights: ωj∼Dirichlet(𝟏) \omega_j \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\mathbf{1}) Priors can modified via robma_default_priors() passed robma().

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: robma_output() prints model-averaged summary. robma_table() returns gt table posterior model probabilities, Bayes factors heterogeneity bias, model-averaged posterior μ\mu.
code: #| eval: false fit_robma <- robma(data)  robma_output(fit_robma) robma_table(fit_robma)

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: model-averaged posterior μ\mu integrates component models, weighted posterior model probabilities. primary bias-corrected estimate. robma_table() reports posterior model probability component model MkM_k. Large posterior probability models without bias component (M1M_1, M2M_2) indicates little evidence publication bias.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: model-averaged posterior μ\mu integrates component models, weighted posterior model probabilities. primary bias-corrected estimate.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: robma_table() reports posterior model probability component model MkM_k. Large posterior probability models without bias component (M1M_1, M2M_2) indicates little evidence publication bias.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: Bridge sampling estimates Monte Carlo error. robma_sensitivity() reruns bridge sampler different random seeds reports variance log-marginal-likelihood estimates: Posterior model probabilities change substantially across seeds indicate MCMC chains need samples bridge sampling reliable.
code: #| eval: false fit_robma <- robma(data, iter_sampling = 5000)  # more samples → more stable BF robma_sensitivity(fit_robma)

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: null_range argument specifies region practical equivalence around zero. Model probabilities computed models predict effect vs. outside range.
code: #| eval: false fit_custom <- robma(   data,   bias_models     = c("none", "selection_weight"),  # exclude PET-PEESE   heterogeneity   = c("random"),                    # random-effects only   priors_effect   = normal(0, 0.5),   null_range      = c(-0.1, 0.1) )

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: Bridge sampling requires well-mixed MCMC chains. Use iter_sampling ≥ 4000 check convergence diagnostics interpreting Bayes factors. RoBMA computationally intensive — fitting six component models bridge sampling takes several minutes per dataset. Use robma(data, parallel = TRUE) fit component models parallel. default model grid assumes bias direction positive (published effects inflated upward). outcomes bias deflates effects, specify appropriate priors weight functions.

title: Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
text: Maier M, Bartoš F, Wagenmakers EJ (2023). Robust Bayesian meta-analysis: Addressing publication bias model-averaging. Psychological Methods, 28(1), 107–122. Bartoš F, Maier M, Wagenmakers EJ, Doucouliagos H, Stanley TD (2023). Robust Bayesian meta-analysis: Model-averaging across formulations publication bias. Psychological Methods.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: standard Gaussian random-effects model assumes true study effects follow single normal distribution. assumption breaks effect distribution bimodal — example, two qualitatively distinct subpopulations studies exist (e.g., patient subgroups, different operationalisations intervention) — small number studies produce effects discrepant distort pooled estimate. bayesma provides two random-effects mixture models handle situations: Two-component Gaussian mixture: models random-effects distribution mixture two normal components different means variances. Robust outlier mixture: identifies individual outlier studies without requiring belong separate substantive subpopulation. See Robust Outlier Mixture (Cruz). vignette covers two-component mixture. outlier variant see Robust Outlier Mixture (Cruz).

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: true-effect distribution mixture two Gaussians: p(θi)=π⋅𝒩(θi∣μ1,τ12)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(θi∣μ2,τ22) p(\theta_i) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_1,\; \tau_1^2) + (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_2,\; \tau_2^2) Marginalising θi\theta_i gives integrated observation-level likelihood: p(yi∣μ1,μ2,τ1,τ2,π)=π⋅𝒩(yi∣μ1,τ12+si2)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(yi∣μ2,τ22+si2) p(y_i \mid \mu_1, \mu_2, \tau_1, \tau_2, \pi) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_1,\; \tau_1^2 + s_i^2) + (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_2,\; \tau_2^2 + s_i^2) two components means μ1<μ2\mu_1 < \mu_2 (ordering constraint resolves label switching) heterogeneities τ1\tau_1, τ2\tau_2. π∼Beta(2,2) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(2,\; 2) μ1,μ2∼𝒩(0,1)constraint μ1≤μ2 \mu_1, \mu_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1) \quad \text{constraint } \mu_1 \leq \mu_2 τ1,τ2∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \tau_1, \tau_2 \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) Beta(2, 2) prior mixing proportion favours balanced mixtures slightly degenerate ones (mass one component), still allowing unequal mixtures.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: true-effect distribution mixture two Gaussians: p(θi)=π⋅𝒩(θi∣μ1,τ12)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(θi∣μ2,τ22) p(\theta_i) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_1,\; \tau_1^2) + (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_2,\; \tau_2^2) Marginalising θi\theta_i gives integrated observation-level likelihood: p(yi∣μ1,μ2,τ1,τ2,π)=π⋅𝒩(yi∣μ1,τ12+si2)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(yi∣μ2,τ22+si2) p(y_i \mid \mu_1, \mu_2, \tau_1, \tau_2, \pi) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_1,\; \tau_1^2 + s_i^2) + (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_2,\; \tau_2^2 + s_i^2) two components means μ1<μ2\mu_1 < \mu_2 (ordering constraint resolves label switching) heterogeneities τ1\tau_1, τ2\tau_2.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: π∼Beta(2,2) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(2,\; 2) μ1,μ2∼𝒩(0,1)constraint μ1≤μ2 \mu_1, \mu_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1) \quad \text{constraint } \mu_1 \leq \mu_2 τ1,τ2∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \tau_1, \tau_2 \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) Beta(2, 2) prior mixing proportion favours balanced mixtures slightly degenerate ones (mass one component), still allowing unequal mixtures.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: summary() reports posteriors μ1\mu_1, μ2\mu_2, τ1\tau_1, τ2\tau_2, π\pi.
code: #| eval: false fit_mix <- bayesma(data, model = "re_mixture")  summary(fit_mix)

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: two means characterise two subpopulations studies. posteriors μ1\mu_1 μ2\mu_2 substantially overlap, data support bimodal structure. π\pi estimated fraction studies first (lower-effect) component. posterior π\pi concentrated near 0 1 indicates near-degenerate mixing — data consistent single Gaussian. Intermediate values (e.g., 0.2–0.8) suggest genuine bimodality. model-averaged pooled effect μ‾=πμ1+(1−π)μ2 \bar{\mu} = \pi \mu_1 + (1-\pi)\mu_2 reported b_Intercept output serves overall summary reporting single number required.
