text: two means characterise two subpopulations studies. posteriors μ1\mu_1 μ2\mu_2 substantially overlap, data support bimodal structure.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: π\pi estimated fraction studies first (lower-effect) component. posterior π\pi concentrated near 0 1 indicates near-degenerate mixing — data consistent single Gaussian. Intermediate values (e.g., 0.2–0.8) suggest genuine bimodality.

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: model-averaged pooled effect μ‾=πμ1+(1−π)μ2 \bar{\mu} = \pi \mu_1 + (1-\pi)\mu_2 reported b_Intercept output serves overall summary reporting single number required.

what: Assessing whether the mixture is supported
title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: Compare mixture model standard Gaussian RE model using WAIC LOO: substantially lower LOO mixture model indicates two-component structure fits meaningfully better. improvement small, standard RE model adequate parsimonious.
code: #| eval: false fit_re  <- bayesma(data) fit_mix <- bayesma(data, model = "re_mixture")  compare_models(fit_re, fit_mix, labels = c("Gaussian RE", "RE mixture"),                criterion = "loo")

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: μ1\mu_1 μ2\mu_2 well-separated (credible intervals non-overlapping), investigate moderators distinguish two study clusters. mixture model can motivate meta-regression suspected moderator: See Meta-Regression.
code: #| eval: false fit_reg <- meta_reg(data, moderators = ~ patient_type)

title: RE mixture models for heterogeneity
text: Label switching: despite μ1≤μ2\mu_1 \leq \mu_2 constraint, MCMC chains can occasionally swap component labels extreme values π\pi. Check trace plots components. Identifiability degrades small kk: mixture adds three extra parameters (μ2\mu_2, τ2\tau_2, π\pi) requires enough studies components adequately informed. two-component model capture complex multimodal structures. Three-component mixtures available rarely warranted practice. Stan code underlying model, see Stan Code — RE Mixture Model.

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Selection models address publication bias explicitly modelling mechanism studies enter (fail enter) published literature. Rather adjusting effect estimate via regression, selection models embed publication process likelihood, correcting inference μ\mu -weighting observations -represented due selection. bayesma implements two complementary families: Copas selection model — latent-variable model selection depends study precision. Vevea-Hedges weight-function models — step-function model selection depends reported p-value. available bayesma().

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Copas & Shi (2000) model study published latent selection variable exceeds zero: Zi=γ0+γ1SEi+δi,δi∼𝒩(0,1) Z_i = \gamma_0 + \frac{\gamma_1}{SE_i} + \delta_i, \qquad \delta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) Study ii observed Zi>0Z_i > 0. selection parameters (γ0,γ1)(\gamma_0, \gamma_1) control shape selection mechanism: γ0\gamma_0 baseline selection intercept (negative → severe selection overall). γ1\gamma_1 governs much easier large-precision (small-SESE) studies publish. Positive γ1\gamma_1 means precise studies likely published regardless result. selection variable ZiZ_i correlated observed effect yiy_i joint bivariate normal: (yiZi)∼𝒩((θiγ0+γ1/SEi),(si2ρsiρsi1)) \begin{pmatrix} y_i \\ Z_i \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\begin{pmatrix} \theta_i \\ \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 / SE_i \end{pmatrix},\; \begin{pmatrix} s_i^2 & \rho\,s_i \\ \rho\,s_i & 1 \end{pmatrix}\right) correlation ρ\rho captures tendency studies large effects published (effect-dependent selection). null ρ=0\rho = 0, selection depends precision, effect magnitude. μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) γ0,γ1∼𝒩(0,1),ρ∼Uniform(−1,1) \gamma_0, \gamma_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \rho \sim \text{Uniform}(-1, 1) Informative priors can supplied via prior_copas(): Restricting ρ≤0\rho \leq 0 encodes expectation publication bias inflates effects upward.
code: #| eval: false fit_copas <- bayesma(   data,   model        = "copas",   prior_copas  = prior_copas(gamma0 = normal(0, 1), rho = uniform(-1, 0)) )

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Copas & Shi (2000) model study published latent selection variable exceeds zero: Zi=γ0+γ1SEi+δi,δi∼𝒩(0,1) Z_i = \gamma_0 + \frac{\gamma_1}{SE_i} + \delta_i, \qquad \delta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) Study ii observed Zi>0Z_i > 0. selection parameters (γ0,γ1)(\gamma_0, \gamma_1) control shape selection mechanism: γ0\gamma_0 baseline selection intercept (negative → severe selection overall). γ1\gamma_1 governs much easier large-precision (small-SESE) studies publish. Positive γ1\gamma_1 means precise studies likely published regardless result. selection variable ZiZ_i correlated observed effect yiy_i joint bivariate normal: (yiZi)∼𝒩((θiγ0+γ1/SEi),(si2ρsiρsi1)) \begin{pmatrix} y_i \\ Z_i \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\begin{pmatrix} \theta_i \\ \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 / SE_i \end{pmatrix},\; \begin{pmatrix} s_i^2 & \rho\,s_i \\ \rho\,s_i & 1 \end{pmatrix}\right) correlation ρ\rho captures tendency studies large effects published (effect-dependent selection). null ρ=0\rho = 0, selection depends precision, effect magnitude.

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5) γ0,γ1∼𝒩(0,1),ρ∼Uniform(−1,1) \gamma_0, \gamma_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \rho \sim \text{Uniform}(-1, 1) Informative priors can supplied via prior_copas(): Restricting ρ≤0\rho \leq 0 encodes expectation publication bias inflates effects upward.
code: #| eval: false fit_copas <- bayesma(   data,   model        = "copas",   prior_copas  = prior_copas(gamma0 = normal(0, 1), rho = uniform(-1, 0)) )

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Vevea & Hedges (1995) model selection step function two-tailed p-value. Studies retained probability ωj\omega_j p-value falls interval jj: p(study observed∣pi)=ωj,pi∈(aj−1,aj] p(\text{study observed} \mid p_i) = \omega_j, \quad p_i \(a_{j-1},\; a_j] likelihood reweighted -represented p-value regions (e.g., p<.05p < .05) contribute proportionally less pooled estimate. bayesma implements one-sided step function default, cut-points .025,.05,.10,.25,.50,1.0.025,\; .05,\; .10,\; .25,\; .50,\; 1.0: ω1≥ω2≥⋯≥ω6=1 \omega_1 \geq \omega_2 \geq \cdots \geq \omega_6 = 1 final weight ω6=1\omega_6 = 1 fixed reference (non-significant studies), ωj\omega_j gives relative probability study p-value category jj published compared study p>.50p > .50. weight parameters assigned Dirichlet prior ensure proper identification: (ω1,…,ω5)∼Dirichlet(𝟏) (\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_5) \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\mathbf{1}) Custom weights can specified via prior_weight_function(). two-sided version uses absolute p-value appropriate effects can either direction.
code: #| eval: false fit_wf1 <- bayesma(   data,   model       = "weight_function",   weight_type = "one_sided"   # default )  fit_wf2 <- bayesma(   data,   model       = "weight_function",   weight_type = "two_sided" )

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Vevea & Hedges (1995) model selection step function two-tailed p-value. Studies retained probability ωj\omega_j p-value falls interval jj: p(study observed∣pi)=ωj,pi∈(aj−1,aj] p(\text{study observed} \mid p_i) = \omega_j, \quad p_i \(a_{j-1},\; a_j] likelihood reweighted -represented p-value regions (e.g., p<.05p < .05) contribute proportionally less pooled estimate. bayesma implements one-sided step function default, cut-points .025,.05,.10,.25,.50,1.0.025,\; .05,\; .10,\; .25,\; .50,\; 1.0: ω1≥ω2≥⋯≥ω6=1 \omega_1 \geq \omega_2 \geq \cdots \geq \omega_6 = 1 final weight ω6=1\omega_6 = 1 fixed reference (non-significant studies), ωj\omega_j gives relative probability study p-value category jj published compared study p>.50p > .50.

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: weight parameters assigned Dirichlet prior ensure proper identification: (ω1,…,ω5)∼Dirichlet(𝟏) (\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_5) \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\mathbf{1}) Custom weights can specified via prior_weight_function().

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: two-sided version uses absolute p-value appropriate effects can either direction.
code: #| eval: false fit_wf1 <- bayesma(   data,   model       = "weight_function",   weight_type = "one_sided"   # default )  fit_wf2 <- bayesma(   data,   model       = "weight_function",   weight_type = "two_sided" )

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: compare_models() overlays posteriors μ\mu model, making easy see much publication-bias correction shifts estimate whether two selection models agree.
code: #| eval: false fit_re    <- bayesma(data) fit_copas <- bayesma(data, model = "copas") fit_wf    <- bayesma(data, model = "weight_function")  compare_models(fit_re, fit_copas, fit_wf,                labels = c("Random-effects", "Copas", "Weight function"))

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Copas model, summary() returns: weight-function model:

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: models require sufficient studies (k≥20k \geq 20 practical minimum weight-function model; Copas can work fewer sensitive prior ρ\rho). Copas model assumes specific functional form selection mechanism. true mechanism differs, correction can imprecise. Weight-function models assume selection operates exclusively p-values. Selection based effect size direction magnitude (without reference p-values) captured. Neither model identifies publication bias filed studies completely missing (.e., selection mechanism strong unpublished studies share observable characteristics published set). model-averaged approach includes PET-PEESE weight functions bias components, see Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis (RoBMA).

title: Selection models for publication bias
text: Copas J, Shi JQ (2000). Meta-analysis, funnel plots sensitivity analysis. Biostatistics, 1(3), 247–262. Vevea JL, Hedges LV (1995). general linear model estimating effect size presence publication bias. Psychometrika, 60(3), 419–435.

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: meta-analysis conclusion credible robustness. Sensitivity analysis systematically varies modelling choices data subsets determine whether pooled estimate stable dependent specific assumptions. bayesma provides structured sensitivity analysis tools prior sensitivity, bias assumption sensitivity, study-level influence.

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: common sensitivity analysis Bayesian meta-analysis varies prior -study heterogeneity τ\tau. sensitivity_plot() shows posterior μ\mu (median 95% CrI) changes across prior specifications. stable estimate across priors indicates conclusion prior-driven.
code: fits <- list(   narrow  = bayesma(data, tau_prior = half_normal(0, 0.1)),   default = bayesma(data, tau_prior = half_cauchy(0, 0.5)),   wide    = bayesma(data, tau_prior = half_cauchy(0, 1.0)) )  sensitivity_plot(fits, parameter = "mu")

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: publication bias models, sensitivity assumed bias magnitude selection mechanism often important prior sensitivity. Copas model’s selection parameters fully identified. sensitivity_plot() Copas shows pooled estimate across grid (γ0,γ1)(\gamma_0, \gamma_1) values: flat surface indicates estimate robust selection mechanism assumption. steep slope indicates high sensitivity.
code: fits_bias <- list(   uncorrected = bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect"),   pet_peese   = bayesma(data, model_type = "pet_peese"),   selection   = bayesma(data, model_type = "selection_weight") )  sensitivity_plot(fits_bias, parameter = "mu") sensitivity_plot(fit_copas, type = "copas_grid") fits_pbias <- purrr::map(   c(beta(1, 9), beta(1, 4), beta(1, 1)),   \(p) bayesma(data, model_type = "mixture_model", p_bias_prior = p) )  sensitivity_plot(fits_pbias, parameter = "mu", labels = c("skeptical", "default", "agnostic"))

what: PET-PEESE vs selection models vs uncorrected
title: Sensitivity analysis
code: fits_bias <- list(   uncorrected = bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect"),   pet_peese   = bayesma(data, model_type = "pet_peese"),   selection   = bayesma(data, model_type = "selection_weight") )  sensitivity_plot(fits_bias, parameter = "mu")

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: Copas model’s selection parameters fully identified. sensitivity_plot() Copas shows pooled estimate across grid (γ0,γ1)(\gamma_0, \gamma_1) values: flat surface indicates estimate robust selection mechanism assumption. steep slope indicates high sensitivity.
code: sensitivity_plot(fit_copas, type = "copas_grid")

title: Sensitivity analysis
code: fits_pbias <- purrr::map(   c(beta(1, 9), beta(1, 4), beta(1, 1)),   \(p) bayesma(data, model_type = "mixture_model", p_bias_prior = p) )  sensitivity_plot(fits_pbias, parameter = "mu", labels = c("skeptical", "default", "agnostic"))

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: Leave-one-study-(LOSO) sensitivity reruns model excluding study turn, showing pooled estimate changes study removed: Studies large influence (estimate changes substantially removed) flagged characteristics examined.
code: loso_results <- purrr::map(   seq_len(nrow(data)),   \(i) bayesma(data[-i, ], model_type = "random_effect") )  sensitivity_plot(loso_results, type = "loso", study_labels = data$study_label)

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: binary outcomes, sensitivity choice summary measure can assessed fitting log-log-RR models:
code: fit_lor <- bayesma(data, estimand = "OR") fit_lrr <- bayesma(data, estimand = "RR") compare_models(OR = fit_lor, RR = fit_lrr)

title: Sensitivity analysis
text: Report: priors varied range tested. Whether conclusions changed qualitatively (significant non-significant, vice versa). specific study assumption affects conclusion. Sensitivity analyses change direction estimate (positive null null positive) highlighted prominently; indicate conclusion robust.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: Standard two-stage random-effects meta-analysis treats within-study standard errors sis_i known. approximately valid study large, studies small samples sis_i estimated non-trivial uncertainty. Ignoring uncertainty produces credible intervals μ\mu narrow. Two adjustments supported bayesma: Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman (HKSJ) correction tt-approximation reference distribution.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: Hartung (1999), Knapp Hartung (2003), Sidik Jonkman (2006) independently proposed correction variance pooled estimator accounts imprecision τ̂2\hat{\tau}^2. standard pooled estimator μ̂=∑iwiyi∑iwi,wi=1si2+τ̂2 \hat{\mu} = \frac{\sum_i w_i y_i}{\sum_i w_i}, \qquad w_i = \frac{1}{s_i^2 + \hat{\tau}^2} HKSJ correction replaces usual variance estimator Var̂HK(μ̂)=1k(k−1)∑=1kwi(yi−μ̂)2 \widehat{\text{Var}}_\text{HK}(\hat{\mu}) = \frac{1}{k(k-1)} \sum_{=1}^k w_i (y_i - \hat{\mu})^2 Inference uses tk−1t_{k-1} reference distribution rather 𝒩(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0,1). Simulation studies show HKSJ correction substantially improves coverage k<20k < 20, improvement pronounced k<10k < 10. Bayesian framework HKSJ correction implemented replacing Gaussian sampling model scaled tt model: yi∣μ,τ,ϕ∼tk−1(μ+ui,ϕ⋅si) y_i \mid \mu, \tau, \phi \sim t_{k-1}\!\left(\mu + u_i,\, \phi \cdot s_i\right) ϕ>0\phi > 0 multiplicative scale factor estimated data. prior ϕ∼Half-t3(0,1)\phi \sim \text{Half-}t_3(0, 1) placed ϕ\phi. formulation recovers spirit HKSJ correction — acknowledging sis_i may imprecise — within fully Bayesian model.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: Bayesian framework HKSJ correction implemented replacing Gaussian sampling model scaled tt model: yi∣μ,τ,ϕ∼tk−1(μ+ui,ϕ⋅si) y_i \mid \mu, \tau, \phi \sim t_{k-1}\!\left(\mu + u_i,\, \phi \cdot s_i\right) ϕ>0\phi > 0 multiplicative scale factor estimated data. prior ϕ∼Half-t3(0,1)\phi \sim \text{Half-}t_3(0, 1) placed ϕ\phi. formulation recovers spirit HKSJ correction — acknowledging sis_i may imprecise — within fully Bayesian model.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: simpler adjustment replaces Gaussian reference distribution μ̂\hat{\mu} tk−1t_{k-1} distribution, without modifying likelihood. approach recommended Hedges Vevea (1998) frequentist meta-analysis sometimes referred DerSimonian–Kacker–Hartung approach. bayesma tt-approximation implemented placing tk−1t_{k-1} prior μ\mu, effectively widening prior tails match tt reference distribution imply. conservative choice: adds uncertainty directly estimated data.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: HKSJ correction preferred adjustment kk small. tt-approximation computationally cheaper alternative slightly lower power.

title: Small-sample adjustments
code: fit_hksj <- bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   small_sample_adjustment = "hksj" )  fit_t <- bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   small_sample_adjustment = "t_approx" )

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: HKSJ correction choice RE distribution (Gaussian, Student-tt, skew-normal) independent. Student-tt RE distribution HKSJ correction accounts -study distributional form imprecision within-study variance estimates.

title: Small-sample adjustments
text: Simulations range kk (5 30), τ\tau values (0 1 log-scale), study sizes consistently show : unadjusted estimator 80–88% coverage nominal 95% level k<10k < 10 HKSJ restores coverage 93–96% settings tt-approximation restores coverage 91–94% HKSJ advantage tt-approximation largest τ̂2\hat{\tau}^2 estimated high uncertainty, .e., kk small τ\tau large.

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
text: bias-corrected Bayesian non-parametric (BC-BNP) model (Verde & Rosner, 2025) represents distribution true effects non-parametrically via Dirichlet process mixture. Publication bias corrected estimating selection probability function effect size precision. See BC-BNP model statistical rationale.

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
text: Likelihood (marginalised latent component assignment): p(yi∣G,π)=∫𝒩(yi∣θ,si2)⋅π(si,θ)⋅dG(θ)/C(π,G) p(y_i \mid G, \pi) = \int \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \theta, s_i^2) \cdot \pi(s_i, \theta) \cdot dG(\theta) \bigg/ C(\pi, G) Dirichlet process approximation (truncated stick-breaking): G≈∑h=1Hπh⋅δμh,πh=vh∏l<h(1−vl),vh∼Beta(1,α0) G \approx \sum_{h=1}^{H} \pi_h \cdot \delta_{\mu_h}, \qquad \pi_h = v_h \prod_{l < h}(1 - v_l), \qquad v_h \sim \text{Beta}(1, \alpha_0) Component distributions: μh∼𝒩(0,σh2),σh∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu_h \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_h^2), \qquad \sigma_h \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) Selection probability: π(si,θ)=logistic(λ0+λ1/si) \pi(s_i, \theta) = \text{logistic}(\lambda_0 + \lambda_1 / s_i)

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> H;             // truncation level for DP   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   vector[H] mu_h;   vector<lower=0>[H] sigma_h;   vector<lower=0, upper=1>[H] v;   real<lower=0> alpha0;   real lambda0;   real lambda1; }  transformed parameters {   simplex[H] weights;   {     real remaining = 1.0;     for (h in 1:(H - 1)) {       weights[h] = v[h] * remaining;       remaining -= weights[h];     }     weights[H] = remaining;   } }  model {   target += gamma_lpdf(alpha0  | 1, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(lambda0 | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(lambda1 | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(mu_h    | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(sigma_h | 0, 0.5);   target += beta_lpdf(v         | 1, alpha0);    for (i in 1:N) {     real sel_i = inv_logit(lambda0 + lambda1 / se[i]);     vector[H] log_liks;     for (h in 1:H) {       log_liks[h] = log(weights[h])                   + normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu_h[h], sqrt(square(se[i]) + square(sigma_h[h])))                   + log(sel_i);     }     target += log_sum_exp(log_liks);   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = dot_product(weights, mu_h); }

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
text: truncation level HH defaults 10; larger values allow distributional flexibility increase computation.
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type   = "bc_bnp",   alpha_prior  = gamma(1, 1),   p_bias_prior = beta(1, 1) )

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
text: stick-breaking construction approximates infinite Dirichlet process HH components. H=10H = 10 sufficient meta-analytic data sets. b_Intercept DP mixture mean: expected true effect averaged non-parametric component distribution. log_sum_exp likelihood marginalises component assignment, avoiding discrete sampling.

title: Stan code — BC-BNP model
text: BC-BNP model computationally expensive. N=30N = 30 studies H=10H = 10, expect roughly 5–10× longer sampling standard RE model. Use parallel_chains = 4 allow longer warmup (iter_warmup = 2000). Divergences near stick-breaking boundaries rare Beta(1, alpha0) prior may occur alpha0 small.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: Jung Aloe (2026) bias-adjusted model extends Verde (2021) replacing composite risk--bias score domain-specific binary RoB indicators. RoB domain contributes bias coefficient ξd\xi_d, allowing model estimate domains strongly associated effect inflation. See Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe 2026) full statistical rationale.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: Decomposition: yi=θi+bi+εi,εi∼𝒩(0,si2) y_i = \theta_i + b_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, s_i^2) True effects: θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2) Bias component (domain-specific): bi∼𝒩(∑d=1DRid⋅ξd,σb2) b_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sum_{d=1}^{D} R_{id} \cdot \xi_d,\, \sigma_b^2\right) Rid∈{0,1}R_{id} \\{0, 1\} RoB indicator study ii domain dd, ξd\xi_d mean bias contribution domain dd. Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) ξd∼𝒩(0,0.5),d=1,…,D,σb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \xi_d \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.5), \quad d = 1, \ldots, D, \qquad \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\, 0.5)

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   int<lower=1> D;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   matrix[N, D] R;              // domain-specific RoB indicators   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[D] xi;   real<lower=0> sigma_b;   vector[K] z_theta;   vector[N] z_b; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u_theta  = tau * z_theta;   vector[N] b_mean   = R * xi;   vector[N] b        = b_mean + sigma_b * z_b; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu      | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau     | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(xi      | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(sigma_b | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_theta);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_b);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u_theta[study] + b, se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept  = mu;   vector[D] b_xi    = xi; }

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: rob_domain_cols names binary RoB domain columns. coded 0 (low risk) 1 (high risk).
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type      = "bias_corrected",   bias_source     = "jung_aloe_2026",   rob_domain_cols = c("rob_randomisation", "rob_deviations",                       "rob_missing", "rob_measurement", "rob_selection") )

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: R * xi matrix-vector product: b_mean[] = sum_d R[,d] * xi[d]. positive ξd\xi_d means high risk domain dd associated upward bias. b_xi generated quantities provides posterior domain coefficient.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: Jung & Aloe (2026) model DD additional parameters compared Verde (2021). Use compare_models() assess whether domain-specific coefficients improve predictive accuracy: small meta-analyses (k<20k < 20), additional parameters may estimable, Verde (2021) preferable.
code: compare_models(   verde = fit_verde,   jung  = fit_jung )

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)
text: Verde (2021) model. many RoB domains (D>5D > 5) relative studies (kk), ξd\xi_d weakly identified. Consider group-level prior (horseshoe regularised horseshoe) ξd\xi_d shrinkage towards zero.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Verde (2021) decomposes study’s observed effect true effect component latent bias component. bias component parameterised linear function observed risk--bias score ρi∈[0,1]\rho_i \[0, 1]. See Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021) statistical rationale.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: Decomposition: yi=θi+bi+εi,εi∼𝒩(0,si2) y_i = \theta_i + b_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, s_i^2) True effects: θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2) Bias component: bi∼𝒩(ρi⋅ξ,σb2) b_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\rho_i \cdot \xi,\, \sigma_b^2) Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) ξ∼𝒩(0,0.5),σb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \xi \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\, 0.5)

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   vector<lower=0, upper=1>[N] rho;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real xi;   real<lower=0> sigma_b;   vector[K] z_theta;   vector[N] z_b; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u_theta = tau * z_theta;   vector[N] b       = rho .* xi + sigma_b * z_b; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu      | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau     | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(xi      | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(sigma_b | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_theta);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_b);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u_theta[study] + b, se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu;   real b_xi        = xi; }

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type  = "bias_corrected",   bias_source = "verde_2021",   rho_col     = "rob_composite_score" )

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: rho .* xi element-wise multiplication: b_i = rho_i * xi + sigma_b * z_b[]. b_Intercept = mu bias-adjusted pooled effect (effect RoB scores equal zero). b_xi quantifies mean bias per unit RoB score. z_theta (study-level true effects) z_b (study-level biases) use non-centred parameterisation efficient sampling.

title: Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Verde 2021)
text: model two overlapping random-effect structures (z_theta z_b), can create funnel geometry τ\tau σb\sigma_b small. Increasing adapt_delta 0.99 recommended. Inspection R̂\hat{R} variance components essential.

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: common-effect meta-analysis model assumes studies estimate true effect μ\mu. Observed differences study estimates arise solely sampling error. -study heterogeneity parameter.

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: Likelihood: yi∣μ∼𝒩(μ,si2),=1,…,k y_i \mid \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\, s_i^2), \quad = 1, \ldots, k Prior: μ∼𝒩(0,1) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1) Derived quantity: b_Intercept=μ b\_\text{Intercept} = \mu posterior analytically tractable (conjugate Gaussian) bayesma fits via MCMC consistency rest pipeline.

title: Stan code — common-effect model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   real mu; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(y  | mu, se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: bayesma() selects model model_type = "common_effect". data list passed Stan contains: Internally bayesma_stan_data() computes yiy_i sis_i raw outcome columns (events, means, SDs, sample sizes) passing Stan.
code: list(   N  = nrow(data),   y  = data$yi,       # pre-computed effect estimates   se = data$sei       # pre-computed standard errors )

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: prior μ∼𝒩(0,1)\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) weakly informative log-SMD scale. studies scales (e.g., raw mean differences physical units), prior widened via mu_prior = normal(0, sigma) σ\sigma reflects plausible range effects. common-effect model single parameter. k≥5k \geq 5, likelihood dominates prior posterior largely data-driven. random-effects structure; τ\tau parameter; non-centred parameterisation needed.

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: model fully identified single observation (k=1k = 1). kk increases, posterior μ\mu concentrates around precision-weighted average yiy_i.

title: Stan code — common-effect model
text: None. posterior unimodal near-Gaussian. MCMC mixes rapidly default settings.

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
text: Copas selection model (Copas & Shi, 2001) models publication probability function study precision (sample size). Smaller studies — higher standard errors — less likely published larger studies. model estimates pooled effect selection mechanism jointly.

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
text: Outcome model: yi=μ+ϵi,ϵi∼𝒩(0,si2+τ2) y_i = \mu + \epsilon_i, \quad \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, s_i^2 + \tau^2) Selection mechanism: P(publishedi∣γ)=Φ(γ0+γ1si) P(\text{published}_i \mid \gamma) = \Phi\!\left(\gamma_0 + \frac{\gamma_1}{s_i}\right) Φ\Phi standard normal CDF, γ0\gamma_0 controls baseline publication probability, γ1\gamma_1 controls association precision (1/si1/s_i) publication. Joint bivariate model: outcome yiy_i selection indicator δi=1\delta_i = 1 jointly modelled bivariate normal: (yiδi*)∼𝒩2((μγ0+γ1/si),(si2+τ2ρσyρσy1)) \begin{pmatrix} y_i \\ \delta_i^* \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}_2\!\left(\begin{pmatrix} \mu \\ \gamma_0 + \gamma_1/s_i \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} s_i^2 + \tau^2 & \rho \sigma_y \\ \rho \sigma_y & 1 \end{pmatrix}\right) ρ\rho correlation true outcome latent propensity publish, δi*>0⇒δi=1\delta_i^* > 0 \Rightarrow \delta_i = 1. observed-data likelihood (conditioning δi=1\delta_i = 1) : p(yi∣δi=1)∝ϕ(yi−μσi)⋅Φ(γ0+γ1/si+ρ(yi−μ)/σi1−ρ2) p(y_i \mid \delta_i = 1) \propto \phi\!\left(\frac{y_i - \mu}{\sigma_i}\right) \cdot \Phi\!\left(\frac{\gamma_0 + \gamma_1/s_i + \rho(y_i - \mu)/\sigma_i}{\sqrt{1 - \rho^2}}\right) σi2=si2+τ2\sigma_i^2 = s_i^2 + \tau^2. Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),γ0∼𝒩(0,1),γ1∼Half-Normal(0,1),ρ∼Uniform(−1,1) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \gamma_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \gamma_1 \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\, 1), \qquad \rho \sim \text{Uniform}(-1,\, 1)

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real gamma0;   real<lower=0> gamma1;   real<lower=-1, upper=1> rho; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu     | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau    | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(gamma0 | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(gamma1 | 0, 1);   target += uniform_lpdf(rho   | -1, 1);    for (i in 1:N) {     real sigma_i = sqrt(square(se[i]) + square(tau));     real z_sel   = gamma0 + gamma1 / se[i];     real z_adj   = (z_sel + rho * (y[i] - mu) / sigma_i) / sqrt(1 - square(rho));      target += normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sigma_i) + normal_lcdf(z_adj | 0, 1)             - normal_lcdf(z_sel | 0, 1);   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "selection_copas",   gamma_prior = list(gamma0 = normal(0, 1), gamma1 = half_normal(0, 1)) )

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
text: γ1>0\gamma_1 > 0 enforces direction constraint: larger studies (smaller sis_i) likely published. ρ\rho captures relationship effect magnitude publication. Positive ρ\rho means larger effects likely published (common publication bias mechanism). normalising denominator normal_lcdf(z_sel | 0, 1) corrects fact published studies observed.

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
text: Copas model identified without prior information either γ0\gamma_0 ρ\rho. bayesma provides sensitivity plot grid (γ0,γ1)(\gamma_0, \gamma_1) values:
code: sensitivity_plot(fit_copas, type = "copas_grid")

title: Stan code — Copas selection model
text: Copas likelihood non-convex can multiple local modes (μ,ρ)(\mu, \rho) space. Use least 4 chains, inspect traces, run adapt_delta = 0.99.

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
text: longitudinal meta-analysis model estimates treatment effect evolves time across studies. Studies contribute multiple time-point observations; parametric trajectory describes population-level effect time, study-level random intercepts capturing -study heterogeneity. vignette documents linear trajectory implementation. See Longitudinal meta-analysis statistical rationale alternative trajectory forms.

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
text: Likelihood: yit∣θit∼𝒩(θit,sit2) y_{} \mid \theta_{} \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_{},\, s_{}^2) Trajectory: θit=(μ0+ui)+μ1⋅tit+vit \theta_{} = (\mu_0 + u_i) + \mu_1 \cdot t_{} + v_{} Priors: μ0∼𝒩(0,1),μ1∼𝒩(0,0.5),τu∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),τv∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \mu_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \tau_u \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \tau_v \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5)

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   vector[N] time;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu0;   real mu1;   real<lower=0> tau_u;   real<lower=0> tau_v;   vector[K] z_u;   vector[N] z_v; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau_u * z_u;   vector[N] v = tau_v * z_v; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu0   | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(mu1   | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_u | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau_v | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_u);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z_v);    target += normal_lpdf(y | (mu0 + u[study]) + mu1 * time + v, se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu0;   real b_time      = mu1; }

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
text: time vector Stan data constructed data[[time_col]], centred median observation time interpretability.
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   time_col   = "weeks",   trajectory = "linear" )

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
text: uiu_i study-level random intercept (deviation μ0\mu_0 t=0t = 0). vitv_{} study--time residual capturing deviations linear trajectory within studies. exponential decay trajectory, additional parameter λ>0\lambda > 0 required Half-Normal(0,1)\text{Half-Normal}(0, 1) prior. b_time reports posterior rate change μ1\mu_1 per unit centred time variable.

title: Stan code — longitudinal model
text: residual term vitv_{} can weakly identified study contributes one two time-points. case, fix τv=0\tau_v = 0 (drop vitv_{} term) improve mixing. model reduces random-intercept longitudinal meta-analysis.

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: meta-regression model extends random-effects model design matrix study-level moderators. Moderator coefficients estimated alongside -study heterogeneity.

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) Regression moderators: θi=μ+𝐱i⊤𝛃+ui,ui∼𝒩(0,τ2) \theta_i = \mu + \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_i, \quad u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \tau^2) Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),βj∼𝒩(0,0.5),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5)

title: Stan code — meta-regression
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   int<lower=1> P;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   matrix[N, P] X;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   vector[P] beta;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu   | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(beta | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau  | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + X * beta + u[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu;   vector[P] b      = beta; }

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: meta_reg() constructs design matrix X user’s formula: X columns term formula model matrix expansion (dummy coding factors, polynomial expansion poly() terms). Centring scaling applied passing Stan.
code: fit_mr <- meta_reg(   data,   formula = ~ duration + mean_age,   center  = TRUE,   scale   = TRUE )

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: default 𝒩(0,0.5)\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5) prior βj\beta_j can overridden per coefficient: beta_priors named list mapping column names prior objects. Columns named use beta_prior.
code: meta_reg(   data,   formula      = ~ duration + mean_age,   beta_prior   = normal(0, 0.5),   beta_priors  = list(duration = normal(0, 0.2), mean_age = normal(0, 1)) )

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: mu intercept: expected effect continuous moderators centred values (typically mean) categorical moderators reference level. interpretation depends centring; always use center = TRUE interpretable intercepts. tau residual -study SD unexplained moderators. τ\tau substantially smaller τ\tau intercept-model, moderators account meaningful proportion heterogeneity.

title: Stan code — meta-regression
text: Meta-regression posteriors well-behaved moderators uncorrelated sample size adequate. Problems arise : Moderators highly collinear (VIF > 5): joint posterior 𝛃\boldsymbol{\beta} elongated mixing slow. design matrix nearly rank-deficient: Stan warn numerical issues matrix operations. PP large relative kk: τ\tau overparameterised divergences may occur.

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
text: Maier mixture model (Maier et al., 2023) represents observed effects arising two populations: genuinely unbiased studies publication-bias-inflated studies. proportion biased studies inflated effect mean estimated jointly true effect. See Mixture model (Maier) full statistical rationale.

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
text: Mixture likelihood: p(yi∣θ,σθ,μb,σb,πb)=(1−πb)⋅𝒩(yi;θ,σθ2+si2)+πb⋅𝒩(yi;μb,σb2+si2) p(y_i \mid \theta, \sigma_\theta, \mu_b, \sigma_b, \pi_b) = (1 - \pi_b) \cdot \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_i;\, \theta,\, \sigma_\theta^2 + s_i^2\right) + \pi_b \cdot \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_i;\, \mu_b,\, \sigma_b^2 + s_i^2\right) constraint μb>θ\mu_b > \theta (biased studies overestimate effect). Priors: θ∼𝒩(0,1),σθ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \sigma_\theta \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) δb=μb−θ∼Half-Normal(0,0.5),σb∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \delta_b = \mu_b - \theta \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) πb∼Beta(1,4) \pi_b \sim \text{Beta}(1,\, 4)

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   real theta;   real<lower=0> sigma_theta;   real<lower=0> delta_b;   real<lower=0> sigma_b;   real<lower=0, upper=1> pi_b; }  transformed parameters {   real mu_b = theta + delta_b; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(theta       | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(sigma_theta | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(delta_b     | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(sigma_b     | 0, 0.5);   target += beta_lpdf(pi_b          | 1, 4);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += log_mix(       pi_b,       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu_b,   sqrt(square(sigma_b)     + square(se[i]))),       normal_lpdf(y[i] | theta,  sqrt(square(sigma_theta) + square(se[i])))     );   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = theta;   real b_mu_b      = mu_b;   real b_pi_b      = pi_b; }

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type   = "mixture_model",   p_bias_prior = beta(1, 4) )

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
text: delta_b = mu_b - theta constrained positive, enforcing assumption biased studies overestimate true effect. eliminates one two label-switching modes. log_mix() marginalises latent component assignment ziz_i, allowing continuous sampling. Beta(1, 4) prior πb\pi_b encodes prior expectation majority published studies unbiased.

title: Stan code — Mixture model (Maier)
text: mixture constraint mu_b > theta removes one label-switching mode posterior πb\pi_b σb\sigma_b can still multimodal two components well-separated. Use adapt_delta = 0.99, inspect per-chain posteriors, report sensitivity prior πb\pi_b.

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: multiarm model extends one-stage binomial model studies three arms. Within-study random effects arm jointly modelled correlated normal distribution, accounting shared control arm -arm correlations within studies.

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: Likelihood: rij∼Binomial(nij,πij),j=1,…,Ai r_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij},\, \pi_{ij}), \quad j = 1, \ldots, A_i logit(πij)=γi+∑t=1Tθt⋅𝟙[tij=t]+εij \text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \theta_t \cdot \mathbb{1}[t_{ij} = t] + \varepsilon_{ij} Within-study random effects: 𝛆i∼𝒩T(𝟎,𝚺),𝚺=diag(𝛕)⋅Ω⋅diag(𝛕) \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_i \sim \mathcal{N}_T(\mathbf{0},\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}), \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) \cdot \Omega \cdot \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) Priors: γi∼𝒩(0,10),θt∼𝒩(0,2.5),𝛕∼Half-t3(0,2.5),Ω∼LKJ(η) \gamma_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 10), \qquad \theta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 2.5), \qquad \boldsymbol{\tau} \sim \text{Half-}t_3(0,\, 2.5), \qquad \Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(\eta)

title: Stan code — multiarm model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> S;   int<lower=1> T;   array[N] int<lower=0> events;   array[N] int<lower=1> n;   array[N] int<lower=1> study;   array[N] int<lower=0> treatment; }  parameters {   vector[S] gamma;   vector[T] theta;   vector<lower=0>[T] tau;   matrix[S, T] z;   cholesky_factor_corr[T] L_Omega; }  transformed parameters {   matrix[S, T] epsilon = (diag_pre_multiply(tau, L_Omega) * z')'; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(gamma   | 0, 10);   target += normal_lpdf(theta   | 0, 2.5);   target += student_t_lpdf(tau  | 3, 0, 2.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(to_vector(z));   target += lkj_corr_cholesky_lpdf(L_Omega | 1);    {     vector[N] eta;     for (i in 1:N) {       eta[i] = gamma[study[i]];       if (treatment[i] > 0) {         eta[i] += theta[treatment[i]] + epsilon[study[i], treatment[i]];       }     }     target += binomial_logit_lpmf(events | n, eta);   } }  generated quantities {   vector[T] b_treat = theta;   corr_matrix[T] Omega = multiply_lower_tri_self_transpose(L_Omega); }

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: Selected multiarm = TRUE stage = "one_stage". rho_prior argument sets LKJ concentration:
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   stage      = "one_stage",   multiarm   = TRUE,   rho_prior  = lkj(eta = 1) )

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: Cholesky factorisation L_Omega correlation matrix used instead full correlation matrix efficiency. multiply_lower_tri_self_transpose(L_Omega) recovers Ω\Omega generated quantities block reporting. treatment[] = 0 case (control arm) contributes study baseline γi\gamma_i linear predictor.

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: correlation matrix Ω\Omega identified -study variation across multiarm studies. multiarm studies (<5< 5), Ω\Omega poorly estimated. case, fix Ω=\Omega = (identity, implying independent arms) removing LKJ prior setting L_Omega = identity_matrix(T).

title: Stan code — multiarm model
text: Cholesky parameterisation NCP random effects matrix greatly improve sampling. Divergences common TT large (>3> 3) multiarm studies . Increase adapt_delta 0.99 needed.

title: Stan code — multivariate model
text: multivariate meta-analysis model jointly estimates pooled effects PP correlated outcomes. Within-study outcome correlations treated known (imputed); -study correlations estimated via LKJ prior -study correlation matrix.

title: Stan code — multivariate model
text: Likelihood: 𝐲i∣𝛉i∼𝒩P(𝛉i,𝐒i) \mathbf{y}_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}_i \sim \mathcal{N}_P(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i,\, \mathbf{S}_i) 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i known within-study covariance matrix (constructed reported SEs imputed within-study correlation ρwithin\rho_\text{within}). Random effects: 𝛉i∼𝒩P(𝛍,𝚺),𝚺=diag(𝛕)⋅Ω⋅diag(𝛕) \boldsymbol{\theta}_i \sim \mathcal{N}_P(\boldsymbol{\mu},\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}), \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) \cdot \Omega \cdot \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\tau}) Priors: μp∼𝒩(0,1),τp∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),Ω∼LKJ(η) \mu_p \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau_p \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(\eta)

title: Stan code — multivariate model
code: data {   int<lower=1> K;   int<lower=1> P;   array[K] vector[P] y;   array[K] matrix[P, P] S;  // within-study covariance matrices }  parameters {   vector[P] mu;   vector<lower=0>[P] tau;   matrix[K, P] z;   cholesky_factor_corr[P] L_Omega; }  transformed parameters {   matrix[K, P] u = (diag_pre_multiply(tau, L_Omega) * z')'; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu       | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau      | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(to_vector(z));   target += lkj_corr_cholesky_lpdf(L_Omega | 1);    for (i in 1:K) {     matrix[P, P] Sigma_total = S[i] + quad_form_diag(       multiply_lower_tri_self_transpose(L_Omega), tau     );     target += multi_normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu + u[i]', Sigma_total);   } }  generated quantities {   vector[P] b_Intercept = mu;   corr_matrix[P] Omega  = multiply_lower_tri_self_transpose(L_Omega); }

title: Stan code — multivariate model
text: Fitted via bayesma_mv(): rho_within used construct within-study covariance matrices 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i. rho_within = NULL, Riley et al. (2008) marginalisation ρwithin\rho_\text{within} used.
code: bayesma_mv(   data,   outcomes   = c("lnOR_primary", "lnOR_secondary"),   se_cols    = c("se_primary", "se_secondary"),   rho_within = 0.5 )

title: Stan code — multivariate model
text: total covariance study ii 𝐒i+𝚺\mathbf{S}_i + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}: within-study uncertainty plus -study heterogeneity. constructed model block using quad_form_diag. b_Intercept vector length PP; first element primary outcome’s pooled effect.

title: Stan code — multivariate model
text: multivariate model can slow P>3P > 3 due P×PP \times P matrix operations likelihood loop. large PP, consider sequential modelling strategy fixing Ω\Omega identity matrix.

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: bayesma fits models Stan via cmdstanr interface. vignette explains interface works, documents notation conventions used across Stan Code vignettes, describes extract modify generated Stan code.

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: call bayesma() runs six-stage internal pipeline: spec (bayesma_spec()): validates arguments resolves model type, likelihood, stage, RE distribution, prior specifications structured list. code (bayesma_stan_code()): generates Stan program character string spec. data (bayesma_stan_data()): prepares named list data passed cmdstanr::cmdstan_model()$sample(). fit: compiles Stan model runs MCMC. Compilation cached. extract (bayesma_extract()): reshapes posterior draws tidy format. output (bayesma_output()): formats posterior display. stage can returned early inspection: return_stage = "code" returns Stan program character string; print cat().
code: spec → code → data → fit → extract → output spec  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "spec") code  <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "code") sdata <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "data") fit   <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "fit")

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: Stan Code vignettes use following notation consistently: Stan variable names follow convention wherever possible.

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: random-effects models bayesma use non-centred parameterisation (NCP) study-level random effects: NCP separates scale (τ\tau) shape (zz) random effects. equivalent centred parameterisation samples efficiently τ\tau small relative prior, geometry posterior near τ=0\tau = 0 better behaved NCP.
code: parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] z;          // standard normal auxiliary }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z;  // re-centred in transformed parameters } parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] u; } model {   u ~ normal(0, tau); }

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: returned string valid Stan. can edited passed back via custom_model: larger modifications, write custom code .stan file:
code: code <- bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", return_stage = "code") cat(code) modified <- stringr::str_replace(   code,   "target \\+= cauchy_lpdf\\(tau \\| 0, 0.5\\);",   "target += half_normal_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.25);" )  fit <- bayesma(data, custom_model = modified) writeLines(code, "my_model.stan") # ... edit my_model.stan ... fit <- bayesma(data, custom_model = readLines("my_model.stan"))

title: Stan code — overview and conventions
text: Divergent transitions. adapt_delta warnings appear, increase adapt_delta: Persistent divergences increasing adapt_delta indicate pathological posterior geometry, often caused funnel-shaped posterior near τ=0\tau = 0. Switching NCP (already default) placing informative prior τ\tau resolves cases. Effective sample size. Aim bulk ESS > 400 per parameter. Low ESS τ\tau common kk small posterior τ\tau near-zero; indicate bug inflate Monte Carlo error tail quantities. Rhat. R̂<1.01\hat{R} < 1.01 requirement reliable inference. Values 1.05 indicate chains mixed. Compilation caching. Stan compiles model C++ binary first use. Subsequent calls model fast. model code changes (including whitespace cmdstanr versions), recompilation triggered.
code: bayesma(data, adapt_delta = 0.99)

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: PET-PEESE (Stanley & Doucouliagos, 2014) corrects publication bias regressing effects standard errors (PET) squared standard errors (PEESE). intercept estimates effect infinite precision — unbiased estimate.

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: PET likelihood: yi∣α,β∼𝒩(α+β⋅si,si2+τ2) y_i \mid \alpha, \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\alpha + \beta \cdot s_i,\, s_i^2 + \tau^2) PEESE likelihood: yi∣α,β∼𝒩(α+β⋅si2,si2+τ2) y_i \mid \alpha, \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\alpha + \beta \cdot s_i^2,\, s_i^2 + \tau^2) Priors: α∼𝒩(0,1),β∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5)

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: PET model, replace square(se) se mean (needed) different specification.
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   real alpha;   real beta;   real<lower=0> tau; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(alpha | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(beta  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau   | 0, 0.5);    target += normal_lpdf(y | alpha + beta * square(se),                            sqrt(square(se) + square(tau))); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = alpha; }

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: default PEESE, preferred meta-analytic effect expected non-zero. PET preferred null testing whether effect adjusting publication bias.
code: bayesma(data, model_type = "pet_peese", pet_peese_form = "peese") bayesma(data, model_type = "pet_peese", pet_peese_form = "pet")

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: alpha bias-corrected pooled effect (effect si=0s_i = 0). beta publication-bias slope: rate effects grow standard error increases. tau captures residual -study heterogeneity explained precision-effect relationship. b_Intercept = alpha estimand reported bayesma_output().

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: PET-PEESE identified meaningful variation sis_i across studies. studies similar precision, slope β\beta weakly identified intercept α\alpha uncertain. limitation method, model specification error.

title: Stan code — PET-PEESE
text: PET-PEESE linear regression model samples efficiently. divergences expected. potential issue τ\tau near zero posterior becomes flat; slightly informative prior τ\tau helps.

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: Gaussian random-effects model standard Bayesian meta-analysis model. Study-level true effects θi\theta_i drawn normal distribution mean μ\mu standard deviation τ\tau. Observed effects yiy_i noisy realisations θi\theta_i.

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) Random effects: θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2)⇔θi=μ+τzi,zi∼𝒩(0,1) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\, \tau^2) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \theta_i = \mu + \tau z_i, \quad z_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) Derived quantities: ui=τzi(study-level deviation),b_Intercept=μ u_i = \tau z_i \quad (\text{study-level deviation}), \qquad b\_\text{Intercept} = \mu

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: default model_type = "random_effect" re_dist = "normal". Stan data list includes study index array, mapping row data study. two-stage meta-analysis, study contributes one row; N=KN = K. one-stage models multiple arms per study, N>KN > K. default prior τ\tau Half-Cauchy(0, 0.5). places substantial prior mass τ=1\tau = 1 allowing larger values. can changed tau_prior:
code: bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", tau_prior = half_normal(0, 0.25))

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: z parameterisation separates τ\tau shape random effects. τ\tau near zero, centred parameterisation (using u directly) creates funnel-shaped posterior uiu_i must approach zero together τ→0\tau \0. NCP avoids sampling zz fixed 𝒩(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0,1) scale.

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: k<5k < 5, τ\tau weakly identified. posterior τ\tau reflects prior heavily. case: Report sensitivity conclusions τ\tau prior. Consider using informative τ\tau prior external source (e.g., Turner et al. 2015). posterior mean μ\mu still valid estimator, credible interval may misleading.

title: Stan code — Gaussian random-effects model
text: funnel-shaped posterior near τ=0\tau = 0 can cause divergent transitions centred parameterisation. NCP (used default) resolves cases. divergences persist: Increase adapt_delta 0.99. Place informative prior τ\tau (e.g., half_normal(0, 0.5) instead half_cauchy(0, 0.5)).

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
text: random-effects mixture model heterogeneity (distinct publication bias mixture model) represents distribution true study effects mixture two Gaussian components. captures bimodal heterogeneity — example, two distinct populations studies exist produce qualitatively different effect sizes. model mixture RE distribution framed explicitly heterogeneity assessment tool rather distributional sensitivity analysis.

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) Mixture RE prior: p(θi)=π⋅𝒩(θi∣μ1,τ12)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(θi∣μ2,τ22) p(\theta_i) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_1,\, \tau_1^2) + (1 - \pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_2,\, \tau_2^2) Marginal likelihood (integrated θi\theta_i): p(yi)=π⋅𝒩(yi∣μ1,τ12+si2)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(yi∣μ2,τ22+si2) p(y_i) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_1,\, \tau_1^2 + s_i^2) + (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_2,\, \tau_2^2 + s_i^2) Priors: π∼Beta(2,2),μj∼𝒩(0,1),τj∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(2,\, 2), \qquad \mu_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau_j \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) ordering constraint μ1≤μ2\mu_1 \leq \mu_2 resolve label switching.

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   ordered[2] mu;   vector<lower=0>[2] tau;   real<lower=0, upper=1> pi_mix; }  model {   target += beta_lpdf(pi_mix | 2, 2);   target += normal_lpdf(mu   | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau  | 0, 0.5);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += log_mix(       pi_mix,       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu[1], sqrt(square(tau[1]) + square(se[i]))),       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu[2], sqrt(square(tau[2]) + square(se[i])))     );   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = pi_mix * mu[1] + (1 - pi_mix) * mu[2];   real b_pi        = pi_mix; }

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   re_dist    = "mixture" )

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
text: ordered[2] mu declaration enforces μ1≤μ2\mu_1 \leq \mu_2, fully resolves label switching component means. mixing weight π\pi still identified relabelling (π,μ1,μ2)↔(1−π,μ2,μ1)(\pi, \mu_1, \mu_2) \leftrightarrow (1-\pi, \mu_2, \mu_1), ordering constraint breaks symmetry. b_Intercept mixture-weighted mean effect — expected true effect randomly drawn study.

what: Distinguishing from the Maier publication bias mixture
title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
text: RE mixture Maier model use two Gaussian components. key difference purpose constraints:

title: Stan code — RE mixture model (heterogeneity)
text: mixture RE model: use adapt_delta = 0.99 inspect per-chain traces.

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: two-component Gaussian mixture random-effects model allows distribution true study effects bimodal. Studies assumed arise one two populations — example, short-term long-term follow-populations, low-dose high-dose populations. mixing weight, component means, component SDs estimated data.

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) Random effects (mixture): p(θi)=π⋅𝒩(θi∣μ1,τ12)+(1−π)⋅𝒩(θi∣μ2,τ22) p(\theta_i) = \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_1,\, \tau_1^2) + (1 - \pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu_2,\, \tau_2^2) Priors: π∼Beta(2,2),μj∼𝒩(0,1),τj∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(2,\, 2), \qquad \mu_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau_j \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5) constraint μ1<μ2\mu_1 < \mu_2 imposed resolve label switching.

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se; }  parameters {   real<upper=0> mu1;   real<lower=0> mu2;   real<lower=0> tau1;   real<lower=0> tau2;   real<lower=0, upper=1> pi_mix; }  model {   target += beta_lpdf(pi_mix | 2, 2);   target += normal_lpdf(mu1  | 0, 1);   target += normal_lpdf(mu2  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau1 | 0, 0.5);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau2 | 0, 0.5);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += log_mix(       pi_mix,       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu1, sqrt(square(tau1) + square(se[i]))),       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu2, sqrt(square(tau2) + square(se[i])))     );   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = pi_mix * mu1 + (1 - pi_mix) * mu2; }

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: Selected model_type = "random_effect" re_dist = "mixture". constraint μ1<0\mu_1 < 0 μ2>0\mu_2 > 0 resolves label switching anchoring component 1 negative effects component 2 positive effects. components expected sign, use custom model ordered constraint: ordered[2] mu. b_Intercept mixture-weighted mean: (1−π)μ1+πμ2(1-\pi)\mu_1 + \pi\mu_2.
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   re_dist    = "mixture" )

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: log_mix() function marginalises discrete component assignment zi∈{1,2}z_i \\{1, 2\}, avoids poor mixing occurs sampling discrete parameters directly. constraint component means (μ1<0<μ2\mu_1 < 0 < \mu_2) sufficient condition eliminate label switching may inappropriate components expected sign. case, use ordered vector: prior allows components range plausible values.
code: ordered[2] mu;

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: Two-component mixture models require k≥20k \geq 20 reliable separation components. smaller kk: mixing weight π\pi poorly identified. posterior may multimodal (two label-switching modes fully eliminated constraint). Results strongly prior-dependent.

title: Stan code — mixture random-effects model
text: Mixture models among challenging posteriors sample efficiently Stan. Divergences slow mixing common. Mitigations: Use adapt_delta = 0.99. Increase warmup iterations (iter_warmup = 2000). Inspect chain traces individually. Run prior predictive simulation verify identifiability fitting.

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: skew-normal random-effects model allows distribution true study effects asymmetric. appropriate effects bounded () natural floor ceiling, literature characterised many small effects long tail large effects one direction. skew-normal distribution generalises Gaussian shape parameter αsk\alpha_\text{sk}. Positive αsk\alpha_\text{sk} produces right skew; negative produces left skew; αsk=0\alpha_\text{sk} = 0 recovers Gaussian.

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2) Random effects: θi∼SN(ξ,ω,αsk) \theta_i \sim \text{SN}(\xi,\, \omega,\, \alpha_\text{sk}) ξ\xi location, ω>0\omega > 0 scale, αsk\alpha_\text{sk} shape. mean variance skew-normal : 𝔼[θi]=ξ+ω⋅δ⋅2/π,δ=αsk1+αsk2 \mathbb{E}[\theta_i] = \xi + \omega \cdot \delta \cdot \sqrt{2/\pi}, \quad \delta = \frac{\alpha_\text{sk}}{\sqrt{1 + \alpha_\text{sk}^2}} Var(θi)=ω2(1−2δ2π) \text{Var}(\theta_i) = \omega^2 \left(1 - \frac{2\delta^2}{\pi}\right) Priors: ξ∼𝒩(0,1),ω∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),αsk∼𝒩(0,1) \xi \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \omega \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \alpha_\text{sk} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real xi;   real<lower=0> omega;   real alpha_sk;   vector[K] theta_raw; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] theta;   {     real delta  = alpha_sk / sqrt(1 + square(alpha_sk));     real sigma1 = omega * sqrt(1 - square(delta));     vector[K] mu_sn = xi + omega * delta * abs(theta_raw);     theta = mu_sn + sigma1 * theta_raw;   } }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(xi       | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(omega    | 0, 0.5);   target += normal_lpdf(alpha_sk | 0, 1);   target += std_normal_lpdf(theta_raw);    target += skew_normal_lpdf(theta | xi, omega, alpha_sk);   target += normal_lpdf(y | theta[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = xi + omega * (alpha_sk / sqrt(1 + square(alpha_sk))) * sqrt(2.0 / pi()); }

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: Selected model_type = "random_effect" re_dist = "skew_normal". b_Intercept set mean skew-normal distribution (location ξ\xi), reported pooled effect comparable Gaussian Student-tt models.
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type = "random_effect",   re_dist    = "skew_normal",   alpha_prior = normal(0, 1) )

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: Stan’s built-skew_normal_lpdf used efficiency. non-centred parameterisation skew-normal requires generating truncated normal auxiliary variables; current implementation uses direct skew-normal log-density instead, well-calibrated moderate |αsk||\alpha_\text{sk}|. |αsk||\alpha_\text{sk}| large (5), skew-normal highly asymmetric MCMC can slow. practice, meaningful skewness captured |αsk|∈[0.5,3]|\alpha_\text{sk}| \[0.5, 3].

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: shape parameter αsk\alpha_\text{sk} identified kk sufficient observe distributional tail. Simulation studies suggest k≥20k \geq 20 reliable estimation. smaller kk, set αsk\alpha_\text{sk} fixed value use Gaussian model.

title: Stan code — skew-normal random-effects model
text: posterior αsk\alpha_\text{sk} ω\omega can multimodal kk small, data consistent (ω large,αsk≈0)(\omega \text{ large}, \alpha_\text{sk} \approx 0) (wide symmetric) (ω moderate,|αsk| large)(\omega \text{ moderate}, |\alpha_\text{sk}| \text{ large}) (narrow skewed). Multiple chains trace plot inspection essential.

Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid 	heta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(	heta_i,\, s_i^2) Random effects: θi∼tν(μ,τ2) 	heta_i \sim t_

title: Stan code — Student-t random-effects model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real<lower=2> nu;   vector[K] z;   vector<lower=0>[K] v; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z ./ sqrt(v / nu); }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu   | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau  | 0, 0.5);   target += gamma_lpdf(nu    | 2, 0.1);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);   target += chi_square_lpdf(v | nu);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — Robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.)
text: robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.) extends standard Gaussian random-effects model allowing small proportion studies outliers — studies whose true effects discrepant bulk literature distort estimates μ\mu τ\tau analysed single-component Gaussian. Unlike two-component RE mixture model, assumes two substantive subpopulations different means, outlier model treats second component nuisance: role absorb anomalous studies protect inference μ\mu main component. model fit via bayesma() model = "robust_outlier".

title: Stan code — Robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.)
text: Likelihood: yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\; s_i^2) Outlier mixture prior true effects: p(θi)=(1−π)⋅𝒩(θi∣μ,τ2)+π⋅𝒩(θi∣μ,τout2) p(\theta_i) = (1 - \pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu,\; \tau^2) + \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(\theta_i \mid \mu,\; \tau_{\text{}}^2) two components share mean μ\mu different scales: τ\tau typical studies τout≫τ\tau_{\text{}} \gg \tau outlier studies. outlier component much wider — accommodates extreme effect sizes without pulling μ\mu toward . Marginalised likelihood: p(yi∣μ,τ,τout,π)=(1−π)⋅𝒩(yi∣μ,τ2+si2)+π⋅𝒩(yi∣μ,τout2+si2) p(y_i \mid \mu, \tau, \tau_{\text{}}, \pi) = (1-\pi) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu,\; \tau^2 + s_i^2) + \pi \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu,\; \tau_{\text{}}^2 + s_i^2) Outlier scale parameterisation: τout=C⋅τ,C>1 \tau_{\text{}} = C \cdot \tau, \quad C > 1 scale multiplier CC typically fixed (default C=10C = 10) assigned prior. ensures outlier component always wider main component. Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\; 0.5) π∼Beta(1,9) \pi \sim \text{Beta}(1,\; 9) Beta(1, 9) prior places prior expectation π\pi 0.10, reflecting assumption outliers uncommon.

title: Stan code — Robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   real<lower=1> C;  // outlier scale multiplier (default 10) }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real<lower=0, upper=1> pi_out; }  transformed parameters {   real tau_out = C * tau; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu     | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau    | 0, 0.5);   target += beta_lpdf(pi_out   | 1, 9);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += log_mix(       pi_out,       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(tau_out) + square(se[i]))),       normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(tau)     + square(se[i])))     );   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu;   real b_tau       = tau;   real b_pi_out    = pi_out;    // Posterior outlier probability for each study   vector[N] p_outlier;   for (i in 1:N) {     real lp_out = log(pi_out)      + normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(tau_out) + square(se[i])));     real lp_reg = log1m(pi_out)    + normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(tau)     + square(se[i])));     p_outlier[i] = exp(lp_out - log_sum_exp(lp_out, lp_reg));   } }

title: Stan code — Robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.)
text: generated quantities block computes p_outlier[] — posterior probability study ii belongs outlier component. extracted reported bayesma_output().
code: #| eval: false fit_outlier <- bayesma(   data,   model   = "robust_outlier",   C       = 10,   prior_pi_out = beta(1, 9) )  summary(fit_outlier)

title: Stan code — Robust outlier mixture model (Cruz et al.)
text: Studies p_outlier > 0.5 flagged probable outliers summary table. investigated data quality issues, coding errors, genuine moderators explain discrepancy.
code: #| eval: false bayesma_output(fit_outlier, type = "outlier_probabilities")

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: Small-sample adjustments correct pooled estimate’s credible interval within-study standard errors sis_i estimated rather known. Two adjustments implemented: Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman (HKSJ) multiplicative correction tt-approximation. See Small-sample adjustments statistical rationale.

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: Likelihood (HKSJ): yi∣μ,τ,ϕ∼tk−1(μ+ui,ϕ⋅si) y_i \mid \mu, \tau, \phi \sim t_{k-1}\!\left(\mu + u_i,\, \phi \cdot s_i\right) ui∼𝒩(0,τ2) u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \tau^2) Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),ϕ∼Half-t3(0,1) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \phi \sim \text{Half-}t_3(0,\, 1)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real<lower=0> phi;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.5);   target += student_t_lpdf(phi | 3, 0, 1);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += student_t_lpdf(y[i] | K - 1, mu + u[study[i]], phi * se[i]);   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: Likelihood (HKSJ): yi∣μ,τ,ϕ∼tk−1(μ+ui,ϕ⋅si) y_i \mid \mu, \tau, \phi \sim t_{k-1}\!\left(\mu + u_i,\, \phi \cdot s_i\right) ui∼𝒩(0,τ2) u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \tau^2) Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),ϕ∼Half-t3(0,1) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad \phi \sim \text{Half-}t_3(0,\, 1)

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   real<lower=0> phi;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.5);   target += student_t_lpdf(phi | 3, 0, 1);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    for (i in 1:N) {     target += student_t_lpdf(y[i] | K - 1, mu + u[study[i]], phi * se[i]);   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: tt-approximation widens prior μ\mu tk−1t_{k-1} distribution, effectively matching tail behaviour frequentist tt-based confidence interval: μ∼tk−1(0,σμ) \mu \sim t_{k-1}(0,\, \sigma_\mu) yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2),θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2), \quad \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\, \tau^2)
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += student_t_lpdf(mu | K - 1, 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau   | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: tt-approximation widens prior μ\mu tk−1t_{k-1} distribution, effectively matching tail behaviour frequentist tt-based confidence interval: μ∼tk−1(0,σμ) \mu \sim t_{k-1}(0,\, \sigma_\mu) yi∣θi∼𝒩(θi,si2),θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) y_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i,\, s_i^2), \quad \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\, \tau^2)

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> K;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> study; }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   vector[K] z; }  transformed parameters {   vector[K] u = tau * z; }  model {   target += student_t_lpdf(mu | K - 1, 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau   | 0, 0.5);   target += std_normal_lpdf(z);    target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u[study], se); }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
code: bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", small_sample_adjustment = "hksj") bayesma(data, model_type = "random_effect", small_sample_adjustment = "t_approx")

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: HKSJ model estimates ϕ\phi additional parameter. Values ϕ<1\phi < 1 indicate less variability expected sis_i alone (uncommon); ϕ>1\phi > 1 indicates overdispersion. ϕ\phi posterior serves diagnostic: ϕ\phi concentrated well 1, standard errors systematically underestimated.

title: Stan code — small-sample adjustments
text: HKSJ model involves loop observations per-observation tt log-densities. slower vectorised Gaussian typically converges without difficulty. tt-approximation additional computational cost standard RE model.

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
text: Vevea-Hedges (1995) selection model adjusts publication bias explicitly modelling probability study published function pp-value. Studies low-significance bins (high pp-values) underrepresented literature; model corrects upweighting contribution likelihood.

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
text: pp-value bins: pp-value range [0,1][0, 1] divided intervals c0=0<c1<…<cJ=1c_0 = 0 < c_1 < \ldots < c_J = 1. Typical bin boundaries {0.025,0.05,0.10,0.25,0.50,1.0}\{0.025, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 1.0\}. Selection weights: wj=P(published∣pi∈(cj−1,cj]) w_j = P(\text{published} \mid p_i \(c_{j-1}, c_j]) weights wjw_j constrained [0,1][0, 1] typically set w1=1w_1 = 1 (studies p<0.025p < 0.025 always published). Weighted likelihood: L(μ,τ,𝐰∣𝐲)=∏=1kwb()⋅p(yi∣μ,τ)∑j=1Jwj⋅P(pi∈(cj−1,cj]∣μ,τ) L(\mu, \tau, \mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \prod_{=1}^{k} \frac{w_{b()} \cdot p(y_i \mid \mu, \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j \cdot P(p_i \(c_{j-1}, c_j] \mid \mu, \tau)} b()b() bin study ii’s pp-value P(pi∈(cj−1,cj]∣μ,τ)P(p_i \(c_{j-1}, c_j] \mid \mu, \tau) probability falling bin model. Priors: μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),wj∼Beta(1,1) j>1 \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad w_j \sim \text{Beta}(1,\, 1) \text{ } j > 1

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
code: data {   int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> J;   vector[N] y;   vector<lower=0>[N] se;   array[N] int<lower=1> bin;        // p-value bin for each study   matrix[N, J] bin_probs;           // P(p in bin j | mu, tau) for each study }  parameters {   real mu;   real<lower=0> tau;   simplex[J] w_raw; }  transformed parameters {   vector[J] w = w_raw;   w[1] = 1.0; }  model {   target += normal_lpdf(mu  | 0, 1);   target += cauchy_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.5);   for (j in 2:J) {     target += beta_lpdf(w[j] | 1, 1);   }    for (i in 1:N) {     real denom = dot_product(w, bin_probs[i]);     target += log(w[bin[i]]) + normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(se[i]) + square(tau)))             - log(denom);   } }  generated quantities {   real b_Intercept = mu; }

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
text: bin_probs computed internally bayesma_stan_data() using normal CDF evaluated bin boundaries.
code: bayesma(   data,   model_type       = "selection_weight",   p_cutoffs        = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 1.0),   selection_priors = list(w2 = beta(1, 1), w3 = beta(1, 1)) )

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
text: normalising denominator weighted likelihood key computational component. ensures model correctly accounts fact published studies observed. wjw_j parameterisation treats first bin (significant) reference w1=1w_1 = 1. Weaker significance bins wj≤1w_j \leq 1 construction.

title: Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model
text: normalising denominator ∑jwjP(…)\sum_j w_j P(\ldots) depends μ\mu τ\tau, creating complex likelihood surface. many bins small kk, wjw_j posterior multimodal. Increasing adapt_delta 0.99 using iter_warmup = 2000 recommended.

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: workflow follows best practices transparent, reproducible meta-analysis. stages : Data Preparation — Clean structure data Trustworthiness Assessment (INSPECT-SR) — Evaluate study quality systematically Preliminary Exploration (PRIMED) — Understand data structure modeling Risk Bias Assessment (RoB) — Evaluate bias domains Model Specification — Define prior distributions model structure Model Fitting — Fit Bayesian models (standard, bias-corrected, selection, robust) Model Comparison — Compare competing models via LOSO-CV LOO-IC Model Diagnostics — Assess MCMC convergence posterior fit Heterogeneity Assessment — Evaluate -study variation Bias Assessment — Detect adjust publication/reporting bias Subgroup & Moderation Analysis — Explore effect modification (meta-regression) Sensitivity Analysis — Assess robustness modeling choices Interpretation & Reporting — Summarize findings communicate uncertainty

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: Prepare data frame one row per study (per arm multi-arm studies). exact columns depend likelihood: Add multi_arm column group arms within study: Run basic validation:
code: data <- tibble::tibble(   study = c("Author 2020", "Author 2021"),           # Study identifier   event_int = c(10, 15),                              # Events in intervention   event_ctrl = c(8, 12),                              # Events in control   n_int = c(100, 120),                                # Sample size, intervention   n_ctrl = c(100, 110)                                # Sample size, control ) data <- tibble::tibble(   study = c("Author 2020", "Author 2021"),   mean_int = c(5.2, 6.1),                             # Intervention mean   mean_ctrl = c(3.8, 4.5),                            # Control mean   sd_int = c(1.5, 1.8),                               # Intervention SD   sd_ctrl = c(1.4, 1.6),   n_int = c(100, 120),   n_ctrl = c(100, 110) ) data <- tibble::tibble(   study = c("Author 2020", "Author 2021"),   event_int = c(25, 30),                              # Count, intervention   event_ctrl = c(15, 18),                             # Count, control   n_int = c(100, 120),   n_ctrl = c(100, 110) ) data <- tibble::tibble(   study = rep(c("Author 2020", "Author 2020"), each = 2),  # Repeated study name   arm = c("Arm1", "Arm2", "Arm1", "Arm2"),   # ... outcome columns ...   multi_arm = study                                   # Column grouping arms ) # Check required columns bayesma::check_statistics_consistency(data, likelihood = "binomial") bayesma::check_n_consistency(data)  # Verify unique studies stopifnot(!duplicated(data$study))
