title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Assessing studies trustworthy enough include analysis. : Systematically evaluates RCTs across 4 domains using 21 items, including automated statistical checks (Carlisle, GRIM, N consistency, p-value verification). Create data frame study-level information trustworthiness judgements:
code: sr_data <- tibble::tibble(   study = c("Smith 2020", "Jones 2021"),   # D1: Post-publication notices (3 items)   d1_1 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),              # Retraction/EOC check   d1_2 = c("No concerns", "Some concerns"),            # Withdrawal check   d1_3 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),              # Corrigendum check      # D2: Conduct, governance, transparency (5 items)   d2_1 = c("No concerns", "Some concerns"),            # Trial registration   d2_2 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),              # Ethical approval   # ... d2_3, d2_4, d2_5 ...      # D3: Text & publication (2 items)   d3_1 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),   d3_2 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),      # D4: Results & statistics (11 items - some automated)   d4_1 = c("No concerns", "No concerns"),              # Outcomes clearly stated   # ... d4_2, d4_3 (automated), d4_4, ... d4_11 ...      # List-columns for automated checks   baseline = list(     tibble::tibble(variable = "age", group = "int", mean = 45.2, sd = 12.1),     tibble::tibble(variable = "age", group = "int", mean = 46.1, sd = 11.8)   ),      statistics = list(     tibble::tibble(test_name = "t-test", test_statistic = 2.34, df = 198),     tibble::tibble(test_name = "t-test", test_statistic = 2.10, df = 228)   ) ) inspect_results <- bayesma::inspect_sr(sr_data, studyvar = study)  # View summary print(inspect_results)  # View per-check details and failures bayesma::inspect_sr_table(inspect_results, only_failed = TRUE) # Plot trustworthiness across studies bayesma::inspect_plot(inspect_results)  # Summary across all studies bayesma::inspect_summary_plot(inspect_results) # Option 1: Exclude serious concerns meta_data <- bayesma::filter_trustworthy(   data,    inspect_results,   threshold = "some_concerns"  # Allow "No concerns" and "Some concerns" )  # Option 2: Keep all, flag in sensitivity analysis # ... proceed with all data, later check if results change without flagged studies

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: fitting model, explore data structure systematically. : Implements PRIMED workflow (Pustejovsky, Zhang, & Tipton) — describes dependence structure, explores moderators, inspects auxiliary data, visualizes effect size distribution (last, avoid outcome-driven decisions). primed() function produces: 1. Data Summary — Number studies, effect sizes, studies multiple effects, missing data 2. Dependence Structure — effect sizes nest within studies 3. Study Characteristics — Moderator frequencies distributions 4. Auxiliary Data Quality — Standard errors, outcome ranges, completeness 5. Effect Size Distribution — Visualizations (histogram, Q-Q, summary) prefer granular control:
code: primed_results <- bayesma::primed(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   moderators = c("intervention_type", "study_design"),  # Optional: variables to explore   es_col = "yi",                                        # If pre-calculated effects   se_col = "sei"                                        # If pre-calculated SEs )  print(primed_results) # Access via: primed_results$summary # Access via: primed_results$dependence_table  # E.g., "52 effects from 18 studies" primed_results$moderator_tables primed_results$auxiliary_inspection primed_results$es_distribution_plot # Dependence structure meta_data |>   dplyr::group_by(study) |>   dplyr::summarise(n_es = dplyr::n(), .groups = "drop") |>   dplyr::count(n_es) |>   dplyr::rename(n_effect_sizes_per_study = n_es, n_studies = n)  # Moderator frequencies meta_data |>   dplyr::count(intervention_type)  # SE ranges meta_data |>   dplyr::summarise(     se_min = min(sei, na.rm = TRUE),     se_mean = mean(sei, na.rm = TRUE),     se_max = max(sei, na.rm = TRUE)   )

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Deciding model fit. : Specifies prior distributions, likelihood, random effect structure, bias-handling mechanisms. Fit simple random-effects model: Fit multiple bias-handling models simultaneously: Document choices:
code: spec <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,              # For binomial   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",       # or "common_effect"   stage = "two_stage",                # or "one_stage"   re_dist = "normal",                 # or "t", "skew_normal", "mixture"      # Priors   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 1),   # Pooled effect prior   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 0.5)  # Heterogeneity prior )  # Inspect the specification print(spec) spec <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "bias_corrected",      # Systematic bias adjustment   stage = "two_stage",      # Bias prior   b_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 0.25)  # Prior on bias shift ) spec <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "selection_weight",    # or "selection_copas"   stage = "two_stage",   p_cutoffs = c(0.025, 0.05),        # P-value thresholds for weighting      # Selection priors   selection_priors = bayesma::prior_weight_function() ) spec <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   re_dist = "mixture",               # Two-component robust mixture   robust = TRUE,                     # Add outlier component   n_components = 2,   robust_weight = bayesma::beta(1, 9)  # Prior on outlier weight ) spec <- bayesma::meta_reg_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   moderators = ~ intervention_type + study_design,  # Formula of moderators      # Priors   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 1),   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 0.5),   # Beta priors for moderator coefficients (auto-generated)   rescale_priors = 1  # Scale to outcome SD ) spec <- bayesma::robma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   method = "bridge",                  # or "ss" (spike-slab)   bias_indicator = c("bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight"),   null_range = c(-0.1, 0.1)          # Define practically equivalent effect ) # Visualize priors vs. possible effect sizes bayesma::bayesma_prior_density(   spec,   plot_range = c(-2, 2) )  # Compare to published guidelines bayesma::build_mu_prior_overlay(likelihood = "binomial")

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: specification, fit model data. : Compiles Stan code, samples posterior, extracts results. control, use individual stages:
code: # One-call pipeline fit <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   stage = "two_stage",      # MCMC settings   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   seed = 1234,      # Optional: inspect intermediate stages   return_stage = "full"  # or "spec", "code", "data", "fit" )  # Summary print(fit) # Stage 1: Specification spec <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(...)  # Stage 2: Generate Stan code code <- bayesma::bayesma_stan_code(spec) print(code)  # Inspect if needed  # Stage 3: Build Stan data stan_data <- bayesma::bayesma_stan_data(spec)  # Stage 4: Compile and sample fit <- bayesma::bayesma_fit(stan_data, code, chains = 4, ...)  # Stage 5: Extract results effects <- bayesma::bayesma_extract(fit)  # Stage 6: Build output object output <- bayesma::bayesma_output(fit, spec, effects) # One call mreg_fit <- bayesma::meta_reg(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   moderators = ~ intervention_type + study_design,   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000 )  # Or modular mreg_spec <- bayesma::meta_reg_spec(...) mreg_code <- bayesma::meta_reg_stan_code(mreg_spec) mreg_data <- bayesma::meta_reg_stan_data(mreg_spec) mreg_fit <- bayesma::meta_reg_fit(mreg_data, mreg_code, chains = 4, ...) mreg_effects <- bayesma::meta_reg_extract(mreg_fit) mreg_output <- bayesma::meta_reg_output(mreg_fit, mreg_spec, mreg_effects) robma_fit <- bayesma::robma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   method = "bridge",   bias_indicator = c("bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight"),   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000 )  print(robma_fit) egger_fit <- bayesma::egger(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000 )  # Plot Egger regression bayesma::egger_plot(egger_fit)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: # One-call pipeline fit <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   stage = "two_stage",      # MCMC settings   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   seed = 1234,      # Optional: inspect intermediate stages   return_stage = "full"  # or "spec", "code", "data", "fit" )  # Summary print(fit)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Deciding competing models. : Compares models using leave-one-study-cross-validation (LOSO-CV) across-stage comparison, LOO-IC within-stage comparison. LOSO-CRPS: Lower better. Averaged proper scoring rule. LOSO-ELPD: Higher better. Expected log predictive density. Calibration: Empirical coverage ≈ nominal (e.g., 95% CI contain true value 95% time). Miscalibration: Mean |empirical − nominal| across coverage levels. Zero perfect.
code: fit_re <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for random-effects model   model_type = "random_effect" )  fit_ce <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for common-effect model   model_type = "common_effect" )  fit_robust <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for robust mixture model   re_dist = "mixture",   robust = TRUE )  # Compare via LOSO-CV comparison <- bayesma::compare_models(   "Random-effects" = fit_re,   "Common-effect" = fit_ce,   "Robust" = fit_robust,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   criterion = "loso",  # or "loo"   quiet = FALSE )  print(comparison) # LOSO-CRPS (continuous ranked probability score) comparison bayesma::compare_plot(comparison, type = "loso_crps")  # Calibration plot (are prediction intervals accurate?) bayesma::compare_plot(comparison, type = "calibration")  # Summary table bayesma::compare_table(comparison)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: fit_re <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for random-effects model   model_type = "random_effect" )  fit_ce <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for common-effect model   model_type = "common_effect" )  fit_robust <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... specification for robust mixture model   re_dist = "mixture",   robust = TRUE )  # Compare via LOSO-CV comparison <- bayesma::compare_models(   "Random-effects" = fit_re,   "Common-effect" = fit_ce,   "Robust" = fit_robust,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   criterion = "loso",  # or "loo"   quiet = FALSE )  print(comparison)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: # LOSO-CRPS (continuous ranked probability score) comparison bayesma::compare_plot(comparison, type = "loso_crps")  # Calibration plot (are prediction intervals accurate?) bayesma::compare_plot(comparison, type = "calibration")  # Summary table bayesma::compare_table(comparison)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: LOSO-CRPS: Lower better. Averaged proper scoring rule. LOSO-ELPD: Higher better. Expected log predictive density. Calibration: Empirical coverage ≈ nominal (e.g., 95% CI contain true value 95% time). Miscalibration: Mean |empirical − nominal| across coverage levels. Zero perfect.

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: fitting model — proceeding comparison, bias assessment, inference. : Evaluates MCMC convergence (trace plots, Rhat, ESS, autocorrelation) model fit (posterior predictive check). Poor convergence invalidates downstream inference; poor predictive check suggests model misrepresents data. diagnostics() produces single patchwork panel combining key diagnostics: page contains six panels: Trace plots — chains mix freely around stable centre Posterior predictive check — replicated data resemble observed data Rhat — parameters < 1.01 Effective sample size (bulk) — > 400 per parameter Autocorrelation — decay zero within 10–20 lags MCMC diagnostics table — divergences, max treedepth, E-BFMI prior predictive check (verify priors sensible seeing data):
code: bayesma::diagnostics(fit_re) # Default: density overlay bayesma::pp_check(fit_re)  # Alternative plot types bayesma::pp_check(fit_re, type = "ecdf_overlay") bayesma::pp_check(fit_re, type = "stat", stat = "mean") bayesma::pp_check(fit_re, type = "stat", stat = "sd") fit_prior <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns and model spec ...   sample_prior = TRUE   # Sample from prior, not posterior )  # Visualize prior predictions vs. plausible data range bayesma::pp_check(fit_prior) # Re-run with stricter sampler settings if diagnostics flag issues fit_re <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   # ...   adapt_delta   = 0.99,   max_treedepth = 14 )  bayesma::diagnostics(fit_re)

code: bayesma::diagnostics(fit_re)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Understanding variation studies. : Estimates visualizes -study variance (τ²) intra-class correlation. expect new study:
code:
# From standard meta-analysis tau_summary <- fit |>   bayesma::extract_summary() |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "tau")  print(tau_summary) # Shows: median, mad, quantiles (2.5%, 97.5%)  # Interpret cat("Posterior median τ:", tau_summary$median, "
") cat("95% CrI for τ:", tau_summary$q2.5, "to", tau_summary$q97.5, "
") # Prior-posterior plot for tau bayesma::build_tau_prior_overlay(fit)  # Forest plot (includes tau visually) bayesma::bayes_forest(fit, data = meta_data) # Extract predictive draws for a new study pred_draws <- fit$draws |>   posterior::subset_draws(variable = "mu_new") |>   as.numeric()  # Summarize prediction cat("Posterior predictive median:", median(pred_draws), "
") cat("95% prediction interval:",      quantile(pred_draws, c(0.025, 0.975)), "
")

code: # Extract predictive draws for a new study pred_draws <- fit$draws |>   posterior::subset_draws(variable = "mu_new") |>   as.numeric()  # Summarize prediction cat("Posterior predictive median:", median(pred_draws), "\n") cat("95% prediction interval:",      quantile(pred_draws, c(0.025, 0.975)), "\n")

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Evaluating whether publication bias, small-study effects, systematic bias affect results. : Fits models bias-handling mechanisms compares results unbiased model. Fit multiple bias models simultaneously average: Visual test publication bias:
code: # Fit selection model fit_selection <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "selection_weight",   p_cutoffs = c(0.025, 0.05),   chains = 4 )  # Compare to unbiased model comparison_bias <- bayesma::compare_models(   "No bias" = fit_re,   "Selection bias" = fit_selection,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  print(comparison_bias) # PET-PEESE regression fit_pet_peese <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "pet_peese",   chains = 4 )  # Plot regression line bayesma::overall_plot(fit_pet_peese, data = meta_data) # Bias-corrected model: assumes some studies have systematic bias fit_bias_corrected <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "bias_corrected",   b_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 0.25),   chains = 4 )  # Compare comparison_bias2 <- bayesma::compare_models(   "No bias" = fit_re,   "Bias-corrected" = fit_bias_corrected,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study ) robma_fit <- bayesma::robma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   method = "bridge",   bias_indicator = c("none", "bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight"),   chains = 4 )  print(robma_fit) # Shows posterior model probabilities + averaged effect estimate fit_egger <- bayesma::egger(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   chains = 4 )  # Plot with credible bands bayesma::egger_plot(fit_egger, data = meta_data)  # Extract slope (publication bias indicator) slope_summary <- fit_egger |>   bayesma::extract_summary() |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "beta_egger")  print(slope_summary) # Standard funnel plot bayesma::funnel_plot(fit_re, data = meta_data)  # With publication bias correction bayesma::funnel_plot(fit_selection, data = meta_data)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Exploring effect modification (e.g., efficacy vary age group, intervention type, study quality?) : Fits meta-regression models study-level moderators. continuous moderator (e.g., study year), plot regression surface: categorical moderators (e.g., intervention type), compare subgroups:
code: spec <- bayesma::meta_reg_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",      # Moderator formula (like lm/glm)   moderators = ~ intervention_type + study_duration,      # Priors   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 1),   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 0.5),   # Beta priors auto-scaled to outcome variance   rescale_priors = 1 )  print(spec) mreg_fit <- bayesma::meta_reg(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   moderators = ~ intervention_type + study_duration,   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000 )  print(mreg_fit) # Extract moderator coefficients mreg_effects <- bayesma::extract_summary(mreg_fit) |>   dplyr::filter(grepl("beta", variable))  print(mreg_effects)  # Visualize: predicted effects across moderator values bayesma::metareg_mod_plot(   mreg_fit,   data = meta_data,   moderator = "intervention_type" )  # Or custom prediction plot bayesma::sensitivity_plot(   mreg_fit,   type = "moderator",   moderator = "study_duration" ) # Data with new study years to predict new_years <- tibble::tibble(study_year = seq(2010, 2025, by = 1))  # Predicted effects pred <- bayesma::sensitivity_plot(   mreg_fit,   type = "moderator",   moderator = "study_year",   newdata = new_years )  print(pred) # Summarize effects by subgroup subgroup_summary <- mreg_fit$summary |>   dplyr::filter(grepl("beta_intervention", variable)) |>   dplyr::mutate(     subgroup = gsub("beta_intervention_type\\[(.*)\\]", "\\1", variable)   )  print(subgroup_summary)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: spec <- bayesma::meta_reg_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",      # Moderator formula (like lm/glm)   moderators = ~ intervention_type + study_duration,      # Priors   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 1),   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 0.5),   # Beta priors auto-scaled to outcome variance   rescale_priors = 1 )  print(spec)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: # Extract moderator coefficients mreg_effects <- bayesma::extract_summary(mreg_fit) |>   dplyr::filter(grepl("beta", variable))  print(mreg_effects)  # Visualize: predicted effects across moderator values bayesma::metareg_mod_plot(   mreg_fit,   data = meta_data,   moderator = "intervention_type" )  # Or custom prediction plot bayesma::sensitivity_plot(   mreg_fit,   type = "moderator",   moderator = "study_duration" )

code: # Data with new study years to predict new_years <- tibble::tibble(study_year = seq(2010, 2025, by = 1))  # Predicted effects pred <- bayesma::sensitivity_plot(   mreg_fit,   type = "moderator",   moderator = "study_year",   newdata = new_years )  print(pred)

text: categorical moderators (e.g., intervention type), compare subgroups:
code: # Summarize effects by subgroup subgroup_summary <- mreg_fit$summary |>   dplyr::filter(grepl("beta_intervention", variable)) |>   dplyr::mutate(     subgroup = gsub("beta_intervention_type\\[(.*)\\]", "\\1", variable)   )  print(subgroup_summary)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Assessing robustness modeling assumptions data decisions. : Refits models different assumptions summarizes results change. posterior depend prior? conclusion hold excluding studies? Automated sensitivity across multiple bias assumptions: stage analysis matter?
code: # Fit with weak prior spec_weak <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 2),        # Wider prior   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 1)   # Wider prior ) fit_weak <- bayesma::bayesma_fit(..., spec = spec_weak)  # Fit with strong prior spec_strong <- bayesma::bayesma_spec(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   mu_prior = bayesma::normal(0, 0.5),      # Narrower prior   tau_prior = bayesma::half_normal(0, 0.25) ) fit_strong <- bayesma::bayesma_fit(..., spec = spec_strong)  # Compare posteriors comparison_prior <- bayesma::compare_models(   "Weak prior" = fit_weak,   "Default prior" = fit_re,   "Strong prior" = fit_strong,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  print(comparison_prior) # Remove outliers or studies with concerns meta_subset <- meta_data |>   dplyr::filter(!study %in% c("Outlier 2020", "Flagged 2021"))  fit_subset <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_subset,   studyvar = study,   # ... same specification as fit_re ... )  comparison_subset <- bayesma::compare_models(   "All studies" = fit_re,   "Excluding outliers" = fit_subset,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study ) # RoBMA includes implicit sensitivity via model averaging robma_fit <- bayesma::robma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   method = "bridge",   bias_indicator = c("none", "bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight") )  print(robma_fit) # Posterior model probabilities show sensitivity to bias model  # Visualize effect estimate under each model bayesma::robma_table(robma_fit) fit_1s <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   stage = "one_stage",            # Full likelihood on raw data   chains = 4 )  fit_2s <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   stage = "two_stage",            # Summary effect estimates   chains = 4 )  comparison_stage <- bayesma::compare_models(   "One-stage" = fit_1s,   "Two-stage" = fit_2s,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  print(comparison_stage) fit_normal <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   re_dist = "normal"   # Symmetric )  fit_t <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   re_dist = "t"        # Heavy-tailed, robust to outliers )  fit_skew <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   re_dist = "skew_normal"  # Asymmetric )  comparison_re <- bayesma::compare_models(   "Normal" = fit_normal,   "t-distribution" = fit_t,   "Skew-normal" = fit_skew,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  print(comparison_re) # Create sensitivity plot object sens_plot <- bayesma::sensitivity_plot(   fit_re,   data = meta_data,   # Include multiple sensitivity scenarios   include_null_range = TRUE,   include_prior_scales = c(0.5, 1, 2) )  # Render with custom titles sens_rendered <- bayesma::render_sensitivity_patchwork(   sens_plot,   title = "Sensitivity to Modeling Choices" )  print(sens_rendered) # RoBMA automatically averages over multiple bias assumptions robma_fit <- bayesma::robma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   # ... outcome columns ...   method = "bridge",   bias_indicator = c("none", "bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight") )  # Posterior model probabilities show which assumptions drive conclusions robma_summary <- print(robma_fit) # Inspect: "Model posteriors" section  # Forest plot showing weighted effect (averaged over models) bayesma::bayes_forest(robma_fit, data = meta_data) # Visualize robustness: how does effect estimate change with assumption? robma_sensitivity <- bayesma::robma_sensitivity(   robma_fit,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  # Default is to show effect estimate under each bias assumption print(robma_sensitivity)  # Plot bayesma::render_sensitivity_patchwork(robma_sensitivity)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: use: Summarizing findings publication stakeholders. : Extracts formats results, confidence intervals, evidential summaries. Ensure report: Study Selection — Search strategy, inclusion/exclusion, PRISMA diagram Study Characteristics — Table included studies (N, design, outcome, baseline characteristics) Risk Bias — RoB plot + summary, INSPECT-SR results used Preliminary Exploration — PRIMED tables + visualizations Main Result — Pooled effect (point estimate + 95% CrI), τ (heterogeneity) Model Justification — random-effects? likelihood? Prior specification. Model Comparison — LOSO-CV LOO-IC results comparing main models Model Diagnostics — Rhat < 1.01, ESS > 400, zero divergences, posterior predictive check Bias Assessment — Selection models, PET-PEESE, egger plots, robustness model choice Subgroup Analysis — Meta-regression results (applicable) Sensitivity Analysis — Prior sensitivity, studies-excluded sensitivity, random-effects distribution sensitivity Interpretation — Effect magnitude, uncertainty, implications Reproducibility — R code, seed, data availability statement
code:
# Get all summary statistics summary_df <- fit_re |>   bayesma::extract_summary()  # Pooled effect estimate mu_summary <- summary_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "mu")  cat("Posterior estimate of pooled effect:
") cat("  Median (95% CrI):",      round(mu_summary$median, 3),      "(", round(mu_summary$q2.5, 3), " to ", round(mu_summary$q97.5, 3), ")
")  # Heterogeneity (τ) tau_summary <- summary_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "tau")  cat("
Between-study standard deviation:
") cat("  Median (95% CrI):",      round(tau_summary$median, 3),      "(", round(tau_summary$q2.5, 3), " to ", round(tau_summary$q97.5, 3), ")
") # P(θ > 0) — probability effect is positive theta_draws <- fit_re$draws |>   posterior::subset_draws(variable = "mu") |>   as.numeric()  prob_positive <- mean(theta_draws > 0) cat("P(effect > 0):", round(prob_positive, 3), "
")  # P(effect in null range) — probability of negligible effect null_range <- c(-0.1, 0.1) prob_null <- mean(theta_draws >= null_range[1] & theta_draws <= null_range[2]) cat("P(effect in [−0.1, 0.1]):", round(prob_null, 3), "
") # Bayes factor: null (θ = 0) vs. alternative bf_null <- bayesma::coefficient_evidence(fit_re) print(bf_null) # Shows BF10 and BF01  # Interpretation if (bf_null$bf10 > 10) {   cat("Strong evidence for non-zero effect
") } else if (bf_null$bf10 < 0.1) {   cat("Strong evidence for zero effect
") } else {   cat("Inconclusive evidence
") } # Posterior model probabilities robma_summary <- print(robma_fit)  # Shows: "No bias", "Bias-corrected", "PET-PEESE", "Selection model" # E.g., 60% weight to "Selection model" means that model best describes data  # Weighted effect estimate across models effect_weighted <- robma_fit |>   bayesma::extract_summary() |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "mu")  cat("Model-averaged effect estimate:
") cat("  Median (95% CrI):",      round(effect_weighted$median, 3),      "(", round(effect_weighted$q2.5, 3), " to ", round(effect_weighted$q97.5, 3), ")
") # Forest plot table forest_table <- bayesma::bayes_forest(fit_re, data = meta_data) print(forest_table)  # Model comparison table comparison_table <- bayesma::compare_table(comparison) print(comparison_table)  # RoBMA table (per-model results) robma_table <- bayesma::robma_table(robma_fit) print(robma_table)  # Sensitivity analysis table (if using ROBMA) robma_sens <- bayesma::robma_sensitivity(robma_fit, data = meta_data, studyvar = study) sensitivity_table <- bayesma::robma_table(robma_sens) print(sensitivity_table)

") cat("  Median (95% CrI):",      round(mu_summary$median, 3),      "(", round(mu_summary$q2.5, 3), " to ", round(mu_summary$q97.5, 3), ")

Between-study standard deviation:

")

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code: # P(θ > 0) — probability effect is positive theta_draws <- fit_re$draws |>   posterior::subset_draws(variable = "mu") |>   as.numeric()  prob_positive <- mean(theta_draws > 0) cat("P(effect > 0):", round(prob_positive, 3), "\n")  # P(effect in null range) — probability of negligible effect null_range <- c(-0.1, 0.1) prob_null <- mean(theta_draws >= null_range[1] & theta_draws <= null_range[2]) cat("P(effect in [−0.1, 0.1]):", round(prob_null, 3), "\n")

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
code:
# Bayes factor: null (θ = 0) vs. alternative bf_null <- bayesma::coefficient_evidence(fit_re) print(bf_null) # Shows BF10 and BF01  # Interpretation if (bf_null$bf10 > 10) {   cat("Strong evidence for non-zero effect
") } else if (bf_null$bf10 < 0.1) {   cat("Strong evidence for zero effect
") } else {   cat("Inconclusive evidence
") }

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: Ensure report: Study Selection — Search strategy, inclusion/exclusion, PRISMA diagram Study Characteristics — Table included studies (N, design, outcome, baseline characteristics) Risk Bias — RoB plot + summary, INSPECT-SR results used Preliminary Exploration — PRIMED tables + visualizations Main Result — Pooled effect (point estimate + 95% CrI), τ (heterogeneity) Model Justification — random-effects? likelihood? Prior specification. Model Comparison — LOSO-CV LOO-IC results comparing main models Model Diagnostics — Rhat < 1.01, ESS > 400, zero divergences, posterior predictive check Bias Assessment — Selection models, PET-PEESE, egger plots, robustness model choice Subgroup Analysis — Meta-regression results (applicable) Sensitivity Analysis — Prior sensitivity, studies-excluded sensitivity, random-effects distribution sensitivity Interpretation — Effect magnitude, uncertainty, implications Reproducibility — R code, seed, data availability statement

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: condensed example binary outcome:
code: # Load data data(dat.smith2021, package = "bayesma") meta_data <- dat.smith2021  # Stage 1: Trustworthiness check (abbreviated) sr_results <- bayesma::inspect_sr(meta_data, studyvar = study) meta_data <- bayesma::filter_trustworthy(meta_data, sr_results)  # Stage 2: Preliminary exploration primed_results <- bayesma::primed(meta_data, studyvar = study) print(primed_results)  # Stage 3-4: RoB (external; use your own rob_data) # bayesma::rob_plot(rob_data)  # Stage 5-6: Fit main model fit_re <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "random_effect",   stage = "two_stage",   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000 )  print(fit_re)  # Fit bias-adjusted model fit_selection <- bayesma::bayesma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   model_type = "selection_weight",   stage = "two_stage",   chains = 4 )  # Stage 7: Compare models comparison <- bayesma::compare_models(   "Random-effects" = fit_re,   "Selection model" = fit_selection,   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study )  print(comparison)  # Stage 8: Model diagnostics bayesma::diagnostics(fit_re) bayesma::pp_check(fit_re)  # Stage 9: Heterogeneity tau <- bayesma::extract_summary(fit_re) |>   dplyr::filter(variable == "tau") print(tau)  # Stage 10: Bias assessment bayesma::egger_plot(fit_selection, data = meta_data) bayesma::funnel_plot(fit_re, data = meta_data)  # Stage 11: Meta-regression (if moderators available) mreg_fit <- bayesma::meta_reg(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   moderators = ~ study_year + study_design,   chains = 4 )  # Stage 12: Sensitivity via ROBMA robma_fit <- bayesma::robma(   data = meta_data,   studyvar = study,   event_int = event_int,   event_ctrl = event_ctrl,   n_int = n_int,   n_ctrl = n_ctrl,   likelihood = "binomial",   method = "bridge",   bias_indicator = c("none", "bias_corrected", "pet_peese", "selection_weight"),   chains = 4 )  print(robma_fit)  # Stage 13: Results & reporting summary_main <- bayesma::extract_summary(fit_re) |>   dplyr::filter(variable %in% c("mu", "tau"))  print(summary_main)  # Forest plot bayesma::bayes_forest(fit_re, data = meta_data)  # Model-averaged forest plot bayesma::bayes_forest(robma_fit, data = meta_data)

title: Bayesian meta-analysis workflow with bayesma
text: PRIMED: Pustejovsky & Tipton (2024). “Preliminary Investigation Meta-analytic Databases” INSPECT-SR: Wilkinson et al. (2025). “INSPECT-SR: Trustworthiness RCTs” Bayesian Meta-Analysis: Röver, Knapp, & Friede (2021). “Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman approach” RoBMA: Bartoš & Maier (2023). “Robust Bayesian Model Averaging” Stan Meta-Analysis: McElreath (2020). “Statistical Rethinking” workflow document accompanies bayesma R package. latest function documentation, see ?bayesma, ?primed, ?inspect_sr, related help pages.

text: Rather one monolithic generate_stan_model() many arguments, ’d suggest block-builder pattern — ’re already partway helpers like emit_prior_target() emit_robust_likelihood(). idea : keeps block independently testable debuggable eliminating duplication.
code: # Each block has its own builder build_data_block(stage, likelihood, use_t_likelihood, use_multi_arm, ...) build_parameters_block(model_type, re_dist, use_robust, ...) build_transformed_parameters_block(...) build_model_block(...) build_generated_quantities_block(...)  # Then a thin orchestrator generate_stan_model <- function(stage, model_type, likelihood, re_dist, ...) {   parts <- c(     build_functions_block(re_dist),     build_data_block(stage, likelihood, ...),     build_parameters_block(model_type, re_dist, ...),     build_transformed_parameters_block(stage, model_type, ...),     build_model_block(stage, likelihood, model_type, ...),     build_generated_quantities_block(stage, model_type, ...)   )   paste(parts[nzchar(parts)], collapse = "\n\n") }

text: ’s wrong — ’s defensible choice. Stanley & Doucouliagos (2014, 2017) argue 1/sqrt(n) variance-stabilised predictor PET test, 1/n PEESE, grounds SE noisy estimate (especially small samples) using weight predictor creates mechanical correlation. Using sample size avoids endogeneity problem. said, literature isn’t fully settled . original PET-PEESE formulation (Stanley & Doucouliagos, 2012) used SE SE^2, many implementations still (e.g., metafor::regtest, RoBMA package). sample-size-based version assumes fairly direct relationship 1/sqrt(n) SE, holds cleanly standardised mean differences approximate log odds ratios log risk ratios SE depends event rates well sample size. Since package supports binomial Poisson likelihoods alongside Gaussian, might worth noting assumption documentation — 1/sqrt(n) predictor directly motivated continuous outcomes. robma context component models averaged, approximation likely fine. Short answer: ’re solid ground citing Stanley (2017). Just transparent choice.

text: Pustejovsky’s 2017 blog post comparing traditional PET-PEESE (using SE SE²) vs sample-size variants (SPET/SPEESE using 1/√n 1/n). key findings support bayesma : Traditional PET/PEESE using SE/SE² wildly incorrect Type-error rates d V mechanically correlated Sample-size variants (SPET/SPEESE using 1/√n 1/n) maintain correct Type-error rates SPET/SPEESE exactly unbiased absence publication bias publication bias, SPEESE emerges recommended default even though single estimator consistently outperforms others actually reinforces choice. Pustejovsky’s simulations show exactly ’d want sample-size variants: Traditional PET/PEESE (using SE SE²) badly inflated Type-error rates even publication bias, d V mechanically correlated — need d compute V SPET/SPEESE (using 1/sqrt(n) 1/n) maintain correct Type-error rates exactly unbiased ’s publication bias actual publication bias, neither variant consistently dominates, Pustejovsky’s “gun head” recommendation SPEESE (1/n) implementation aligns Stanley (2017) Pustejovsky’s independent simulation evidence. one thing worth noting post observation 1/sqrt(n) predictor specifically motivated standardized mean differences two-group designs — circles back caveat mentioned log ORs log RRs, SE--sample-size relationship complex. within robma model-averaging framework, component models weighted evidence, reasonable approximation. anything, might consider documenting Stanley (2017) Pustejovsky (2017) references PET-PEESE function documentation explain choice.

title: Bayesian Meta Analyses in R
text: bayesma R package conducting Bayesian meta-analyses using Stan. provides complete, opinionated workflow data preparation model fitting, publication bias correction, heterogeneity assessment, reporting — sensible defaults full prior control every stage.

title: Bayesian Meta Analyses in R
text: new Bayesian Meta Analysis, recommended read workflow vignette vignettes addition resources: Bayesian Meta-Analysis: Practical Introduction bayesmeta package vignettes Matti Vuorre’s Blog: Meta-analysis special case Bayesian multilevel modeling Solomon Kurz’s blog- Bayesian meta-analysis brms: Part Part II Meta Analysis R- Chapter 13: Bayesian Meta-Analysis

title: Bayesian Meta Analyses in R
text: Install development version GitHub: bayesma uses cmdstanr Stan backend. already : C++ toolchain required: Rtools Windows, Xcode command line tools macOS (xcode-select --install).
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("BLMoran/bayesma") install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c("https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev", getOption("repos"))) cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()

title: Bayesian Meta Analyses in R
text: bayesma covers full Bayesian meta-analysis workflow: full walkthrough stage available workflow vignette.

title: Bayesian Meta Analyses in R
code: one_stage_re <- bayesma(   data       = binary_outcome,   studyvar   = "Author",   event_ctrl = "Event_Control",   event_int  = "Event_Intervention",   n_ctrl     = "N_Control",   n_int      = "N_Intervention",   likelihood = "binomial",   estimand   = "OR",   model_type = "random_effect",   stage      = "one_stage",   mu_prior   = normal(0, 1),   tau_prior  = half_cauchy(0, 0.5) )

what: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test
title: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test
text: Computes Bayes factor comparing genuine randomisation (uniform p-values) fabrication (non-uniform).

title: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test
code: bayes_carlisle_test(p_values, prior_a = 1, prior_b = 1, n_grid = 200)

title: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test
text: p_values Numeric vector. P-values baseline comparisons. prior_a, prior_b Numeric. Beta prior shape parameters (default 1, 1). n_grid Integer. Grid size numerical integration (default 200).

title: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test
text: list components: bf_too_similar BF p-values biased towards 1. bf_too_different BF p-values biased towards 0. bf_nonuniform Overall BF non-uniformity. posterior_prob_fabrication Posterior probability. posterior_mean_p Mean observed p-values. n_comparisons Number comparisons. interpretation Evidence description.

title: Bayesian Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — bayes_carlisle_test

what: Create Bayesian Forest Plot for Meta-Analysis — bayes_forest
title: Create Bayesian Forest Plot for Meta-Analysis — bayes_forest
text: function creates Bayesian forest plot meta-analysis. supports brmsfit objects (fitted via brms) bayesma objects (fitted via bayesma). model class detected automatically.

title: Create Bayesian Forest Plot for Meta-Analysis — bayes_forest
code: bayes_forest(   model,   data,   measure,   studyvar = NULL,   year = NULL,   c_n = NULL,   i_n = NULL,   c_event = NULL,   i_event = NULL,   c_mean = NULL,   i_mean = NULL,   c_sd = NULL,   i_sd = NULL,   c_time = NULL,   i_time = NULL,   sort_studies_by = "author",   subgroup = FALSE,   subgroup_var = NULL,   sort_subgroup_by = "alphabetical",   label_outcome = "Outcome",   label_control = "Control",   label_intervention = "Intervention",   title = NULL,   subtitle = NULL,   title_align = "left",   xlim = NULL,   x_breaks = NULL,   add_rope = FALSE,   rope_value = NULL,   rope_color = "grey50",   shrinkage_output = "density",   null_value = NULL,   color_palette = NULL,   color_study_posterior_null_left = "deepskyblue",   color_study_posterior_null_right = "violet",   color_study_posterior = "dodgerblue",   color_study_posterior_outline = "blue",   color_overall_posterior = "blue",   color_shrinkage_pointinterval = "purple",   color_shrinkage_outline = "purple",   color_shrinkage_fill = NULL,   split_color_by_null = FALSE,   color_favours_control = "firebrick",   color_favours_intervention = "dodgerblue",   add_arm_labels = TRUE,   reverse_arm_labels = FALSE,   add_pred = FALSE,   add_pred_subgroup = FALSE,   pred_output = c("density", "pointinterval"),   color_pred_posterior = "forestgreen",   color_pred_outline = "darkgreen",   color_pred_pointinterval = "forestgreen",   plot_width = 4,   add_rob = FALSE,   rob_tool = c("rob2", "robins_i", "quadas2", "robins_e"),   add_rob_legend = FALSE,   exclude_high_rob = FALSE,   font = NULL )

title: Create Bayesian Forest Plot for Meta-Analysis — bayes_forest
text: model fitted model object. Either brmsfit object (class 'brmsfit') bayesma object (class 'bayesma'). data data frame containing study data used meta-analysis. measure Character string specifying effect measure. Must one : "" (Odds Ratio), "HR" (Hazard Ratio), "RR" (Risk Ratio), "IRR" (Incidence Rate Ratio), "MD" (Mean Difference), "SMD" (Standardized Mean Difference). studyvar Column name containing study identifiers/authors. Default NULL. Required brmsfit models. bayesma models, extracted model object provided. year Column name containing publication years. Default NULL. c_n Column name containing control group sample sizes. Required , RR, MD, SMD. i_n Column name containing intervention group sample sizes. Required , RR, MD, SMD. c_event Column name containing control group event counts. Required , RR, IRR. i_event Column name containing intervention group event counts. Required , RR, IRR. c_mean Column name containing control group means. Required MD, SMD. i_mean Column name containing intervention group means. Required MD, SMD. c_sd Column name containing control group standard deviations. Required MD, SMD. i_sd Column name containing intervention group standard deviations. Required MD, SMD. c_time Column name containing control group time periods. Required IRR. i_time Column name containing intervention group time periods. Required IRR. sort_studies_by Character string specifying sort studies. Options: "author" (default), "year", "effect". subgroup Logical indicating whether create subgroup analysis. Default FALSE. subgroup_var Character string. Name variable data use subgroup analysis. sort_subgroup_by Character string vector specifying subgroup ordering. Options: "alphabetical" (default), "effect", custom character vector subgroup names. label_outcome Character string outcome label. Default "Outcome". label_control Character string control group label. Default "Control". label_intervention Character string intervention group label. Default "Intervention". title Character string plot title. Default NULL (title). subtitle Character string plot subtitle. Default NULL (subtitle). title_align Character string specifying title alignment. Options: "left" (default), "center"/"centre", "right". xlim Numeric vector length 2 specifying x-axis limits. Default NULL (auto-scaled). x_breaks Numeric vector specifying custom x-axis break points. Default NULL (uses measure-specific defaults). add_rope Logical indicating whether add ROPE (Region Practical Equivalence). Default FALSE. rope_value Numeric vector length 2 specifying ROPE range, single value symmetric range around null. Default NULL (uses Kruschke's recommendations: /HR/RR/IRR = c(0.9, 1.1), SMD = c(-0.1, 0.1), MD requires specification). rope_color Color ROPE shading. Default transparent grey. shrinkage_output Character string specifying shrinkage visualization. Options: "density" (default) "pointinterval". null_value Numeric value specifying x-axis value null line. Default NULL (uses measure-specific defaults). color_palette Character vector colors plot. Default "violet". color_study_posterior Color study posterior densities. Default "dodgerblue". color_study_posterior_outline Color study posterior outlines. Default "blue". color_overall_posterior Color overall posterior. Default "blue". color_shrinkage_pointinterval Color shrinkage point intervals (used shrinkage_output = "pointinterval"). Default "purple". color_shrinkage_outline Color shrinkage plot outlines. Default "purple". color_shrinkage_fill Color shrinkage plot fill. Default NULL. split_color_by_null Logical. TRUE, posterior densities split coloured based whether values fall null value. color_favours_control Colour used density regions favouring control group split_color_by_null = TRUE. color_favours_intervention Colour used density regions favouring intervention group split_color_by_null = TRUE. add_arm_labels Logical indicating whether display "Favours Control" / "Favours Intervention" labels density plot. Default TRUE. reverse_arm_labels Logical indicating whether swap positions "Favours Control" "Favours Intervention" labels. Default FALSE. add_pred_subgroup Logical indicating whether add prediction interval rows subgroup subgroup = TRUE. FALSE (default), prediction row added overall pooled effect. Ignored subgroup = FALSE. pred_output Character string specifying visualisation prediction interval row. title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
text: Computes Bayes factor quantifying evidence reported mean integer-scale data inconsistent reported sample size, accounting rounding uncertainty.

title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
code: bayes_grim_test(   mean_value,   n,   decimals = NULL,   max_items = 10,   n_tolerance = 0 )

title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
text: mean_value Numeric. reported mean. n Integer. sample size. decimals Integer. Number decimal places reported mean. NULL (default), inferred reported value. max_items Integer. Maximum plausible value integer scale (default 10). Used define range possible integer sums. n_tolerance Integer. many values around reported N consider plausible (default 0, exact N ).

title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
text: list components: bf_inconsistent Numeric. Bayes factor favour inconsistency (fabrication). posterior_prob_inconsistent Numeric. Posterior probability inconsistency assuming equal prior odds. consistent_at_n Logical. Classical GRIM result exact N. consistent_nearby Logical. GRIM-consistent nearby N. interpretation Character. Evidence strength label.

title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
text: H0 (genuine data), mean must equal k/n integer k, rounded reported decimal places. H1 (fabricated data), mean drawn uniformly plausible range. BF_10 = P(data | H1) / P(data | H0).

title: Bayesian GRIM Test — bayes_grim_test
code: # Consistent mean: evidence for genuine data bayes_grim_test(2.60, n = 20) #> $bf_inconsistent #> [1] 1.005 #>  #> $posterior_prob_inconsistent #> [1] 0.5012469 #>  #> $consistent_at_n #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $consistent_nearby #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $interpretation #> [1] "weak_evidence" #>  #> $mean_value #> [1] 2.6 #>  #> $n #> [1] 20 #>  #> $decimals #> [1] 1 #>   # Inconsistent mean: evidence for fabrication bayes_grim_test(2.47, n = 20) #> $bf_inconsistent #> [1] Inf #>  #> $posterior_prob_inconsistent #> [1] 1 #>  #> $consistent_at_n #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $consistent_nearby #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $interpretation #> [1] "strong_inconsistency" #>  #> $mean_value #> [1] 2.47 #>  #> $n #> [1] 20 #>  #> $decimals #> [1] 2 #>

code: bayesma(   data,   studyvar,   event_ctrl = NULL,   event_int = NULL,   mean_ctrl = NULL,   mean_int = NULL,   sd_ctrl = NULL,   sd_int = NULL,   n_ctrl = NULL,   n_int = NULL,   likelihood = c("binomial", "gaussian", "poisson"),   model_type = c("random_effect", "common_effect", "bias_corrected", "bc_bnp",     "selection_copas", "selection_weight", "pet_peese", "mixture_model"),   stage = c("one_stage", "two_stage"),   re_dist = c("normal", "t", "skew_normal", "mixture"),   small_sample = c("none", "t_approx", "hjsk"),   multi_arm = NULL,   rho_prior = NULL,   mu_prior = NULL,   tau_prior = NULL,   gamma_prior = NULL,   nu_prior = NULL,   alpha_prior = NULL,   mixture_priors = NULL,   b_prior = NULL,   p_bias_prior = NULL,   w_bias_prior = NULL,   mu_beta_prior = NULL,   tau_beta_prior = NULL,   bnp_concentration_max = NULL,   bnp_K_max = NULL,   use_known_bias = FALSE,   selection_priors = NULL,   p_cutoffs = c(0.025, 0.05),   n_components = 2L,   robust = FALSE,   robust_prior = NULL,   robust_df = 4,   robust_weight = NULL,   custom_model = NULL,   custom_data = NULL,   estimand = NULL,   cate_covariate = NULL,   baseline_risk = NULL,   re_min_k = NULL,   sample_prior = FALSE,   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   seed = 1234,   ... )

title: Run a Bayesian Meta-Analysis in Stan — bayesma
text: data data frame one row per study (per arm multi-arm studies). studyvar Character. Column name study identifiers. event_ctrl, event_int Character (binomial/poisson). Event counts. mean_ctrl, mean_int Character (gaussian). Arm means. sd_ctrl, sd_int Character (gaussian). Arm SDs. n_ctrl, n_int Character. Arm sample sizes. likelihood Character. "binomial", "gaussian", "poisson". model_type Character. "random_effect", "common_effect", "bias_corrected", "bc_bnp" (Bias-Corrected Bayesian Non-Parametric; Verde 2025), "selection_copas" (Robust Bayesian Copas; Bai 2020), "selection_weight", "pet_peese", "mixture_model" (Bayesian meta-analytic mixture; Maier 2024). stage Character. "one_stage" "two_stage". re_dist Character. "normal", "t", "skew_normal", "mixture". model_type = "selection_copas", "normal" "t" supported; pass "t" Bai (2020) Robust Bayesian Copas (RBC) formulation heavy-tailed random effects (recommended; see nu_prior). small_sample Character. "none", "t_approx", "hjsk". multi_arm Character NULL. Column name indicating multi-arm study grouping. used stage = "one_stage". rho_prior Prior within-study correlation multi-arm studies. mu_prior Prior pooled effect mu. tau_prior Prior RE standard deviation tau. gamma_prior Prior study baselines gamma. One-stage . nu_prior Prior RE degrees freedom nu. re_dist = "t" . alpha_prior Prior RE skewness shape. re_dist = "skew_normal" . mixture_priors Named list priors mixture components. b_prior Prior bias shift B. model_type = "bias_corrected" . p_bias_prior Prior proportion biased studies. Used bias_corrected bc_bnp. w_bias_prior Prior per-study bias weight. bias_corrected . mu_beta_prior Prior bias-component location (DP base). Used bc_bnp . Default uniform(-15, 15). tau_beta_prior Prior bias-component scale (DP base). Used bc_bnp . Default half_cauchy(0, 1). bnp_concentration_max Numeric. Upper bound DP concentration alpha ~ Uniform(0.5, bnp_concentration_max). bc_bnp . Default NULL resolves data via Verde (2025) eqn (20). bnp_K_max Integer. Truncation level stick-breaking approximation. bc_bnp . Default NULL resolves 1 + ceiling(5 * bnp_concentration_max). use_known_bias Logical. TRUE, data must contain biased column. selection_priors Named list priors selection model parameters. p_cutoffs Numeric vector. One-sided p-value cutpoints weight-function selection model. n_components Integer. Number mixture components. robust Logical. Adds two-component outlier mixture. robust_prior Prior outlier component location. robust_df Degrees freedom outlier component. robust_weight Prior mixing weight main component. custom_model Optional Stan program (character scalar). non-NULL, code generation bypassed user's program used verbatim. custom_data Optional named list Stan data entries override augment automatically built data list. estimand Character. user-facing target effect: "", "RR", "HR", "IRR", "MD", "SMD" (relative-effect / mean-difference estimands; behave today), "RD" / "ARR", "ATE", "ATT", "CATE" (marginal estimands; computed posterior draws via bayesma_marginal() attached output $marginal). Default NULL infers likelihood. cate_covariate Character. Column name study-level covariate condition estimand = "CATE". Required CATE requested. baseline_risk Numeric, 0 1, "study_mean". Reference baseline risk used back-transform relative effects absolute scale two-stage binomial fits estimand c("RD","ARR","ATE","ATT"). Default NULL falls back observed control-arm event rate. re_min_k Optional numeric. set model_type = "random_effect", model downgraded "common_effect" warning number unique studies re_min_k. Default NULL (enforcement). Useful subgroup analyses strata studies stable random-effects estimation. sample_prior Logical. TRUE, samples prior predictive distribution fitting model without conditioning data likelihood. result can passed pp_check() verify prior places mass plausible observable values. Use prior elicitation sanity-checking — calibrating priors analysis dataset (circular). Default FALSE. chains, iter_warmup, iter_sampling, adapt_delta, seed MCMC settings. ... Passed cmdstanr::sample().

what: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: Implements Bayesian approach assessing small-study effects (funnel plot asymmetry) meta-analysis, based Shi, Chu & Lin (2020). method controls false positive rates using latent "true" standard errors rather sample standard errors Egger-type regression. standard Egger's test can inflated false positive rates effect sizes (particularly odds ratios) intrinsically correlated standard errors. Bayesian method addresses limitation modeling true within-study variances hierarchical model.

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
code: bayesma_egger(   data,   study,   event_ctrl = NULL,   event_int = NULL,   n_ctrl,   n_int,   mean_ctrl = NULL,   mean_int = NULL,   sd_ctrl = NULL,   sd_int = NULL,   likelihood = c("binomial", "gaussian", "poisson"),   heterogeneity = c("multiplicative", "additive"),   alpha_prior = NULL,   beta_prior = NULL,   kappa_prior = NULL,   gamma_prior = NULL,   d_prior = NULL,   tau_prior = NULL,   credible_level = 0.9,   chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 2000,   iter_sampling = 4000,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   seed = 1234,   ... )

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: data data frame containing study data. study Character. Column name study identifiers. event_ctrl, event_int Character. Column names event counts control intervention groups. Required binary outcomes. n_ctrl, n_int Character. Column names sample sizes control intervention groups. mean_ctrl, mean_int Character. Column names means control intervention groups. Required continuous outcomes. sd_ctrl, sd_int Character. Column names standard deviations. Required continuous outcomes. likelihood Character. "binomial" (default), "gaussian", "poisson". heterogeneity Character. "multiplicative" (default) "additive". Specifies heterogeneity structure Egger regression. "multiplicative": Assumes Var(y_i) = kappa^2 * sigma_i^2 "additive": Assumes Var(y_i) = sigma_i^2 + gamma^2 alpha_prior Prior regression intercept. Default: normal(0, 100). beta_prior Prior regression slope (small-study effect parameter). Default: normal(0, 100). kappa_prior Prior multiplicative heterogeneity SD (heterogeneity = "multiplicative"). Default: uniform(0, 2). gamma_prior Prior additive heterogeneity SD (heterogeneity = "additive"). Default: uniform(0, 2). d_prior Prior overall log (mu). Default: normal(0, 100). tau_prior Prior -study heterogeneity SD. Default: uniform(0, 2). credible_level Numeric. Credible interval level inference. Default: 0.90 (90% CrI, consistent typical small-study effects testing). chains Integer. Number MCMC chains. Default: 4. iter_warmup Integer. Number warmup iterations per chain. Default: 2000. iter_sampling Integer. Number sampling iterations per chain. Default: 4000. adapt_delta Numeric. Target acceptance rate. Default: 0.95. seed Integer. Random seed reproducibility. Default: 1234. ... Additional arguments passed cmdstanr::sample().

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: list class "bayesma_egger" containing: fit cmdstanr fit object summary Summary statistics key parameters draws Posterior draws data frame beta_summary Summary slope parameter (small-study effects) conclusion Character string interpreting result meta List metadata including priors, settings, effect sizes stan_code generated Stan code stan_data data passed Stan

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: method uses two-level hierarchical model: Level 1 (Meta-analysis model): binary outcomes odds ratios: $$n_{ij1} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij\cdot}, p_{ij})$$ $$\text{logit}(p_{ij}) = \mu_i + \delta_i \cdot (j=1)$$ $$\delta_i \sim N(d, \tau^2)$$ Level 2 (Egger regression): multiplicative heterogeneity: $$y_i \sim N(\alpha + \beta \sigma_i, \kappa^2 \sigma_i^2)$$ additive heterogeneity: $$y_i \sim N(\alpha + \beta \sigma_i, \sigma_i^2 + \gamma^2)$$ \(\sigma_i\) latent "true" standard error derived posterior distributions \(p_{i0}\) \(p_{i1}\).

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: slope parameter \(\beta\) indicates strength small-study effects: credible interval \(\beta\) excludes 0, evidence small-study effects (funnel plot asymmetry). Positive \(\beta\): Studies smaller precision tend show larger positive effects (potential missing negative studies). Negative \(\beta\): Studies smaller precision tend show larger negative effects (potential missing positive studies).

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
text: Shi L, Chu H, Lin L. (2020). Bayesian approach assessing small-study effects meta-analysis binary outcome controlled false positive rate. Research Synthesis Methods, 11(4):535-552. doi:10.1002/jrsm.1415

title: Bayesian Egger's Test for Small-Study Effects — bayesma_egger
code: # Example with binary outcome data result <- bayesma_egger(   data = my_meta_data,   study = "study_id",   event_ctrl = "events_control",   event_int = "events_treatment",   n_ctrl = "n_control",   n_int = "n_treatment",   likelihood = "binomial" ) #> Error: object 'my_meta_data' not found  # View results print(result) #> Error: object 'result' not found summary(result) #> Error: object 'result' not found

title: Compute a marginal estimand from a bayesma fit — bayesma_marginal
text: RD / ARR / ATE — binomial, one-stage Computed via marginal standardisation (g-computation) posterior draws per-study baseline logit (gamma[s]) pooled log-(mu), optionally shifted study-level random effects (epsilon[s]). per-study RD plogis(gamma[s] + mu + epsilon[s]) - plogis(gamma[s]), averaged across studies weighted harmonic mean arm sample sizes. corresponds population-weighted ATE observed study mix requires external baseline assumption. RD / ARR / ATE — binomial, two-stage Back-transforms posterior draws pooled log-using baseline risk drawn per-iteration Beta distribution fitted (method--moments) observed control-arm event rates. Propagates baseline uncertainty posterior RD. fixed scalar baseline_risk bypasses uses supplied value directly (old behaviour). ATE — gaussian Equivalent MD. Returns pooled posterior absolute scale. ATT One-stage: weighted intervention-arm sample size (n_int / sum(n_int)). Two-stage: back-transform ATE intervention-size-weighted baseline. Without IPD arm-size-weighted ATE treated, true causal ATT — interpret caution. CATE Routes meta_reg() moderators = cate_covariate. Reports meta-regression effect; user expected evaluate specific covariate value downstream.

title: Build a bivariate meta-analysis specification object — bayesma_mv_spec
text: data data frame one row per study containing arm-level data outcomes. studyvar Column study identifiers (unquoted). mean_ctrl_1, mean_int_1 Columns control intervention means outcome 1 (unquoted). sd_ctrl_1, sd_int_1 Columns control intervention SDs outcome 1 (unquoted). n_ctrl_1, n_int_1 Columns control intervention sample sizes outcome 1 (unquoted). mean_ctrl_2, mean_int_2 Columns control intervention means outcome 2 (unquoted). sd_ctrl_2, sd_int_2 Columns control intervention SDs outcome 2 (unquoted). n_ctrl_2, n_int_2 Columns control intervention sample sizes outcome 2 (unquoted). outcome_labels Character vector length 2 labels two outcomes. Default: c("outcome_1", "outcome_2"). likelihood Character. Currently "gaussian". stage Character. "two_stage" (effect sizes computed modelled) "one_stage" (marginalised model). rho_within Numeric scalar [-1, 1]. Within-study correlation two outcomes, assumed known. Default: 0.5. mu_prior Prior pooled effects. Either single prior (applied outcomes) named list elements matching outcome_labels. tau_prior Prior -study SDs. Either single prior named list. rho_between_prior Prior -study correlation. Default: uniform(-1, 1). custom_model Optional character scalar containing complete Stan code override generated program. custom_data Optional named list merged Stan data list.

what: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
title: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
text: Renders parameterised Quarto template publication-style statistical report. Section structure follows bayesma workflow guide; prose style follows typical systematic review (Introduction, Methods, Results, Overall Interpretation). sections toggleable via run_* arguments.

title: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
code: bayesma_report(   data,   studyvar,   yearvar,   estimand = c("OR", "RR", "HR", "IRR", "MD", "SMD", "RD", "ARR", "ATE", "ATT", "CATE"),   cate_covariate = NULL,   baseline_risk = NULL,   event_ctrl = NULL,   event_int = NULL,   mean_ctrl = NULL,   mean_int = NULL,   sd_ctrl = NULL,   sd_int = NULL,   n_ctrl = NULL,   n_int = NULL,   outcome_name = "the primary outcome",   null_range,   title = "Bayesian meta-analysis report",   subtitle = "Generated with bayesma",   author = "",   prospero_id = "",   background = "",   hypothesis = "",   inclusion_criteria = character(),   exclusion_criteria = character(),   quality_tool = "Cochrane RoB 2",   software_note = "",   rob_data = NULL,   priors = NULL,   run_inspect_sr = FALSE,   run_rob = FALSE,   run_egger = TRUE,   run_robma = TRUE,   run_metareg = FALSE,   run_subgroup = FALSE,   run_sensitivity_priors = TRUE,   moderators = character(),   subgroup_var = character(),   primary_model = "auto",   re_min_k = NULL,   output_file,   render = FALSE )

title: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
text: data data frame one row per study (per arm). studyvar Character. Column name study identifiers. yearvar Character. Column name publication year. estimand Character. Target effect: "", "RR", "HR", "IRR", "MD", "SMD", "RD" / "ARR", "ATE", "ATT", "CATE". marginal estimands (RD/ATE/ATT/CATE) computed via bayesma_marginal() natural scale. cate_covariate Character. Study-level covariate name. Required estimand = "CATE". baseline_risk Numeric (0, 1) "study_mean". Reference baseline used back-transform two-stage binomial fits RD/ATE/ATT. event_ctrl, event_int, n_ctrl, n_int Character. Column names event counts arm sizes (binary measures). mean_ctrl, mean_int, sd_ctrl, sd_int Character. Column names arm means SDs (continuous measures). outcome_name Character. Human-readable outcome label used titles prose. null_range Numeric length-2 vector. Null/ROPE range natural scale (e.g. c(0.9, 1.1) ). title, subtitle, author Character. Report metadata. prospero_id Character. Optional PROSPERO registration identifier. background, hypothesis Character (scalar vector). Prose Introduction Methods sections. element becomes paragraph. inclusion_criteria, exclusion_criteria Character vectors. element becomes bullet point. quality_tool Character. Risk--bias tool name (default Cochrane RoB 2). software_note Character. Optional override Software paragraph. rob_data Optional pre-scored risk--bias data frame rob_plot(). Used run_rob = TRUE. priors Optional named list prior settings sensitivity panel. element list mu_prior tau_prior. Defaults vague / weak_reg / informative. run_inspect_sr, run_rob, run_egger, run_robma, run_metareg, run_subgroup, run_sensitivity_priors Logical. Section toggles. moderators Character vector. Moderator columns meta-regression. Required run_metareg = TRUE. subgroup_var Character vector. Subgroup column(s). Required run_subgroup = TRUE. primary_model Character. "auto" (default; selected via compare_models()) one candidate model labels (e.g. "two_stage_re", "two_stage_re_hksj"). re_min_k Optional numeric. Forwarded bayesma() (used per subgroup level): downgrade random-effects common-effect number studies scope threshold. Default NULL. output_file Character. Path generated .qmd. render Logical. TRUE, also render .qmd HTML.

title: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
text: Invisibly, path generated .qmd file.

title: Generate a Bayesian meta-analysis report — bayesma_report
text: function writes .qmd file plus companion .rds (containing data, rob_data, priors) directory output_file. render = TRUE renders .qmd HTML via Quarto CLI.

what: Build a bayesma specification object — bayesma_spec
title: Build a bayesma specification object — bayesma_spec
text: Validates argument combination, resolves default priors, extracts relevant vectors data, computes study-level effect sizes needed, returns list every downstream stage consumes.

title: Build a bayesma specification object — bayesma_spec
code: bayesma_spec(   data,   studyvar,   event_ctrl = NULL,   event_int = NULL,   mean_ctrl = NULL,   mean_int = NULL,   sd_ctrl = NULL,   sd_int = NULL,   n_ctrl = NULL,   n_int = NULL,   likelihood = c("binomial", "gaussian", "poisson"),   model_type = c("random_effect", "common_effect", "bias_corrected", "bc_bnp",     "selection_copas", "selection_weight", "pet_peese", "mixture_model"),   stage = c("one_stage", "two_stage"),   re_dist = c("normal", "t", "skew_normal", "mixture"),   small_sample = c("none", "t_approx", "hjsk"),   multi_arm = NULL,   rho_prior = NULL,   mu_prior = NULL,   tau_prior = NULL,   gamma_prior = NULL,   nu_prior = NULL,   alpha_prior = NULL,   mixture_priors = NULL,   b_prior = NULL,   p_bias_prior = NULL,   w_bias_prior = NULL,   mu_beta_prior = NULL,   tau_beta_prior = NULL,   bnp_concentration_max = NULL,   bnp_K_max = NULL,   use_known_bias = FALSE,   selection_priors = NULL,   p_cutoffs = c(0.025, 0.05),   n_components = 2L,   robust = FALSE,   robust_prior = NULL,   robust_df = 4,   robust_weight = NULL,   custom_model = NULL,   custom_data = NULL,   estimand = NULL,   cate_covariate = NULL,   baseline_risk = NULL )

title: Build a bayesma specification object — bayesma_spec
text: data data frame one row per study (per arm multi-arm studies). studyvar Character. Column name study identifiers. event_ctrl, event_int Character (binomial/poisson). Event counts. mean_ctrl, mean_int Character (gaussian). Arm means. sd_ctrl, sd_int Character (gaussian). Arm SDs. n_ctrl, n_int Character. Arm sample sizes. likelihood Character. "binomial", "gaussian", "poisson". model_type Character. "random_effect", "common_effect", "bias_corrected", "bc_bnp" (Bias-Corrected Bayesian Non-Parametric; Verde 2025), "selection_copas" (Robust Bayesian Copas; Bai 2020), "selection_weight", "pet_peese", "mixture_model" (Bayesian meta-analytic mixture; Maier 2024). stage Character. "one_stage" "two_stage". re_dist Character. "normal", "t", "skew_normal", "mixture". model_type = "selection_copas", "normal" "t" supported; pass "t" Bai (2020) Robust Bayesian Copas (RBC) formulation heavy-tailed random effects (recommended; see nu_prior). small_sample Character. "none", "t_approx", "hjsk". multi_arm Character NULL. Column name indicating multi-arm study grouping. used stage = "one_stage". rho_prior Prior within-study correlation multi-arm studies. mu_prior Prior pooled effect mu. tau_prior Prior RE standard deviation tau. gamma_prior Prior study baselines gamma. One-stage . nu_prior Prior RE degrees freedom nu. re_dist = "t" . alpha_prior Prior RE skewness shape. re_dist = "skew_normal" . mixture_priors Named list priors mixture components. b_prior Prior bias shift B. model_type = "bias_corrected" . p_bias_prior Prior proportion biased studies. Used bias_corrected bc_bnp. w_bias_prior Prior per-study bias weight. bias_corrected . mu_beta_prior Prior bias-component location (DP base). Used bc_bnp . Default uniform(-15, 15). tau_beta_prior Prior bias-component scale (DP base). Used bc_bnp . Default half_cauchy(0, 1). bnp_concentration_max Numeric. Upper bound DP concentration alpha ~ Uniform(0.5, bnp_concentration_max). bc_bnp . Default NULL resolves data via Verde (2025) eqn (20). bnp_K_max Integer. Truncation level stick-breaking approximation. bc_bnp . Default NULL resolves 1 + ceiling(5 * bnp_concentration_max). use_known_bias Logical. TRUE, data must contain biased column. selection_priors Named list priors selection model parameters. p_cutoffs Numeric vector. One-sided p-value cutpoints weight-function selection model. n_components Integer. Number mixture components. robust Logical. Adds two-component outlier mixture. robust_prior Prior outlier component location. robust_df Degrees freedom outlier component. robust_weight Prior mixing weight main component. custom_model Optional Stan program (character scalar) supplied user. non-NULL, bayesma_stan_code() returns verbatim. custom_data Optional list Stan data override automatically built data list. Useful custom_model declares variables standard builder know . estimand Character. user-facing target effect: "", "RR", "HR", "IRR", "MD", "SMD" (relative-effect / mean-difference estimands; behave today), "RD" / "ARR", "ATE", "ATT", "CATE" (marginal estimands; computed posterior draws via bayesma_marginal() attached output $marginal). Default NULL infers likelihood. cate_covariate Character. Column name study-level covariate condition estimand = "CATE". Required CATE requested. baseline_risk Numeric, 0 1, "study_mean". Reference baseline risk used back-transform relative effects absolute scale two-stage binomial fits estimand c("RD","ARR","ATE","ATT"). Default NULL falls back observed control-arm event rate.

title: Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — carlisle_test
text: Implements INSPECT-SR check 4.3. genuine randomisation, baseline p-values approximately uniform. Fabricated trials often show implausibly well-matched groups (p-values near 1).

title: Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — carlisle_test
text: Carlisle JB (2017). Data fabrication reasons non-random sampling 5087 randomised, controlled trials anaesthetic general medical journals. Anaesthesia, 72(8), 944-952.

title: Carlisle's Test for Baseline Balance — carlisle_test

what: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
text: Computes Bayesian measures evidence moderator coefficients, including probability direction, null range (Region Practical Equivalence), credible intervals.

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
code: coefficient_evidence(object, null_range = NULL, ci_level = 0.95)

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
text: object bayesma_reg object. null_range Numeric vector length 2 defining null range, NULL (default) skip null range calculation. standardized coefficients, c(-0.1, 0.1) common choice. ci_level Numeric. Credible interval width (default: 0.95).

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
text: tibble columns: term: Coefficient name estimate: Posterior median std_error: Posterior SD ci_lower, ci_upper: Credible interval bounds pd: Probability direction (max P(β>0), P(β<0)) direction: probable direction ("positive" "negative") p_null: Proportion posterior inside null range (specified p_outside_null: Proportion outside null range (specified)

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
text: probability direction proportion posterior side zero median. ranges 0.5 (evidence) 1 (strong evidence direction). pd > 0.95 often considered meaningful evidence, though formal threshold.

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
text: null range defines range values considered "practically equivalent zero". proportion posterior inside null range indicates whether effect practically meaningful: p_null > 0.95: Effect practically zero (accept null) p_null < 0.05: Effect practically meaningful (reject null) Otherwise: Inconclusive Common null range choices: c(-0.1, 0.1) standardized coefficients (Cohen's d scale) c(-0.05, 0.05) log-(corresponds 0.95-1.05)

title: Bayesian Evidence for Meta-Regression Coefficients — coefficient_evidence
